Discuss the **HSE concerns** of the phosphoric acid and HF release. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) Raw Materials & Reactions (7 marks) **Classification of raw materials (2.5 marks):** | Nutrient | Raw Material | Nutrient Content | |---|---|---| | **N** | Anhydrous NH₃, Urea, AN, AS | 82 %, 46 %, 34 %, 21 % | | **P** | Rock Phosphate, Phosphoric Acid (WPA/MPA) | 30 – 35 % P₂O₅, 52 – 54 % P₂O₅ | | **K** | Muriate of Potash (KCl), SOP | 60 % K₂O, 50 % K₂O | | **S** | Elemental S, H₂SO₄, Gypsum | 100 %, 32 %, 18 % | | **Auxiliaries** | Steam, water, anti-cake agents, coating oil | — | **Reactions (4.5 marks — 1.5 each):** $$ \text{(i) Urea:}\quad 2\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ \text{(ii) DAP:}\quad 2\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 $$ $$ \text{(iii) SSP:}\quad \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2 + 2\,\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ #### Model Solution — (b) DAP Manufacture & HSE (7 marks) **DAP — Principle (2.5 marks):** - DAP is the **di-ammonium salt of phosphoric acid** with grade **18-46-0**. - The reaction is **strongly exothermic**; the heat of neutralisation vaporises water, producing a melt of (NH₄)₂HPO₄ that is granulated in a pug-mill / drum granulator. - A *pre-neutraliser* (pipe-cross reactor) feeds slurry at pH ≈ **5.8 – 6.0**; final ammonia addition in the granulator raises pH to **7.8 – 8.0**, completing the reaction. **Process Flow (2 marks):** Phosphoric acid (52 % P₂O₅) is pre-heated → mixed with ammonia in a *pipe-cross reactor* → slurry drops onto a rolling bed of recycled fines in a *drum granulator* → additional NH₃ sparged in granulator → wet granules dried in a co-current rotary dryer → screened (oversize crushed, undersize recycled) → cooled → coated → bagged. **HSE Concerns (2.5 marks):** | Concern | Detail | Control | |---|---|---| | **HF release** from WPA (WPA contains 0.5 – 1.5 % F) | At high T, HF escapes as vapour, attacks glass, bones, lungs | Rubber-lined / SS-904L equipment, **HF scrubber with limestone slurry** (CaCO₃ + 2 HF → CaF₂ + H₂O + CO₂) | | NH₃ slip from reactor/granulator | Toxic gas | Acid scrubber (H₂SO₄ → (NH₄)₂SO₄) | | Phosphogypsum disposal (TSP/SSP co-product) | Radon-222, heavy metals, acidic leachate | Impervious lined stacks, leachate treatment, water recycling | | Dust explosion in dryer | Urea/phosphate dust (Kst 100 – 150 bar·m/s) | Cyclone + bag filter, explosion vents, N₂ inerting | | High-pH burns (NH₃) and low-pH burns (H₃PO₄) | Skin/eye injury | PPE — chemical goggles, full PVC apron, emergency shower & eyewash | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic** > 1. **Confusing "straight" vs. "complex" fertilisers.** A *straight fertiliser* has **one** primary nutrient (e.g., urea, MOP). A *complex fertiliser* has **two or more** chemically combined. Marks are routinely lost when students list urea (a *straight* N) as an example of a complex fertiliser. > 2. **Writing "% N" without specifying N-P₂O₅-K₂O convention.** The KTU convention is **N – P₂O₅ – K₂O**, *not* N-P-K (elemental). DAP = **18-46-0** (P expressed as P₂O₅ equivalent), *not* "18-21-0". > 3. **Forgetting the recycling loop.** Many students describe the synthesis but omit the **decomposer + absorber + carbamate condenser** — this is worth **3 marks** in a 14-mark question. > 4. **Missing the two-stage chemistry.** Writing only the overall reaction loses 1.5 marks. The **carbamate intermediate (NH₂COONH₄)** must appear explicitly. > 5. **Treating HSE as a "tag-on" paragraph.** In PESFT633, *every* process question demands a dedicated HSE paragraph. Examiners allocate **3 – 4 marks** for HSE alone. > 6. **Writing biuret "by-product" without the reaction.** Always include the balanced equation and the temperature threshold (> 145 °C). > 7. **No units in the Formula Sheet.** Always quote temperature in **°C**, pressure in **bar**, NH₃ concentration in **ppm**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Complex fertiliser** = chemically combined **≥ 2 primary nutrients** in a single granule. - **Straight vs. Complex:** Urea (46-0-0) and MOP (0-0-60) are *straight*; DAP, MAP, NPK 17-17-17 are *complex*. - **Urea is the most concentrated solid N-fertiliser** — theoretical **46.65 % N**, sold as **46-0-0**. - **Overall urea synthesis:** 2 NH₃ + CO₂ → NH₂CONH₂ + H₂O; **ΔH ≈ −87 kcal/mol** (exothermic). - **Two-stage mechanism:** carbamate formation (fast, exo) → dehydration to urea (slow, mildly endo). - **Reactor conditions:** **180 – 200 °C, 140 – 200 bar, NH₃/CO₂ ≈ 2.9 – 4.0**; per-pass conversion **~ 53 – 80 %**. - **Total Recycle process** decomposes unreacted carbamate, scrubs NH₃, and returns the carbamate liquor — minimises effluent. - **Biuret** (>1.0 % fertiliser / >0.3 % foliar) forms above **145 °C**; control by short evaporator residence. - **Prill tower** is a key HSE zone: **dust explosion (Kst ≈ 100 bar·m/s)** + NH₃ slip + fall hazards. - **NH₃ TLV = 25 ppm, IDLH = 300 ppm**; refrigerated storage at **−33 °C** is the safest bulk option. - **DAP (18-46-0):** NH₃ + H₃PO₄ neutralisation; **HF** is the dominant HSE hazard; scrub with **limestone slurry**. - **SSP:** rock phosphate + H₂SO₄ → monocalcium phosphate + gypsum; the *gypsum stack* is an environmental liability (radon, leachate). - **HSE regulations relevant in India:** **Factories Act 1948, MSIHC Rules 1989, EP Act 1986, Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, ISO 45001, ISO 14001**. - **Energy benchmark:** urea plant consumes **~ 1.0 – 1.2 t steam / t urea** and **~ 0.6 – 0.8 Gcal thermal energy / t urea** — vital for energy audits. - **For 14-mark questions** always structure the answer as: **(i) Chemistry (1.5 – 2 marks), (ii) Block diagram / flow (2 – 3 marks), (iii) Process description (3 – 4 marks), (iv) Operating conditions table (2 marks), (v) HSE section (3 – 4 marks).**

topics.title: Ammonium Sulphate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ammonium Sulphate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT633)**
> **Ammonium Sulphate** is an inorganic salt with the chemical formula **(NH₄)₂SO₄**, containing approximately **21% nitrogen (N)** and **24% sulphur (S)** by mass. It is one of the oldest and most widely manufactured nitrogenous fertilizers globally, produced both as a primary product and as a significant by-product of several industrial processes including caprolactam manufacture, coke production, and gas cleaning operations.

The compound appears as a white to off-white crystalline solid (granular or powdered form) with a slightly saline/bitter taste. It is highly soluble in water and exhibits a mildly acidic reaction in soil, which is critical for its agronomic behavior.

### 1.2 Fundamental Chemical Identity

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| IUPAC Name | Ammonium tetraoxosulphate(VI) |
| Molecular Formula | (NH₄)₂SO₄ |
| Molecular Weight | **132.14 g/mol** |
| CAS Registry Number | 7783-20-2 |
| Nitrogen Content | **21.0% N (min)** |
| Sulphur Content | **24.0% S** |
| Physical State | White crystalline solid |
| Density (bulk) | ~1.77 g/cm³ |
| Solubility in water (20°C) | **76.4 g/100 mL** |
| pH (0.1 M solution) | ~5.5 |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE] **The "Two-Nutrient Soil Candy" Analogy**
> Imagine a single crystal of ammonium sulphate as a **"ready-to-eat nutritional packet" for plants**. Inside each tiny crystal:
> - The **ammonium part (NH₄⁺)** acts like a **slow-release protein bar** for the plant, providing nitrogen to grow leaves and stems.
> - The **sulphate part (SO₄²⁻)** acts like a **vitamin supplement**, providing sulphur, which plants need to build proteins and enzymes.
>
> Just as a single candy solves two hunger pangs, one crystal of ammonium sulphate solves two nutrient deficiencies simultaneously. Because it dissolves in water, plants can "drink" it directly through their roots.

### 1.4 Significance in Fertilizer Industry

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 (PESFT633)**
> From a **Health, Safety, and Environment (HSE)** perspective, ammonium sulphate is a critical study subject because:
> 1. It is the **most common by-product** in fertilizer/coke/steel industries — making HSE analysis of its manufacture essential.
> 2. **Ammonia (NH₃) release** during production poses serious inhalation hazards.
> 3. **Sulphuric acid handling** in the gypsum process is a major corrosion and burn risk.
> 4. **Dust exposure** during granulation/handling causes respiratory disorders.
> 5. **Effluent discharge** rich in nitrogen causes **eutrophication** of water bodies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Chemical Reaction Framework

The chemistry of ammonium sulphate revolves around a simple neutralization principle — combining an **ammonia source** with a **sulphuric acid source**:

$$2\,NH_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + \text{heat}$$

This is a strongly **exothermic acid-base reaction** with standard enthalpy change ΔH ≈ −**205 kJ/mol**. The heat released must be removed by controlled cooling/crystallization.

### 2.2 Manufacturing Routes — Engineering Logic

Ammonium sulphate is produced via **four major industrial routes**, each with distinct HSE implications:

#### Route 1: Direct Neutralization (Synthetic Process)
- Pure synthetic ammonia + Sulphuric acid
- Reaction occurs in a **saturator** (packed tower) at 60–80°C
- Slurry is centrifuged, dried, and granulated
- **HSE Concern:** H₂SO₄ splashes, NH₃ vapor leak, heat stress

#### Route 2: Gypsum Process (Mineral-based)
- Uses naturally occurring **CaSO₄·2H₂O** (gypsum) + Ammonia + CO₂
- Reaction sequence:
$$CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + 2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CaCO_3 + H_2O$$
- Calcium carbonate is filtered off as by-product
- **HSE Concern:** Particulate exposure, slurry handling, CO₂ asphyxiation in closed vessels

#### Route 3: Caprolactam By-Product
- Produced during the manufacture of **nylon-6 precursor (caprolactam)**
- Reaction mixture is neutralized with ammonia
- **HSE Concern:** Complex organic contaminants, multi-phase effluents

#### Route 4: Coke Oven / Gas Works By-Product
- Ammonia scrubbed from coke oven gas using sulphuric acid
- Historical "gas washing" technique
- **HSE Concern:** Coal tar exposure, hydrogen sulphide co-contamination

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Quick-Reference Formula Box for KTU 2024 ESE**

| Sl. No. | Formula / Equation | Application / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(NH_4)_2SO_4$ molecular weight = $2(14.01) + 8(1.008) + 32.06 + 4(16.00)$ = **132.14 g/mol** | Stoichiometric calculations |
| 2 | N content % = $\dfrac{2 \times 14.01}{132.14} \times 100 = 21.21\%$ | Fertilizer grade specification |
| 3 | S content % = $\dfrac{32.06}{132.14} \times 100 = 24.26\%$ | Agronomic sulphur loading |
| 4 | Acid-Base reaction: $2NH_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4$ | Process chemistry (saturator) |
| 5 | Heat of reaction: $\Delta H = -205 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Heat exchanger design |
| 6 | Solubility at 20°C: **76.4 g/100 mL water** | Crystallizer yield |
| 7 | Bulk density (granular): **1.77 g/cm³** | Storage silo design |
| 8 | TLV-TWA (NH₃): **25 ppm (ACGIH)** | Ventilation & PPE design |
| 9 | TLV-TWA (H₂SO₄ mist): **0.2 mg/m³** | Workplace air monitoring |
| 10 | pH of 0.1 M solution: ~5.5 | Soil/aqueous discharge calculations |

> [!IMPORTANT] **No Vertical Pipes Used** — All absolute value and conditional notations are written using $\vert$ or $\mid$ equivalents to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ammonium sulphate process is a **pioneer case study in industrial symbiosis** — its HSE lessons apply directly to:
- **Nitric acid plants** (HNO₃ + NH₃ pathway)
- **Urea plants** (NH₃ + CO₂ pathway)
- **Sulphuric acid contact plants** (H₂SO₄ + SO₃ + H₂O)
- **Effluent treatment** of nitrogenous wastewater
- **Coke oven by-product recovery units** in steel plants

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Nitrogen Percentage in (NH₄)₂SO₄

**Step 1 — Identify the molecular formula and atomic composition.**

The compound (NH₄)₂SO₄ contains:
- Nitrogen (N): **2 atoms**
- Hydrogen (H): **8 atoms**
- Sulphur (S): **1 atom**
- Oxygen (O): **4 atoms**

**Step 2 — Recall standard atomic weights.**

$$M_N = 14.01 \text{ g/mol}, \quad M_H = 1.008 \text{ g/mol}, \quad M_S = 32.06 \text{ g/mol}, \quad M_O = 16.00 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total molecular mass (M).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} &= 2(M_N) + 8(M_H) + 1(M_S) + 4(M_O) \\
&= 2(14.01) + 8(1.008) + 1(32.06) + 4(16.00) \\
&= 28.02 + 8.064 + 32.06 + 64.00 \\
&= 132.144 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage of nitrogen by mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%N &= \frac{\text{Mass contribution of N}}{\text{Total molecular mass}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2 \times 14.01}{132.144} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{28.02}{132.144} \times 100 \\
&= 21.204\% \approx 21.0\% \text{ N (commercial grade)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**

Commercial fertilizer grade ammonium sulphate is guaranteed to contain a **minimum of 20.5–21.0% N**. The theoretical value of 21.2% matches this industrial specification, confirming the derivation. The small shortfall from 21.2% to 21.0% in real product is due to:
- Trace moisture content (1–2%)
- Free sulphuric acid residues (~0.1%)
- Anti-caking coating additives

### 3.2 Derivation: Heat Duty for Saturator Cooling

In a saturator producing **1 tonne (1000 kg) of (NH₄)₂SO₄ per hour**, calculate the heat to be removed.

**Step 1 — Compute moles of (NH₄)₂SO₄ produced per hour.**

$$
n = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{132.14 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 7.567 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply stoichiometry with heat of reaction.**

From the equation $2NH_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4$, the heat released per kmol of (NH₄)₂SO₄ formed is:

$$\Delta H_{rxn} = -205 \text{ kJ/mol} = -205{,}000 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total heat released per hour.**

$$
Q_{released} = n \times \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert = 7.567 \times 205{,}000 = 1.551 \times 10^9 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to more practical units (kW).**

$$
Q = \frac{1.551 \times 10^9 \text{ kJ/h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} = 430{,}833 \text{ kW} \approx 431 \text{ MW (thermal)}
$$

This enormous heat load mandates:
- **External heat exchangers** in the saturator loop
- **Temperature control** below 80°C to prevent NH₃ flashing
- **Pressure relief devices** and emergency cooling systems
- **Material of construction** resistant to hot H₂SO₄ (stainless steel 316L / rubber-lined carbon steel)

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Route-wise HSE Comparison

| Hazard Parameter | Direct Neutralization | Gypsum Process | Caprolactam By-Product | Coke Oven By-Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NH₃ release risk | High | High | Moderate | High |
| H₂SO₄ splash risk | Very High | Low | Low | High |
| Particulate/dust risk | Moderate | High | Moderate | High |
| CO₂ asphyxiation risk | None | Moderate | None | None |
| Organic contaminant risk | None | None | Very High | Very High |
| Wastewater nitrogen load | Low | High | Very High | Very High |
| Solid waste (sludge) | Low | Very High (CaCO₃) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Overall HSE complexity | Moderate | High | Very High | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Direct Neutralization (Synthetic) Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["NH3 Storage Tank"] --> nodeB["Vaporizer and Preheater"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Saturator Tower Packed Bed 60 to 80 C"]
    nodeD["H2SO4 Storage 98 percent Concentric Tank"] --> nodeE["Acid Dilution and Cooling Unit"]
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF["Slurry Discharge to Centrifuge"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Mother Liquor Recycle Loop"]
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeH["Rotary Dryer"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Screening and Granulation Unit"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Product Storage Silo"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Bagging and Dispatch Section"]
    nodeC -.->|"NH3 Vapour Leak"| nodeL["Scrubber and Vent Treatment"]
    nodeL -.->|"Stack Emission"| nodeM["Atmospheric Discharge"]
    nodeC -.->|"Heat Removal"| nodeN["External Heat Exchanger Cooling Water Loop"]
    nodeN --> nodeC
```

### 4.2 Block-Level HSE Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC["PROCESS ZONE"]
        procA["Ammonium Sulphate Reactor"]
        procB["Crystallizer"]
        procC["Centrifuge"]
        procD["Dryer Granulator"]
    end

    subgraph HSE["HSE CONTROL LAYER"]
        hseA["Gas Scrubber NH3 Removal"]
        hseB["Particulate Filter Baghouse"]
        hseC["Acid Mist Eliminator"]
        hseD["Effluent Treatment Plant Ammonia Stripping"]
        hseE["PPE and Ventilation System"]
    end

    subgraph MON["MONITORING LAYER"]
        monA["Online NH3 Gas Detector"]
        monB["H2SO4 Mist Monitor"]
        monC["Continuous Stack Emission Analyser"]
        monD["Wastewater Nitrogen Analyser"]
    end

    subgraph EMER["EMERGENCY RESPONSE"]
        emA["Eyewash and Safety Shower"]
        emB["Fire Suppression Foam System"]
        emC["Emergency Shutdown ESD System"]
        emD["Spill Containment Bund"]
    end

    PROC --> HSE
    HSE --> MON
    MON --> EMER
    EMER --> PROC
```

### 4.3 Gypsum Process Reaction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mat1["Raw Gypsum CaSO4 2H2O"] --> mill["Ball Mill Pulverization"]
    mat2["Liquid Ammonia NH3 aq"] --> mix
    mat3["Carbon Dioxide CO2"] --> mix
    mill --> mix["Reaction Slurry Tank 50 to 60 C"]
    mix --> react["Main Reactor 1.5 to 3 atm Pressure"]
    react --> filt["Vacuum Filter CaCO3 Separation"]
    filt --> evap["Evaporator and Crystallizer"]
    evap --> cent["Centrifuge and Dryer"]
    cent --> prod["Finished Ammonium Sulphate Crystals"]
    filt -.->|"CaCO3 By product"| reuse["Cement Industry Feedstock"]
    react -.->|"NH3 Vapour Escape"| scrub["Ammonia Scrubber"]
    scrub -.->|"Cleaned Vent Gas"| stack["Atmospheric Vent"]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Note (KTU 2024)**
> All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `procA`, `hseB`, `nodeC`) to comply with Mermaid syntax safeguards. Node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold/italic/HTML tags — for unambiguous rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the chemical formula, molecular weight, and percentage nitrogen content of ammonium sulphate.*

**Model Answer:**
The chemical formula of ammonium sulphate is **(NH₄)₂SO₄**. Its molecular weight is **132.14 g/mol**, and it contains approximately **21% nitrogen (N)** by mass. The compound is an important nitrogenous fertilizer widely used in agriculture, particularly for sulphur-deficient soils and paddy cultivation, where it supplies both nitrogen and sulphur nutrients simultaneously. **[3 marks]**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three health hazards associated with the manufacture of ammonium sulphate.*

**Model Answer:**
Three major health hazards in ammonium sulphate manufacture are:
1. **Ammonia (NH₃) inhalation** — affects the respiratory tract, causing irritation, coughing, and pulmonary edema at high concentrations (TLV 25 ppm).
2. **Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) burns** — corrosive splashes cause severe skin and eye injuries; mist inhalation damages lung tissue.
3. **Dust exposure** during granulation/drying leads to respiratory ailments such as **occupational asthma** and eye irritation. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Convention):** Module-end questions carry an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **direct neutralization process** for the manufacture of ammonium sulphate, with a neat process flow diagram. Discuss the major **process variables** affecting product quality. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The direct neutralization process reacts pure **synthetic ammonia (NH₃)** gas with **concentrated sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)** in a saturator tower. The reaction is:

$$2NH_3(g) + H_2SO_4(aq) \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4(aq) + \text{Heat} \quad (\Delta H = -205 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

**Process Steps:**
1. **Ammonia vaporization** from storage bullets and pre-heating to remove moisture.
2. **Acid preparation** — 98% H₂SO₄ is diluted to ~70–75% and cooled in acid coolers.
3. **Saturator reaction** — NH₃ and H₂SO₄ meet in a packed saturator at 60–80°C. The saturator contains a slurry of (NH₄)₂SO₄ crystals, ensuring controlled crystal growth.
4. **Slurry withdrawal** — crystals are sent to a centrifuge.
5. **Centrifugation and drying** — moisture removed by rotary dryer.
6. **Screening and bagging** — final product is graded and packed.

**Major Process Variables:**
- **Temperature (60–80°C):** Higher temperatures improve crystal size but increase NH₃ escape.
- **pH (4.5–5.5):** Lower pH (acidic) suppresses NH₃ loss but may cause corrosion.
- **Residence time in saturator:** Controls crystal size distribution.
- **Acid concentration:** 70–75% optimal; too concentrated causes localized hot spots.
- **Agitation rate:** Ensures uniform supersaturation and prevents channeling.

**(b)** Discuss the **health, safety, and environmental (HSE)** management strategies in ammonium sulphate plants, with special reference to ammonia release and sulphuric acid handling. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Health Hazards & Controls:**
- NH₃ is a toxic, irritant gas (TLV = 25 ppm, 8-h TWA). Engineering controls include **closed saturators**, **online NH₃ gas detectors** (electrochemical sensors), and **forced ventilation**.
- H₂SO₄ causes severe chemical burns. Controls: **rubber-lined pipes**, **acid-resistant PPE (aprons, face shields, gloves)**, **eyewash stations**, and **emergency showers** within 10 m of handling points.
- Dust exposure: Controlled by **enclosed conveyors**, **baghouse filters**, and **dust suppression water sprays**.

**Safety Strategies:**
- **Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** for all critical lines.
- **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** system for confined space entry (saturator internals).
- **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** interlocks for over-temperature/over-pressure.
- **Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs)** on saturator and acid dilution tanks.
- **Foam-based fire suppression** for organic contaminants (relevant in caprolactam route).

**Environmental Management:**
- **Ammonia scrubbers** (water/acid absorption) reduce stack NH₃ to < 50 mg/Nm³.
- **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** with ammonia stripping and biological nitrification-denitrification for wastewater.
- **Rainwater harvesting bunds** to prevent run-off contamination.
- **ISO 14001 EMS** and **regular environmental audits**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **gypsum process** for ammonium sulphate manufacture. Derive the **theoretical nitrogen percentage** in the product and compare it with the actual commercial grade. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The gypsum process uses naturally occurring **calcium sulphate dihydrate (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** as the sulphate source, reacting it with **ammonia and carbon dioxide**:

$$CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + 2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CaCO_3 \downarrow + H_2O$$

**Process Steps:**
1. Gypsum is pulverized in a ball mill to fine mesh.
2. Pulverized gypsum is mixed with liquid ammonia in a reactor.
3. CO₂ is bubbled through to drive the reaction and precipitate CaCO₃.
4. The slurry is vacuum-filtered to separate CaCO₃ (by-product sent to cement industry).
5. The filtrate is evaporated and crystallized to obtain (NH₄)₂SO₄ crystals.
6. Crystals are centrifuged, dried, and bagged.

**Nitrogen Percentage Derivation:**
Molecular weight of (NH₄)₂SO₄ = 132.14 g/mol.
Mass of N in 1 mole = 2 × 14.01 = 28.02 g.

$$\%N = \frac{28.02}{132.14} \times 100 = 21.21\%$$

**Comparison with Commercial Grade:**
The theoretical nitrogen content is **21.21%**, but the commercial grade typically guarantees **20.5–21.0% N**. The slight shortfall arises from:
- Residual moisture (1–2%) absorbed during handling.
- Free acidity (residual H₂SO₄ from filtrate carryover).
- Anti-caking coating (e.g., kaolin or oil treatment).

**(b)** Discuss the **environmental impacts** of ammonium sulphate production and the **waste minimization strategies** adopted in modern fertilizer complexes. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Major Environmental Impacts:**
1. **Air emissions** — NH₃ slip from saturator vents; particulate matter (PM) from dryers and granulators; SO₂ and NOₓ from combustion systems.
2. **Water pollution** — High Total Nitrogen (TN) effluents, Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), and suspended CaCO₃ (gypsum process). Causes **eutrophication** if discharged untreated.
3. **Solid waste** — Gypsum-process CaCO₃ sludge; spent catalysts; packaging waste.
4. **Soil contamination** — Spillage during transport/storage; accidental release of acid.

**Waste Minimization Strategies:**
- **In-plant recycling** of mother liquor back to the saturator (zero liquid discharge concept).
- **Ammonia recovery units** (e.g., Net Alkali scrubbing) to capture NH₃ slip and convert back to (NH₄)₂SO₄.
- **CaCO₃ by-product utilization** as raw feed in cement manufacturing — converts waste to wealth.
- **Cooling tower blow-down recycling** with Reverse Osmosis (RO).
- **Heat recovery** from saturator exotherm to pre-heat feed streams (pinch analysis).
- **Adoption of green chemistry** — replacing H₂SO₄ with more benign alternatives where feasible.
- **Pollution Prevention (P2)** audits under Responsible Care® and ISO 14001 frameworks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU 2024)**
> 1. **Forgetting the balanced equation** — Many students write NH₃ + H₂SO₄ without balancing, losing 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Confusing nitrogen % with sulphur %** — 21% N vs 24% S — write both clearly.
> 3. **Skipping process flow diagram** — KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** for a labeled flow diagram. A simple box-and-arrow diagram with key streams is sufficient.
> 4. **Ignoring HSE aspects** — A purely chemical answer without HSE discussion scores only 60–70% of marks in PESFT633.
> 5. **No mention of TLV/PEL values** — Always quote **TLV-TWA** for NH₃ (25 ppm) and H₂SO₄ mist (0.2 mg/m³) in HSE answers.
> 6. **Missing CaCO₃ by-product credit** in gypsum process — examiners expect you to mention its **reuse in cement industry**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Chemical formula:** (NH₄)₂SO₄; **MW:** 132.14 g/mol; **N content:** ~21%; **S content:** ~24%
- **Core reaction:** $2NH_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + \text{Heat}$
- **Heat of reaction:** ΔH = −205 kJ/mol (strongly exothermic)
- **Four industrial routes:** Direct Neutralization | Gypsum Process | Caprolactam By-Product | Coke Oven By-Product
- **Saturator conditions:** 60–80°C, pH 4.5–5.5
- **Solubility:** 76.4 g/100 mL water at 20°C
- **TLV-TWA (NH₃):** 25 ppm (ACGIH) — critical HSE number
- **TLV-TWA (H₂SO₄ mist):** 0.2 mg/m³
- **Gypsum reaction:** $CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + 2NH_3 + CO_2 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + CaCO_3 + H_2O$
- **CaCO₃ by-product:** Reuse in cement industry (circular economy)
- **HSE controls:** Ammonia scrubbers, acid-resistant PPE, baghouse filters, ETP, ESD systems
- **Environmental concern:** Eutrophication from nitrogenous effluents — requires biological treatment
- **Granulation advantage:** Reduces dust, improves handling, minimizes caking
- **Application:** Suitable for paddy, sugarcane, oilseeds; especially in sulphur-deficient and alkaline soils
- **Storage:** Cool, dry, ventilated area; away from oxidizers and strong alkalis

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Complex Fertilisers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Complex Fertilisers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **DEFINITION (KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment):** > A **Complex Fertiliser** is a fertiliser in which each individual granule (or particle) contains *two or more* of the primary essential plant nutrients — namely **Nitrogen (N)**, **Phosphorus as P₂O₅**, and **Potassium as K₂O** — in a chemically combined form, produced through a **chemical reaction** between the nutrient-bearing raw materials during manufacture, rather than by simple physical blending or mechanical mixing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):** > - **Complex Fertiliser** → Chemical combination during manufacture; uniform granule composition; requires chemical reaction. > - **Mixed / Blended Fertiliser** → Physical mixing of straight fertilisers; non-uniform if segregation occurs; **no** chemical reaction involved. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **chocolate chip cookie with nuts inside every bite** versus a **bowl where you mix chocolate chips and nuts together**: - If you take a single bite of the cookie, you are guaranteed to get *both* chocolate *and* nuts — the ingredients are **chemically bound and uniformly distributed** in every morsel. → This is a **Complex Fertiliser**. - If you scoop from the bowl, some bites will have mostly chocolate and some mostly nuts (segregation can occur). → This is a **Mixed / Blended Fertiliser**. > [!NOTE] > **Therefore:** Every granule of a complex fertiliser is a *miniature, complete nutrient packet* — guaranteeing uniform nutrient application per unit area of farmland, preventing the common problem of **segregation** observed in bulk-blended fertilisers. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Classification Grades | Class | Nutrient Content Required (KTU FCO Standard) | Example | |---|---|---| | **Low-Analysis Complex** | Total N + P₂O₅ + K₂O $\ge$ **20%** | DAP (18-46-0 = 64%) | | **Medium-Analysis Complex** | 20–30% Total Nutrients | 20-20-0 | | **High-Analysis Complex** | $\ge$ **30%** Total Nutrients | 15-15-15, 17-17-17 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PESFT633 - Module 1):** > Under the Indian **Fertiliser (Control) Order (FCO, 1985)**, complex fertilisers must be **granulated** with a minimum of **90%** of the material passing through a **4 mm IS sieve** and retained on **1 mm IS sieve** for grade compliance. ### 1.4 Major Commercial Examples of Complex Fertilisers $$\text{Diammonium Phosphate (DAP)} \;\; (18-46-0)$$ $$\text{Monoammonium Phosphate (MAP)} \;\; (11-52-0)$$ $$\text{Urea Ammonium Phosphate (UAP)} \;\; (28-28-0 \text{ or } 20-20-0)$$ $$\text{Ammonium Nitrate Phosphate (ANP)} \;\; (23-23-0)$$ $$\text{NPK Grade 15-15-15, 17-17-17, 19-19-19}$$ $$\text{Ammonium Polyphosphate (APP)} \;\; (15-62-0 \text{ liquid})$$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' Behind Complex Fertilisers:** > 1. **Uniformity** — Every granule delivers a fixed ratio of nutrients, eliminating field application errors. > 2. **Higher Nutrient Density** — Reduced shipping/storage cost per unit of nutrient. > 3. **Reduced Caking & Segregation** — Chemical bonding reduces hygroscopic behaviour. > 4. **Slow-Release Tendency** — Some complexes release nutrients over a longer period, reducing leaching losses. > 5. **Compatibility** — Many complexes combine nutrients that are otherwise incompatible in physical mixtures (e.g., Urea + DAP blend suffers from ammonia loss; chemically combined UAP does not). ### 2.2 Classification Logic Tree Complex Fertilisers are classified based on the **chemical species** formed during the granulation reaction: **Step 1: Phosphate Source Identification** - If acidulated with **Phosphoric Acid (H₃PO₄)** → leads to Ammonium Phosphates (MAP, DAP). - If acidulated with **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** → leads to Nitrophosphates (ANP). **Step 2: Nitrogen Source Addition** - **Ammonia (NH₃)** is sparged to neutralize acid and fix nitrogen into the granule. **Step 3: Potassium Addition** - **Muriate of Potash (KCl)** or **Sulphate of Potash (K₂SO₄)** is added as a solid feed to introduce K₂O. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Chemistry Happens in Three Places:** > 1. **Pre-neutralizer / Pipe Reactor** — Acid + Ammonia reaction (exothermic, releases **~125 kcal/kg** of DAP produced). > 2. **Granulator Drum** — Slurry / paste formation and agglomeration of nuclei. > 3. **Dryer-Cooler Circuit** — Moisture removal, crystallization, and hardening of granules. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the high-yield numerical core for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.** | # | Formula / Expression | Variables & Meaning | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\%\,\text{Total Nutrients} = \%N + \%P_2O_5 + \%K_2O$ | Sum of primary nutrient percentages | Dimensionless (%) | | 2 | $\%\,P = \%\,P_2O_5 \times 0.4364$ | Phosphorus (P) $\rightarrow$ Pentaoxide (P₂O₅) conversion factor | — | | 3 | $\%\,P_2O_5 = \%\,P \times 2.2913$ | Reverse conversion | — | | 4 | $\%\,K = \%\,K_2O \times 0.8301$ | Potassium (K) $\rightarrow$ Potash (K₂O) conversion | — | | 5 | $\%\,K_2O = \%\,K \times 1.2046$ | Reverse conversion | — | | 6 | $\%\,N = \dfrac{14 \times n_{atoms}}{\text{Molar Mass}} \times 100$ | Theoretical N content from molecular formula | % | | 7 | $\eta_{granule} = \dfrac{m_{retained\,on\,1\,mm}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | Granulation efficiency | % | | 8 | $Q_{reactor} = m_{NH_3} \times c_{p,NH_3} \times \Delta T$ | Heat released in neutralizer (design calcs) | kJ/h | | 9 | $R_{NH_3} = \dfrac{n_{NH_3,\,consumed}}{n_{H_3PO_4,\,supplied}} \times 100$ | Ammoniation ratio (mole ratio) | % | | 10 | $C_{DAP} = \dfrac{m_{DAP} \times 0.46 \times 2.2913}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | Actual %P₂O₅ in DAP product check | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** Memorize the conversion factors **0.4364** and **2.2913** (P ↔ P₂O₅) and **0.8301** and **1.2046** (K ↔ K₂O). KTU frequently tests inter-conversion numericals. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why Industry Uses Complex Fertilisers:** > - **Precision Agriculture** — Drone-based variable-rate applicators rely on uniform granule size and composition. > - **Bulk Blending Stations** — Use complex fertilisers as a base to achieve custom N-P-K grades. > - **Export Markets** — Granular DAP from India is exported to **Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Africa** under long-term supply contracts. > - **Health & Safety** — Reduces worker exposure to free ammonia and acid dust in field application. ### 2.5 Core Governing Reaction Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **Production of DAP (18-46-0):** > $$\text{Step 1: } H_3PO_4 + NH_3 \rightarrow NH_4H_2PO_4 \quad (MAP)$$ > $$\text{Step 2: } NH_4H_2PO_4 + NH_3 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2HPO_4 \quad (DAP)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Overall stoichiometry of DAP formation from wet-process phosphoric acid:** > $$H_3PO_4 + 2\,NH_3 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2HPO_4$$ > > The heat released in this exothermic reaction is utilized internally for **moisture evaporation** in the granulator, reducing external fuel demand by approximately **30–40%** compared to physical blending. > [!NOTE] > **NPK (15-15-15) Manufacture — Typical Reaction Set:** > $$NH_3 + H_3PO_4 \rightarrow NH_4H_2PO_4$$ > $$NH_4H_2PO_4 + NH_3 + HNO_3 \rightarrow \text{Nitrophosphate matrix}$$ > $$+\,KCl \rightarrow \text{NPK Granule (K bound as } K_2O \text{ equivalent)}$$ ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Nitrogen Content of DAP $(NH_4)_2HPO_4$ > [!NOTE] > **Objective:** To derive the maximum possible N content of Diammonium Phosphate from its molecular formula — a classic KTU numerical problem. **Step 1:** Write the molecular formula and calculate the molar mass. Molecular formula: $(NH_4)_2HPO_4$ Atomic masses (in g/mol): - $N = 14$ - $H = 1$ - $P = 31$ - $O = 16$ Element-by-element mass contribution: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mass of N in 1 mole} &= 2 \times 14 = 28 \;\text{g} \\ \text{Mass of H in 1 mole} &= 9 \times 1 = 9 \;\text{g} \\ \text{Mass of P in 1 mole} &= 1 \times 31 = 31 \;\text{g} \\ \text{Mass of O in 1 mole} &= 4 \times 16 = 64 \;\text{g} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Sum to obtain the molar mass of DAP. $$ \begin{aligned} M_{DAP} &= 28 + 9 + 31 + 64 \\ M_{DAP} &= 132 \;\text{g/mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate the theoretical mass percent of nitrogen. $$ \begin{aligned} \%N_{theoretical} &= \frac{\text{Mass of N per mole}}{\text{Molar mass}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{28}{132} \times 100 \\ &= 21.21\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Commercial DAP is sold as 18-46-0 (not 21-21-53):** > Industrial DAP is **not 100% pure** $(NH_4)_2HPO_4$. It contains moisture (1–2%), free phosphoric acid (0.5–1%), and process impurities (Fe, Al, Ca, Mg, F, SO₄²⁻ from wet-process acid). The actual guaranteed analysis is therefore **18% N** and **46% P₂O₅**, reflecting commercial product purity. ### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical P₂O₅ Content of DAP **Step 1:** From the same 1 mole of $(NH_4)_2HPO_4$: Mass of elemental P per mole = $31 \;\text{g}$ **Step 2:** Convert elemental P to its P₂O₅ equivalent using stoichiometric ratio. Molar mass of $P_2O_5 = 2 \times 31 + 5 \times 16 = 142 \;\text{g/mol}$ Molar mass of $P = 31 \;\text{g/mol}$, so for $P_2O_5$ we need 2 moles of P = $62 \;\text{g}$ Conversion factor (P → P₂O₅): $$ \begin{aligned} f_{P \to P_2O_5} &= \frac{M_{P_2O_5}}{2 \times M_P} = \frac{142}{62} = 2.2913 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate the theoretical %P₂O₅ in pure DAP. $$ \begin{aligned} \%P_2O_5 &= \frac{31 \times 2.2913}{132} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{71.03}{132} \times 100 \\ &= 53.81\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Convert to commercial product basis (assumed 85.5% pure DAP). $$ \begin{aligned} \%P_2O_5^{commercial} &= 53.81 \times 0.855 \\ &= 46.0\% \;\;\checkmark \;\; \text{(matches DAP grade 18-46-0)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Problem: Formulating NPK 19-19-19 from Raw Materials > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** > A fertilizer plant wishes to manufacture **1 tonne (1000 kg)** of NPK grade **19-19-19**. The available raw materials are: > - Urea (CO(NH₂)₂, 46% N) > - DAP ((NH₄)₂HPO₄, 18% N, 46% P₂O₅) > - MOP (KCl, 60% K₂O) > - Inert filler (0% nutrients) > > Calculate the mass of each raw material required (assuming 5% process loss of nutrients). **Step 1:** Identify the nutrient demand per tonne of product. Total N required = $19\%$ of 1000 kg = **190 kg N** Total P₂O₅ required = $19\%$ of 1000 kg = **190 kg P₂O₅** Total K₂O required = $19\%$ of 1000 kg = **190 kg K₂O** **Step 2:** Apply the 5% process loss correction. Effective nutrient demand (with loss) = $\dfrac{\text{Required nutrient}}{1 - 0.05} = \dfrac{190}{0.95} = 200 \;\text{kg}$ So the formulation must contain at least **200 kg** of each nutrient before loss. **Step 3:** Allocate MOP first (single-nutrient source — simplest). $$ m_{MOP} = \frac{200}{0.60} = 333.33 \;\text{kg MOP} $$ **Step 4:** Allocate DAP for phosphorus (DAP is the main P source). DAP supplies 46% P₂O₅, so: $$ m_{DAP,\,min} = \frac{200}{0.46} = 434.78 \;\text{kg DAP} $$ This DAP also brings in N: $$ N_{from\,DAP} = 434.78 \times 0.18 = 78.26 \;\text{kg N} $$ **Step 5:** Calculate the remaining nitrogen needed from urea. $$ N_{from\,urea} = 200 - 78.26 = 121.74 \;\text{kg N} $$ $$ m_{Urea} = \frac{121.74}{0.46} = 264.65 \;\text{kg Urea} $$ **Step 6:** Calculate inert filler (balance). $$ \begin{aligned} m_{filler} &= 1000 - (333.33 + 434.78 + 264.65) \\ &= 1000 - 1032.76 \\ &= -32.76 \;\text{kg} \quad \text{(NEGATIVE!)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation — Result is Negative:** > The negative value indicates that **pure 19-19-19 cannot be formulated** from this raw material set under the assumed 5% loss condition. The product is **nutrient-over-specified**. In practice, the plant would either: > 1. **Dilute the grade** to a feasible ratio (e.g., 17-17-17 or 15-15-15) using inert filler. > 2. **Add a low-N, high-P source** (such as MAP, 11-52-0) to balance the nutrient load. > 3. **Reduce process losses** below 5%. **Step 7 (Corrected):** Reformulate as 15-15-15. Required nutrients (with 5% loss) = $\dfrac{150}{0.95} = 157.89 \;\text{kg}$ of each. $$ m_{MOP} = \frac{157.89}{0.60} = 263.16 \;\text{kg} $$ $$ m_{DAP} = \frac{157.89}{0.46} = 343.24 \;\text{kg} \quad \rightarrow \text{brings } 61.78\;\text{kg N} $$ $$ m_{Urea} = \frac{157.89 - 61.78}{0.46} = 208.94 \;\text{kg} $$ $$ m_{filler} = 1000 - (263.16 + 343.24 + 208.94) = 184.66 \;\text{kg} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** A balanced **15-15-15 grade** requires **263.16 kg MOP + 343.24 kg DAP + 208.94 kg Urea + 184.66 kg filler** per tonne of finished product. ### 3.4 Derivation: Heat of Reaction in DAP Neutralizer (Design Calculation) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance:** The DAP reaction is highly exothermic. The heat released partially evaporates slurry water, reducing the load on the rotary dryer. **Step 1:** Use Hess's Law to compute $\Delta H$ of: $$ H_3PO_4(aq) + 2\,NH_3(g) \rightarrow (NH_4)_2HPO_4(aq) $$ Standard heats of formation (kJ/mol): - $\Delta H_f^\circ(H_3PO_4, aq) = -1271.7$ - $\Delta H_f^\circ(NH_3, g) = -45.9$ - $\Delta H_f^\circ((NH_4)_2HPO_4, aq) = -1366.0$ **Step 2:** Apply Hess's Law. $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ &= \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants}) \\ &= (-1366.0) - [(-1271.7) + 2 \times (-45.9)] \\ &= -1366.0 - [-1271.7 - 91.8] \\ &= -1366.0 - (-1363.5) \\ &= -2.5 \;\text{kJ/mol} \quad (\text{net standard value}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Account for the additional heat released by hydration and partial MAP formation, which is observed industrially as approximately **−125 kcal per kg of DAP produced** (≈ −523 kJ/kg). This is the actual design value used. **Step 4:** Calculate steam equivalent generated per tonne of DAP. $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{released} &= 523 \;\text{kJ/kg} \times 1000 \;\text{kg} = 523{,}000 \;\text{kJ} \\ m_{water\,evap} &= \frac{Q_{released}}{\lambda_{steam}} = \frac{523{,}000}{2260} \approx 231.4 \;\text{kg water} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Producing 1 tonne of DAP internally generates enough heat to evaporate **~231 kg of water** from the slurry, drastically reducing external fuel (diesel / furnace oil) consumption in the dryer. This is a key **HSE benefit** of the complex-fertiliser route over physical blending. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: DAP Production Flow (Mermaid Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Material Storage
Phosphoric Acid H3PO4] --> B[Pre-Neutralizer Reactor
Acid + Ammonia] A1[Anhydrous Ammonia NH3
Storage bullets] --> B C[Ground Phosphate Rock
Optional feed] --> D[Attack Tank
Sulfuric Acid digestion] D --> A B --> E[Slurry Mixer
pH 4.5 to 5.0 control] E --> F[Rotary Granulator Drum
Agglomeration zone] F --> G[Rotary Dryer
Counter-current hot air] G --> H[Rotary Cooler
Ambient air cooling] H --> I[Screening Unit
Oversize Undersize Fines] I --> J[Product Crusher
Oversize recycle] I --> K[Coating Drum
Oil and anti-caking agent] I --> L[Fines Recycle
Back to Granulator] K --> M[Finished DAP Storage
Silos 18-46-0 grade] J --> F L --> F N[Stack Scrubber
Wet scrubber for NH3] --> B O[Dust Collection
Bag filter / cyclone] --> F O --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - The **Pre-Neutralizer (B)** is the heart of the plant — the exothermic reaction occurs here. > - **Fines (L)** and **crushed oversize (J)** are recycled to the granulator to maximize yield and minimize waste. > - The **Scrubber (N)** and **Dust Collector (O)** are critical **HSE control points** — they prevent ammonia slip and particulate emissions (Module 1 relevance: Health, Safety, Environment). ### 4.2 Process Hierarchy: Classification of Fertiliser Products ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fertilisers] --> B[Straight Fertilisers
Single nutrient] A --> C[Complex Fertilisers
Chemical combination] A --> D[Mixed Blended Fertilisers
Physical mixing] B --> B1[Urea 46-0-0] B --> B2[DAP 18-46-0 as straight P source] B --> B3[MOP 0-0-60] C --> C1[NP Complex
e.g. DAP 18-46-0] C --> C2[NPK Complex
e.g. 15-15-15] C --> C3[PK Complex
e.g. 0-20-20] D --> D1[Bulk Blends
BB fertilizer] D --> D2[Suspension Fertilisers] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: HSE Control Architecture in a Complex Fertiliser Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process Unit
Granulator Neutralizer] --> B[Hazard Source] B --> B1[NH3 Slip toxic release] B --> B2[HF and H3PO4 acid burns] B --> B3[Particulate dust PM2.5 PM10] B --> B4[Heat stress and noise] B1 --> C1[Control: Water scrubber
pH less than 2] B2 --> C2[Control: PPE acid suits
Eyewash stations] B3 --> C3[Control: Bag filter cyclones
Wet suppression] B4 --> C4[Control: Insulation
PPE hearing protection] C1 --> D[Safe Operating Condition] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D E[Regulatory Framework] --> E1[FCO 1985 India] E --> E2[OSHA PEL NIOSH] E --> E3[EPA Effluent guidelines] E --> E4[ISO 45001 OHSMS] E1 --> D E2 --> D E3 --> D E4 --> D ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway from the Block Diagram:** > A modern complex fertiliser plant integrates **engineering controls** (scrubbers, filters, insulation), **administrative controls** (PPE, training, SOPs), and **regulatory compliance** (FCO, OSHA, EPA, ISO 45001) into a single layered HSE defence system — directly addressing the course objectives of **PESFT633**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > *Two KTU-style Part A questions with model answers.* **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between a **Complex Fertiliser** and a **Mixed (Blended) Fertiliser**. Give one example of each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Complex Fertiliser | Mixed / Blended Fertiliser | |---|---|---| | **Mode of Manufacture** | Produced by **chemical reaction** between raw materials | Produced by **physical mixing** of straight fertilisers | | **Granule Composition** | Each granule contains **all** declared nutrients uniformly | Nutrient distribution is **non-uniform**; prone to segregation | | **Segregation Risk** | Negligible | High (due to particle size & density differences) | | **Storage Stability** | High (low caking tendency) | Moderate to Low (caking common) | | **Nutrient Density** | Generally high | Depends on blend recipe | | **Example** | DAP $(18-46-0)$ | Bulk Blend of Urea + DAP + MOP (e.g., 19-19-19) | **[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for any 2 distinguishing points, 1 mark for examples]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** Write the **chemical reactions** involved in the manufacture of **Diammonium Phosphate (DAP)**. State its guaranteed nutrient composition. **Model Answer:** **Step 1 (MAP formation):** $$H_3PO_4 + NH_3 \longrightarrow NH_4H_2PO_4 \quad (\text{Monoammonium Phosphate})$$ **Step 2 (DAP formation):** $$NH_4H_2PO_4 + NH_3 \longrightarrow (NH_4)_2HPO_4 \quad (\text{Diammonium Phosphate})$$ **Overall Net Reaction:** $$H_3PO_4 + 2\,NH_3 \longrightarrow (NH_4)_2HPO_4$$ **Guaranteed Nutrient Composition (FCO 1985):** - **Nitrogen (N)** = **18% minimum** - **Phosphorus pentoxide (P₂O₅)** = **46% minimum** - **Grade notation:** 18-46-0 **[3 marks: 1 mark each for MAP step, DAP step, grade composition]** --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > *Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE of the two choices. Each question is 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain the **manufacturing process of DAP** with a neat flow diagram. Describe the role of the **pre-neutralizer** and the **granulator drum** in the process. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the **Health, Safety and Environmental (HSE) hazards** associated with DAP manufacture. Suggest suitable **control measures** for each hazard. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer — Part (a):** The industrial manufacture of DAP follows the **wet-process slurry granulation route**: 1. **Raw Material Preparation:** Wet-process phosphoric acid (50–54% P₂O₅) from sulfuric acid digestion of phosphate rock is stored in rubber-lined MS tanks. Anhydrous ammonia is received via cryogenic tankers and stored in bullets. 2. **Pre-Neutralization:** Phosphoric acid and gaseous ammonia are reacted in a **stainless-steel pre-neutralizer** maintained at pH **4.5–5.0**. The reaction: $$H_3PO_4 + 2\,NH_3 \longrightarrow (NH_4)_2HPO_4 + \text{Heat}$$ is exothermic (~523 kJ/kg DAP), and the heat partially evaporates water from the slurry. 3. **Granulation:** The hot slurry (moisture ~25–30%) is sprayed onto a rolling bed of recycled fines in a **rotary granulator drum** (length-to-diameter ratio ~2.5–3.5, inclination 2–4°, rotation 5–8 rpm). Tumbling action builds granules by **layering and agglomeration**. 4. **Drying:** Wet granules (moisture 5–6%) enter a **co-current rotary dryer** with hot flue gas at 200–250 °C, reducing moisture to **< 1.5%**. 5. **Cooling & Screening:** Dried granules pass through a **rotary cooler** and a **double-deck vibrating screen** (4 mm top, 1 mm bottom). The **on-size fraction** (1–4 mm) is the finished product; oversize is crushed and recycled; undersize is recycled directly to the granulator. 6. **Coating & Storage:** Product is coated with **0.2–0.5% oil + anti-caking agent** in a coating drum and stored in silos. **Role of Pre-Neutralizer:** - Provides **uniform pH control** (4.5–5.0) to optimize MAP/DAP ratio. - Uses exothermic heat to pre-concentrate slurry. - Improves **granulation efficiency** and reduces ammonia slip. **Role of Granulator Drum:** - Provides **tumbling action** to agglomerate nuclei into spherical granules. - Allows **layered growth** of granule size. - The recycle-to-feed ratio is typically **2:1 to 4:1** for stable operation. **[7 marks: 2 marks process steps, 2 marks reactions, 1.5 marks pre-neutralizer role, 1.5 marks granulator role]** **Model Answer — Part (b):** | Hazard | Source | Control Measure | |---|---|---| | **Ammonia (NH₃) Toxicity** | Leaks from NH₃ bullets, pre-neutralizer, granulator | Water scrubbers; ammonia detectors; SCBA; emergency shutoff valves; OSHA PEL = 25 ppm (8-h TWA) | | **Phosphoric Acid Burns** | Spillage from H₃PO₄ tanks and pipelines | Rubber-lined tanks; acid-resistant PPE (PVC suits, face shield); emergency showers and eyewash stations | | **Hydrogen Fluoride (HF)** | Released from fluoroapatite in phosphate rock | Limestone scrubbers; HF-specific absorbent (calcium gluconate gel for first aid) | | **Particulate Dust (PM₂.₅/PM₁₀)** | Granulator, dryer, screening, conveying | Bag filters; cyclones; wet suppression; closed conveying; PPE dust masks | | **Heat Stress & Burns** | Dryer at 200–250 °C; exothermic reactions | Insulation; ventilated control rooms; rotation of workers; PPE heat-resistant clothing | | **Noise (> 85 dBA)** | Rotary equipment, crushers, blowers | Acoustic enclosures; ear plugs/muffs; periodic audiometry | | **Wastewater (Gypsum Slurry)** | Phosphoric acid plant effluent | Settling ponds; reuse in cement/gypsum board; pH neutralization before discharge | | **Greenhouse Gas (CO₂, N₂O)** | Urea and ammonia handling | N₂O abatement in nitric acid plants (catalytic reduction); leak detection and repair (LDAR) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Do NOT** write "ammonia is corrosive" — it is **toxic and irritant**, not strongly corrosive. Phosphoric acid is the corrosive agent. > 2. **Do NOT** omit the **HF hazard** — it is a syllabus-specific HSE point often missed. > 3. **Do NOT** mention only "PPE" as a control — KTU requires **engineering controls** (scrubbers, insulation) as the primary defence and PPE as the last layer (hierarchy of controls). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **(a)** Define **Complex Fertiliser**. Explain the **classification** of complex fertilisers with at least **two examples** in each class. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A complex fertiliser plant produces **1000 kg/batch** of NPK grade **15-15-15**. The available raw materials are: Urea (46% N), DAP (18% N, 46% P₂O₅), MOP (60% K₂O), and inert filler. Assuming **no process loss**, calculate the mass of each raw material required per batch. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer — Part (a):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** A complex fertiliser is a fertiliser that contains **two or more primary nutrients (N, P₂O₅, K₂O)** in **chemically combined form**, manufactured through a **chemical reaction** during granulation, such that every granule has a **uniform nutrient composition**. **Classification (with two examples each):** **Class 1: NP Complex Fertilisers (Nitrogen-Phosphorus)** - *Example 1:* **Diammonium Phosphate (DAP)** — 18-46-0 - *Example 2:* **Monoammonium Phosphate (MAP)** — 11-52-0 **Class 2: NPK Complex Fertilisers (Nitrogen-Phosphorus-Potassium)** - *Example 1:* **NPK 15-15-15** — Balanced grade - *Example 2:* **NPK 17-17-17** — High-analysis grade **Class 3: PK Complex Fertilisers (Phosphorus-Potassium, no N)** - *Example 1:* **0-20-20 grade** for legumes (no nitrogen needed) - *Example 2:* **0-25-25 grade** for sugarcane **Class 4: Ammonium Phosphate Sulphate (APS)** - *Example 1:* **16-20-0** (APS) - *Example 2:* **20-20-0** (Ammonium Sulphate Phosphate) **Class 5: Urea Ammonium Phosphate (UAP)** - *Example 1:* **28-28-0** (Urea + DAP reaction product) - *Example 2:* **34-17-0** (high-N UAP) **[7 marks: 2 marks definition, 5 marks for classification with 1 mark per class + 0.5 per example]** **Model Answer — Part (b):** **Step 1: Nutrient requirement per batch (1000 kg of 15-15-15).** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{required} &= 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \;\text{kg} \\ P_2O_{5,required} &= 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \;\text{kg} \\ K_2O_{required} &= 0.15 \times 1000 = 150 \;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Mass of MOP for K₂O (simplest allocation).** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{MOP} &= \frac{150}{0.60} = 250 \;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Mass of DAP for P₂O₅.** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{DAP} &= \frac{150}{0.46} = 326.09 \;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Nitrogen contributed by DAP.** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{from\,DAP} &= 326.09 \times 0.18 = 58.70 \;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Remaining nitrogen from urea.** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{from\,urea} &= 150 - 58.70 = 91.30 \;\text{kg} \\ m_{Urea} &= \frac{91.30}{0.46} = 198.48 \;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Inert filler (balance).** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{filler} &= 1000 - (250 + 326.09 + 198.48) \\ &= 1000 - 774.57 \\ &= 225.43 \;\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Verification.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total N} &= (326.09 \times 0.18) + (198.48 \times 0.46) = 58.70 + 91.30 = 150 \;\text{kg} \;\checkmark \\ \text{Total P}_2\text{O}_5 &= 326.09 \times 0.46 = 150 \;\text{kg} \;\checkmark \\ \text{Total K}_2\text{O} &= 250 \times 0.60 = 150 \;\text{kg} \;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Recipe (per 1000 kg batch of NPK 15-15-15):** > - **DAP:** 326.09 kg > - **Urea:** 198.48 kg > - **MOP:** 250.00 kg > - **Inert Filler:** 225.43 kg > - **Total:** 1000.00 kg **[7 marks: 1 mark for step 1, 1 mark step 2, 1 mark step 3, 1 mark step 4, 1 mark step 5, 1 mark step 6, 1 mark for verification]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes in Numericals:** > 1. **Do NOT** forget to subtract the nitrogen brought in by DAP before calculating urea — this is the most common error. KTU deducts **2 full marks** for this mistake. > 2. **Do NOT** assume that the filler mass is zero — always perform the balance check. > 3. **Always show the verification step** — it earns 1 mark and demonstrates conceptual clarity. > 4. **Convert percentages carefully** — use the form "0.18" not "18" in division; many students lose marks on this conversion. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 - Complex Fertilisers):** - ☐ **Definition:** Complex fertiliser = 2+ primary nutrients (N, P₂O₅, K₂O) in **chemically combined** form, in each granule. - ☐ **Distinction:** Complex = chemical reaction; Mixed = physical blending; Straight = single nutrient. - ☐ **Major Examples:** DAP (18-46-0), MAP (11-52-0), NPK 15-15-15, UAP (28-28-0), ANP (23-23-0). - ☐ **Key Conversion Factors (MEMORIZE):** P ↔ P₂O₅ : 0.4364 / 2.2913 ; K ↔ K₂O : 0.8301 / 1.2046. - ☐ **Theoretical N in pure DAP:** 21.21% (but sold as 18% due to impurities). - ☐ **Theoretical P₂O₅ in pure DAP:** 53.81% (sold as 46% commercially). - ☐ **DAP Reactions:** H₃PO₄ + NH₃ → MAP ; MAP + NH₃ → DAP. Net: H₃PO₄ + 2 NH₃ → (NH₄)₂HPO₄. - ☐ **Process Units (in order):** Pre-neutralizer → Granulator → Dryer → Cooler → Screens → Coater → Silos. - ☐ **Critical HSE Hazards:** NH₃ toxicity (PEL 25 ppm), H₃PO₄ corrosivity, HF release from rock, particulate dust, noise, heat. - ☐ **Key Controls:** Wet scrubbers, bag filters, acid-resistant PPE, closed conveying, LDAR programs. - ☐ **Granulation Spec:** 90% product must be 1–4 mm IS sieve size (FCO 1985). - ☐ **Heat Recovery:** DAP reaction releases ~523 kJ/kg, equivalent to evaporating ~231 kg water/tonne. - ☐ **Regulatory Framework:** FCO 1985 (India), OSHA (USA), EPA (effluent), ISO 45001 (OHSMS). - ☐ **Formulation Rule:** Allocate MOP (K) first, then DAP (P), then urea to balance N, then filler to balance mass.

topics.title: Organic Fertiliser (Composting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Organic Fertiliser (Composting) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):** > *Organic fertilizers are carbon-based compounds derived from natural sources such as plant residues, animal manures, and municipal wastes, used to improve soil fertility by supplying essential nutrients and enhancing microbial activity. Composting is the controlled biological decomposition of organic matter into a stable, humus-rich end product under managed conditions of moisture, aeration, and temperature.* ### 1.1 What is an Organic Fertiliser? An **organic fertiliser** is any fertiliser derived from naturally occurring plant or animal-based materials, containing nutrients in complex organic forms. Unlike synthetic mineral fertilisers (Urea, DAP, MOP), organic fertilisers release nutrients slowly through microbial mineralisation. **Standard Classification as per Fertiliser Control Order (FCO, India):** - **Bulky organic manures** — FYM (Farmyard Manure), compost, vermicompost, green manure - **Concentrated organic manures** — Oil cakes, bone meal, fish meal, blood meal - **Biofertilisers** — Rhizobium, Azotobacter, PSB (Phosphate Solubilising Bacteria) ### 1.2 What is Composting? Composting is a **bio-oxidative process** in which heterogeneous organic substrates are decomposed and stabilised by a mixed microbial population under controlled aerobic, thermophilic conditions, producing CO₂, H₂O, mineralised nutrients, and a stable, sanitised end-product called **Humus** or **Compost**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Garden Magic"** > Imagine a heap of vegetable peels, dried leaves, and kitchen waste left in a corner of your garden. After 60–90 days, with occasional turning and sprinkling of water, the pile turns dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling. What happened? > Nature's recyclers — **bacteria and fungi** — ate up the waste, multiplied, generated heat (the pile became warm!), and converted the smelly trash into black gold for plants. That dark product is **compost**, and the process is **composting**. It is essentially *biological incineration without fire*, where microbes do the burning, and the ash is plant food. ### 1.4 Key Physical / Chemical Constants - **C:N Ratio (Optimum):** 25:1 to 30:1 (initial); < 20:1 (final mature compost) - **Moisture content:** **50–60 % by weight** (critical window) - **pH range:** 6.5 – 8.0 - **Thermophilic temperature:** **55 – 65 °C** (peak); up to **70 °C** in static piles - **Bulk density of finished compost:** 0.5 – 0.8 g/cm³ - **Maturity period:** 60 – 180 days (depends on method) - **Pathogen destruction temperature:** > **55 °C for 3 consecutive days** (USEPA / FCO standard) - **Volumetric reduction:** ~40–60 % of initial substrate ### 1.5 Why Composting is Critical for the Fertiliser Industry - Converts **agricultural waste** (~350 MT/year in India) into wealth. - Reduces dependence on imported urea/DAP. - Improves **soil organic carbon (SOC)**, which has plummeted to < **0.5 %** in Indian soils (ideal: 1.5 – 2 %). - Mitigates **methane emissions** from open waste dumping. - Forms the backbone of the **circular nutrient economy** — N-P-K loops back to soil. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Bio-Chemistry of Composting (The "Why" Behind the Process) Composting proceeds in **four distinct microbial phases**, governed by temperature: **Phase 1 — Mesophilic (Ambient, 0–3 days)** - Temperature: 20 – 40 °C - Dominant microbes: Mesophilic bacteria (*Bacillus*, *Pseudomonas*). - Easily degradable sugars, starches, and amino acids are consumed. - pH drops slightly due to organic acid production. **Phase 2 — Thermophilic (Active, 3–15 days)** - Temperature: **40 – 70 °C** - Dominant microbes: Thermophilic bacteria (*Thermus*, *Bacillus stearothermophilus*), *Actinomycetes*. - Proteins, fats, cellulose, hemicellulose broken down. - **Pathogens, weed seeds, and fly larvae are destroyed** (sanitisation). **Phase 3 — Cooling / Mesophilic Decline (15–30 days)** - Temperature falls back to 30 – 40 °C. - Mesophiles recolonise; **Actinomycetes** dominate — they create the earthy "forest-floor" smell (geosmin & 2-methylisoborneol). - Lignin degradation begins. **Phase 4 — Maturation / Curing (30–180+ days)** - Temperature: ambient. - Humification occurs — formation of stable humic and fulvic acids. - C:N ratio stabilises near 10:1. - Phytotoxins (ammonia, organic acids) dissipate. ### 2.2 Critical Process Parameters | Parameter | Optimum Range | Effect of Deviation | |---|---|---| | C:N Ratio | 25:1 – 30:1 (start) | Low → NH₃ loss; High → slow decomposition | | Moisture | 50 – 60 % | <40 % → microbial dormancy; >65 % → anaerobic, odours | | Aeration / O₂ | **> 5 – 10 %** in pile voids | Anaerobic → CH₄, H₂S, foul odours | | Temperature | 55 – 65 °C (peak) | >70 °C → kills beneficial microbes | | pH | 6.5 – 8.0 | <5.5 → fungal dominance, slow process | | Particle size | 1.25 – 5 cm | Smaller → compaction, less O₂; larger → slow surface attack | ### 2.3 Types / Methods of Composting 1. **Aerobic Composting** — Pile / Windrow / In-vessel (rapid, odourless, preferred). 2. **Anaerobic Composting** — Biogas / Methanogenic digesters. 3. **Vermicomposting** — Use of *Eisenia foetida* / *Eudrilus eugeniae* earthworms. 4. **In-vessel / Mechanical Composting** — Rotating drums, closed reactors. 5. **Static Pile / Passive Composting** — With/without forced aeration. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical Equations) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **KTU high-yield** and must be memorised verbatim. They are tested directly in Part A and Part B numerical questions. **Formula 1 — Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C/N)** $$C/N = \dfrac{\text{Mass of Carbon in substrate}}{\text{Mass of Nitrogen in substrate}}$$ **Formula 2 — C/N Ratio Adjustment (Mixing Two Substrates)** $$(C/N)_{mix} = \dfrac{M_1 \cdot (C/N)_1 + M_2 \cdot (C/N)_2}{M_1 + M_2}$$ where $M_1, M_2$ are the masses of the two substrates. **Formula 3 — Moisture Content (% wet basis)** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100$$ **Formula 4 — Ammonia Volatilisation Loss** $$\% N_{loss} = k \cdot e^{-\alpha (C/N)} \quad \text{(empirical, pH dependent)}$$ **Formula 5 — Oxygen Consumption Rate (respiration index)** $$R = \dfrac{dO_2}{dt} = K \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$$ where $E_a$ is the activation energy, $R$ is the universal gas constant, $T$ is absolute temperature. **Formula 6 — Compost Stability Index (Solvita®-type)** $$SI = \dfrac{CO_2-C}{NH_3-N} \quad (\text{ratio of respiration to ammonification})$$ **Formula 7 — Volume Reduction** $$V_{reduction}\% = \dfrac{V_{initial} - V_{final}}{V_{initial}} \times 100$$ ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial scale:** 1000–5000 MT/day plants in India (e.g., Indore, Mumbai BMC plants). - **Emission control:** Aerobic composting reduces CH₄ by **>90 %** vs. landfilling. - **Carbon sequestration:** ~0.3 t CO₂-eq sequestered per ton of compost applied. - **Soil remediation:** Compost immobilises heavy metals (Pb, Cd) in contaminated soils. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Derivations (Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] > These derivations are modelled directly on the type of numerical problems asked in **KTU University Exam — Part B (14 marks)** questions of the 2024 Scheme. --- #### **Derivation 1 — C/N Ratio Adjustment Using Mixing Rule** **Given:** - Substrate A (Sawdust): C/N = 400:1, Mass = $M_1$ kg - Substrate B (Sewage sludge): C/N = 8:1, Mass = $M_2$ kg - **Target C/N = 30:1** **Find:** Ratio of $M_1 : M_2$ required. **Step 1 — Write the mass-balance equation for C and N.** Let $M_1$ = mass of substrate A, $M_2$ = mass of substrate B. For carbon: $C_{total} = M_1 \cdot c_1 + M_2 \cdot c_2$ For nitrogen: $N_{total} = M_1 \cdot n_1 + M_2 \cdot n_2$ where $c_i$ and $n_i$ are the C and N fractions. **Step 2 — Express the target C/N ratio.** $$\dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{total}} = 30$$ $$\dfrac{M_1 \cdot c_1 + M_2 \cdot c_2}{M_1 \cdot n_1 + M_2 \cdot n_2} = 30$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical C/N values.** Since $(C/N)_1 = c_1/n_1 = 400$ and $(C/N)_2 = c_2/n_2 = 8$: - Let $n_1 = 1$ unit → $c_1 = 400$ units (sawdust) - Let $n_2 = 1$ unit → $c_2 = 8$ units (sludge) So, total carbon $= 400 M_1 + 8 M_2$ and total nitrogen $= M_1 + M_2$. **Step 4 — Equate to target.** $$\dfrac{400 M_1 + 8 M_2}{M_1 + M_2} = 30$$ **Step 5 — Cross-multiply.** $$400 M_1 + 8 M_2 = 30 M_1 + 30 M_2$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange terms.** $$400 M_1 - 30 M_1 = 30 M_2 - 8 M_2$$ $$370 M_1 = 22 M_2$$ **Step 7 — Simplify the ratio.** $$\dfrac{M_1}{M_2} = \dfrac{22}{370} = \dfrac{11}{185} \approx 0.0595$$ $$\boxed{M_1 : M_2 \approx 1 : 16.82}$$ **Step 8 — Mass Verification (Sanity Check).** If $M_1 = 1$ kg, $M_2 = 16.82$ kg: - C = $400(1) + 8(16.82) = 400 + 134.56 = 534.56$ units - N = $1 + 16.82 = 17.82$ units - C/N = $534.56 / 17.82 = 30.0$ ✓ (target achieved) **Conclusion:** Approximately **17 kg of sewage sludge is needed per 1 kg of sawdust** to obtain an optimum C/N of 30. --- #### **Derivation 2 — Moisture Content Calculation** **Given:** Wet compost mass = $M_{wet} = 250$ kg, oven-dried mass = $M_{dry} = 110$ kg. **Step 1 — Apply the moisture formula.** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{250 - 110}{250} \times 100 = \dfrac{140}{250} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{MC_{wb} = 56 \%}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** The compost lies within the **50 – 60 % optimum** window → biologically active. No re-watering required. --- #### **Derivation 3 — Volume Reduction During Composting** **Given:** Windrow initial volume = $50$ m³; final cured compost volume = $22$ m³. **Step 1 — Apply reduction formula.** $$V_{reduction}\% = \dfrac{V_{i} - V_f}{V_i} \times 100 = \dfrac{50 - 22}{50} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Compute.** $$\boxed{V_{reduction}\% = 56 \%}$$ **Step 3 — Mass loss inference.** Since water loss accounts for ~80 % of volume loss, the actual dry-mass mineralisation is ~10–12 %, consistent with typical aerobic composting literature values. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Microbial Phases vs. Parameters | Phase | Temp (°C) | Dominant Microbe | Substrate Consumed | End-products | |---|---|---|---|---| | Mesophilic | 20 – 40 | *Bacillus*, *Pseudomonas* | Sugars, starches | CO₂, organic acids | | Thermophilic | 40 – 70 | *Thermus*, *Actinomycetes* | Proteins, cellulose | CO₂, NH₃, heat | | Cooling | 30 – 40 | *Actinomycetes*, fungi | Lignin, hemicellulose | Humic precursors | | Curing | 20 – 30 | Mesophiles, fungi | Recalcitrants | Stable humus, fulvic acid | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow — Industrial Windrow Composting ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Organic Waste
Agricultural residue, Food waste, Manure] --> B[Pre-Processing
Shredding and Sieving] B --> C[Substrate Characterisation
CN ratio, Moisture, pH] C --> D{C/N Ratio
25 to 30 target} D -- Ratio less than 20 --> E[Add Bulking Agent
Sawdust, Straw, Dry Leaves] D -- Ratio greater than 40 --> F[Add Nitrogen Source
Urea, Poultry Manure] E --> G[Windrow Formation
Height 1.5 to 2.5 m] F --> G G --> H[Phase 1 Mesophilic
20 to 40 C, 0 to 3 days] H --> I[Phase 2 Thermophilic
55 to 65 C, 3 to 15 days] I --> J[Temperature Monitoring
Daily Probes] J --> K[Turning Schedule
Every 3 to 5 days] K --> L[Phase 3 Cooling
30 to 40 C, 15 to 30 days] L --> M[Phase 4 Curing
30 to 90 days] M --> N[Maturity Tests
CN ratio, Germination Index] N --> O[Stabilised Compost
Humus Rich End Product] O --> P[Bagging and Storage] O --> Q[Field Application] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Composting System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputStream["INPUT STREAM"] A1[Organic Waste Feedstock] A2[Bulking Agents] A3[Water and Amendments] end subgraph ReactorCore["REACTOR CORE - PILE OR VESSEL"] B1[Microbial Community
Bacteria, Fungi, Actinomycetes] B2[Heat Generation
Thermophilic Phase] B3[Gas Exchange
O2 in, CO2 out] end subgraph ControlLoop["CONTROL LOOP"] C1[Temperature Sensor] C2[Moisture Sensor] C3[Oxygen Probe] C4[PLC and SCADA] end subgraph OutputStream["OUTPUT STREAM"] D1[Finished Compost
Humus] D2[Process Water
Leachate] D3[Vent Gas
CO2, Water Vapour] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B3 C1 --> C4 C2 --> C4 C3 --> C4 C4 --> A3 B2 --> D1 B2 --> D2 B3 --> D3 ``` ### 4.3 Process Block — Vermicomposting Variant ```mermaid flowchart TD V1[Pre-Digested Organic Waste
Partially Composted] --> V2[Addition of Earthworms
Eisenia foetida] V2 --> V3[Mesophilic Bed
20 to 30 C] V3 --> V4[Earthworm Ingestion and Digestion
7 to 30 days] V4 --> V5[Castings Deposition
Vermicast] V5 --> V6[Harvesting and Sieving] V6 --> V7[Final Vermicompost
Nutrient Rich, 1.5 to 2 percent N] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]** *Define composting. List the four microbial phases of composting with their characteristic temperature ranges.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Composting is the controlled biological decomposition of heterogeneous organic substrates by a mixed microbial population under aerobic, thermophilic conditions, producing CO₂, H₂O, mineralised nutrients, and a stable humus-like end-product. > > **Phases (2 marks):** > 1. **Mesophilic phase** — 20 – 40 °C, dominated by mesophilic bacteria, utilises simple sugars/starches. > 2. **Thermophilic phase** — 40 – 70 °C, dominated by thermophiles, decomposes proteins/cellulose, destroys pathogens. > 3. **Cooling phase** — 30 – 40 °C, mesophiles recolonise, lignin degradation begins. > 4. **Curing/Maturation phase** — ambient, humification, formation of humic/fulvic acids. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *What is the significance of the C/N ratio in composting? Why is 25:1 – 30:1 considered optimum?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Significance (1.5 marks):** C/N ratio governs microbial growth, decomposition rate, and nitrogen loss. Microbes require a C:N substrate of ~24:1 for metabolism. - **If C/N too high** (>40:1) → N becomes limiting, microbes deplete available N, composting slows. - **If C/N too low** (<20:1) → excess N is released as NH₃, leading to nutrient loss, odour, and alkalinity. - **Why 25:1 – 30:1 is optimum (1.5 marks):** This range provides 25–30 units of C for every 1 unit of N, ensuring that ~2/3 of the C is respired as CO₂ while the remaining is assimilated into microbial biomass, leaving minimal residual N for volatilisation. Microbial C/N is ~8:1, so a starting C/N of 25:1 ends at ~10:1 in mature compost — the ideal humus signature. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > In Part B, students commonly lose marks for: (a) **missing units** in final answers (always write % or °C), (b) **skipping intermediate steps** in C/N mixing problems, (c) **not stating assumptions** (e.g., "assuming dry-mass basis"), and (d) **confusing C/N ratio with N percentage**. Always re-read the question before writing the final answer. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024] **(a)** Explain in detail the aerobic windrow method of composting. Discuss the role of turning, aeration, and moisture control with reference to the **C/N ratio** of the substrate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A compost facility receives **1500 kg/day** of food waste (C/N = 15:1) and **800 kg/day** of dry leaves (C/N = 60:1). Determine: **(7 marks)** - (i) The C/N ratio of the blended substrate. - (ii) Whether the blend lies in the optimum range. If not, suggest a corrective measure with calculations. **Model Solution for (a) [7 marks]:** 1. **Aerobic windrow method (2 marks):** Long, triangular or trapezoidal piles (height 1.5–2.5 m, width 3–4 m) of organic substrate, periodically turned to supply O₂. Preferred for large-scale municipal/agro waste. 2. **Role of turning (2 marks):** Replenishes O₂ (>5 % in voids), redistributes moisture/heat, breaks crust, exposes fresh substrate to microbes. Frequency: every 3–5 days in active phase. 3. **Role of moisture & aeration vs C/N (2 marks):** Moisture 50–60 % enables microbial transport of nutrients; below 40 % cells dehydrate, above 65 % O₂ diffuses poorly. C/N dictates amendment strategy: low C/N (e.g., food waste 15:1) → add high-C bulking agent (sawdust 400:1) to lift ratio to 25:1. 4. **Concluding statement (1 mark):** Integrated control of C/N, moisture, aeration, and temperature ensures pathogen sanitisation, minimal NH₃ loss, and stable humus. **Model Solution for (b) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Compute mixed C/N using the mixing formula.** $$(C/N)_{mix} = \dfrac{M_1 (C/N)_1 + M_2 (C/N)_2}{M_1 + M_2}$$ $$(C/N)_{mix} = \dfrac{1500 \times 15 + 800 \times 60}{1500 + 800}$$ $$= \dfrac{22{,}500 + 48{,}000}{2300} = \dfrac{70{,}500}{2300}$$ $$\boxed{(C/N)_{mix} \approx 30.65 : 1}$$ **Step 2 — Compare with optimum range.** Optimum range = 25:1 – 30:1. The blend at **30.65** is just above the optimum upper limit but still **acceptable for aerobic composting** (acceptable range commonly 25:1 – 35:1). **Step 3 — If strict 30:1 target is mandated, compute corrective mass.** Let $x$ kg of dry leaves to be added to original 1500 kg food waste to achieve C/N = 30:1. $$\dfrac{1500 \times 15 + x \times 60}{1500 + x} = 30$$ $$22{,}500 + 60x = 30(1500 + x) = 45{,}000 + 30x$$ $$60x - 30x = 45{,}000 - 22{,}500$$ $$30x = 22{,}500 \implies x = 750 \text{ kg}$$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** Replace 800 kg of dry leaves with **750 kg** of dry leaves and add a small quantity of water to maintain moisture 55 % → final C/N = 30:1 (perfect optimum). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Alternative 2024] **(a)** Compare **aerobic composting, anaerobic composting (biogas), and vermicomposting** with respect to oxygen requirement, end-products, time, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** During composting, a **100 kg wet substrate** has a moisture content of **65 %** (wet basis). Calculate: **(7 marks)** - (i) The mass of dry solids and water in the substrate. - (ii) The amount of water (in kg) to be removed to bring moisture to **55 %** (wet basis). - (iii) The final total mass after moisture correction. **Model Solution for (a) [7 marks]:** A comparative table is the most efficient way to present this answer. | Criterion | Aerobic | Anaerobic (Biogas) | Vermicomposting | |---|---|---|---| | **Oxygen** | Required (>5 %) | Absent (anaerobic) | Limited O₂, mesophilic | | **End-product** | Humus-rich compost | Biogas (CH₄+CO₂) + digestate | Vermicast (high N) | | **Time** | 60 – 90 days | 20 – 40 days retention | 30 – 60 days | | **Temperature** | Thermophilic (55–65 °C) | Mesophilic/thermophilic | Ambient (20–30 °C) | | **Application** | Soil amendment, FCO-grade compost | Energy + nutrient recovery | Organic kitchen-garden-grade | | **Pathogen kill** | Excellent (thermophilic) | Moderate | None (mesophilic) | (Mark distribution: 1 mark for the table + 3 marks for descriptive analysis + 3 marks for application discussion) **Model Solution for (b) [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Use moisture content formula (wet basis).** $$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Solve for dry mass.** With $MC_{wb} = 65$ %, $M_{wet} = 100$ kg: $$65 = \dfrac{100 - M_{dry}}{100} \times 100 \implies M_{dry} = 100 - 65 = 35 \text{ kg}$$ Water in substrate = $100 - 35 = 65$ kg. $$\boxed{M_{dry} = 35 \text{ kg}, \quad M_{water} = 65 \text{ kg}}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate water to be removed for 55 % moisture.** At 55 % MC (wb) with 35 kg dry solids: $$55 = \dfrac{M_{water,new}}{M_{water,new} + 35} \times 100$$ Let $W$ = new water mass. $$0.55 = \dfrac{W}{W + 35} \implies 0.55W + 19.25 = W \implies 0.45W = 19.25$$ $$W = 42.78 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 4 — Water to remove.** $$W_{remove} = 65 - 42.78 = 22.22 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 5 — Final total mass.** $$M_{final} = M_{dry} + W = 35 + 42.78 = 77.78 \text{ kg}$$ $$\boxed{M_{final} \approx 77.78 \text{ kg}, \quad W_{remove} \approx 22.22 \text{ kg}}$$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Bullet Revision — Must-Know for KTU Exam** > > - **Definition:** Composting = controlled aerobic bio-oxidation of organic waste into humus. > - **C/N ratio optimum:** 25:1 – 30:1 (start); < 20:1 (mature). > - **Moisture window:** **50 – 60 %** (wb) — narrow but critical. > - **O₂ in voids:** > **5 – 10 %**; below → anaerobic, CH₄, H₂S, odour. > - **Thermophilic peak:** **55 – 65 °C** for **≥ 3 days** kills pathogens (USEPA / FCO). > - **Mixing rule:** $(C/N)_{mix} = (M_1 \cdot (C/N)_1 + M_2 \cdot (C/N)_2)/(M_1 + M_2)$. > - **Four phases:** Mesophilic → Thermophilic → Cooling → Curing. > - **Maturity indicators:** C/N ≤ 20:1, earthy smell, dark brown, no phytotoxicity, germination index > 80 %. > - **Vermicompost N content:** ~1.5 – 2.0 % N (higher than FYM ~0.5 %). > -
