**FCO India specification:** Compost must have **min. 12 % OC, 0.8 % N, 0.4 % P₂O₅, 0.4 % K₂O**, and **C/N < 20**. > - **Volume reduction:** typically 40 – 60 % of initial. > - **Earthworm species:** *Eisenia foetida* (tiger worm) — most common in Indian vermicomposting. > - **End-product:** stable humus with humic + fulvic acids, slow-release N-P-K + micronutrients. > - **Industrial examples:** Indore composting (first Indian model), Nisargruna biogas plants (IIT-B). ---

topics.title: Bio Fertiliser - Manufacturing Process for Major Intermediates: Ammonia
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bio Fertiliser - Manufacturing Process for Major Intermediates: Ammonia ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Biofertiliser — Formal Definition A **biofertiliser** is a biologically active organic preparation containing living or latent cells of efficient strains of nitrogen-fixing, phosphate-solubilising, or cellulose-decomposing microorganisms that, when applied to seed, soil, or composting pits, multiply and participate in nutrient cycling, thereby enhancing soil fertility and crop productivity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Biofertilisers are positioned as an *eco-friendly, sustainable alternative* to chemical fertilisers. They are *living factories* that manufacture plant nutrients in situ, in contrast to chemical fertilisers which deliver pre-formed nutrients. ### 1.2 Ammonia — The King of Chemical Intermediates **Ammonia (NH₃)** is the single most important nitrogen-bearing intermediate in the global fertiliser industry. Approximately **85%** of the world's ammonia production is consumed directly or indirectly in fertiliser manufacture (urea, ammonium nitrate, ammonium sulphate, DAP, etc.). > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):** Ammonia is a colourless, pungent, alkaline gas (boiling point **−33.34 °C**; molecular weight **17.031 g/mol**) industrially synthesised by the catalytic combination of purified hydrogen and nitrogen under high pressure and temperature, following the **Haber–Bosch process**. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bakery of the Fertiliser World" Think of a fertiliser plant as a giant **bakery**: - **Flour (raw material)** = Natural gas, air, water - **Oven (reactor)** = The ammonia synthesis converter - **Bread (final product)** = NH₃, which is then used to bake *more specialised products* like urea, DAP, and CAN The **hydrogen** comes from natural gas (methane), the **nitrogen** comes from the air, and they are "baked" together in a high-pressure catalytic reactor to form ammonia. Biofertilisers, in contrast, are like a *home garden* — nature's own kitchen where bacteria (e.g., *Rhizobium*, *Azotobacter*) bake nitrogen for plants without industrial ovens. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** A single ammonia plant producing **1,000 metric tons/day** of NH₃ is equivalent to the nitrogen-fixing capacity of approximately **50 million hectares** of leguminous crop land per year — this is why the Haber–Bosch process is called *"the detonator of the population explosion"* (Vaclav Smil). --- ### 1.4 Why Ammonia is the Critical Intermediate | Property | Significance | |---|---| | High nitrogen content (82% N by weight in pure NH₃) | Highest N-density carrier in fertiliser industry | | Easily liquefiable (mild refrigeration) | Cheap storage and transport | | Reactive base | Forms salts with HNO₃, H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄ to give all major N-fertilisers | | Reducing agent | Used in pollution control (DeNOx) | | Precursor to urea (CO(NH₂)₂) | Urea holds **46% N** — most concentrated solid fertiliser | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Haber–Bosch Process — Logical Steps The modern ammonia manufacturing process is a tightly integrated sequence of **eight major unit operations**. Let us break down each: #### Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation - **Natural gas (CH₄)** is the preferred feedstock (also naphtha, heavy oil, coal, or coke can be used). - Natural gas is **desulphurised** first (ZnO guard beds) because sulphur is a *permanent poison* to the nickel reforming catalyst and the iron ammonia synthesis catalyst. #### Step 2 — Primary Reforming (Steam Reforming) Methane reacts with steam over a **nickel-on-alumina (NiO/Al₂O₃)** catalyst packed in tubes inside a fired furnace at **700–850 °C** and **30–40 bar**. $$CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = +206.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ #### Step 3 — Secondary Reforming The partially reformed gas passes over a nickel catalyst with **added air**. The oxygen burns residual methane and supplies the stoichiometric N₂ for downstream ammonia synthesis. $$CH_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -35.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ $$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad \Delta H_{298} = -483.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ #### Step 4 — CO Shift Conversion (Water–Gas Shift) Carbon monoxide is converted to additional hydrogen via the **shift reaction** in two stages: - **High-Temperature Shift (HTS):** Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃ catalyst, **350–450 °C** - **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS):** CuO–ZnO–Al₂O₃ catalyst, **200–250 °C** $$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ > [!NOTE] > The shift reaction is mildly exothermic and **favoured by low temperature** thermodynamically, but **high temperature** is needed for acceptable kinetics — hence the two-stage design. #### Step 5 — CO₂ Removal (Acid Gas Removal) CO₂ is scrubbed using one of: - **MDEA (Methyldiethanolamine)** — modern, energy-efficient - **Benfield (Hot Potassium Carbonate)** — older - **Rectisol (Cold Methanol)** — for sulphur-rich feedstocks #### Step 6 — Final Purification (Methanation) Trace CO and CO₂ (≤ 10 ppm) are hydrogenated back to CH₄ to protect the ammonia catalyst. $$CO + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_4 + H_2O$$ $$CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_4 + 2H_2O$$ #### Step 7 — Compression Synthesis gas is compressed to **150–300 bar** using centrifugal compressors (multi-casing, with steam turbine drive). #### Step 8 — Ammonia Synthesis Loop The heart of the plant. Reaction occurs in a **fixed-bed catalytic converter** with **promoted iron catalyst** (Fe + K₂O + Al₂O₃ + CaO). $$\tfrac{1}{2}N_2 + \tfrac{3}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons NH_3 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -45.9 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Unreacted gas is **recycled**; product NH₃ is condensed by refrigeration and separated. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Formula / Condition | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ammonia synthesis reaction | $\tfrac{1}{2}N_2 + \tfrac{3}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons NH_3$ | — | Stoichiometry | | 2 | Enthalpy of reaction | $\Delta H = -45.9$ kJ/mol | kJ/mol | Exothermic — favours low T | | 3 | Equilibrium constant (700 K) | $K_p \approx 0.0086$ | bar⁻¹ | Predicts conversion | | 4 | Optimum temperature | $T_{opt} = 400\text{–}500$ °C | °C | Trade-off Kinetics ↔ Eqbm | | 5 | Operating pressure | $P = 150\text{–}300$ bar | bar | Shifts eqbm right (Δn = −1) | | 6 | Per-pass conversion | $\approx 15\text{–}25\%$ | % | Recycle ratio calculation | | 7 | Steam reforming of CH₄ | $CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2$ | — | H₂ generation | | 8 | Water–gas shift | $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$ | — | H₂ boost | | 9 | Stoichiometric ratio | $H_2 : N_2 = 3 : 1$ | mol/mol | Inlet composition | | 10 | Le Chatelier (effect of P) | $\frac{\partial \ln K_p}{\partial P}\big\vert_T = -\frac{\Delta V}{RT}$ | — | Pressure effect | | 11 | Van't Hoff equation | $\frac{d \ln K_p}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ | K⁻¹ | Temp effect on $K_p$ | | 12 | Space velocity | $SV = \frac{\text{Vol gas (STP)}}{\text{Vol cat} \cdot h}$ | h⁻¹ | Reactor sizing | | 13 | Catalyst productivity | $\eta = \frac{\text{tons NH}_3}{\text{tons cat} \cdot \text{day}}$ | t/t·d | Performance metric | | 14 | Single-pass yield | $Y = \frac{F_{NH_3,out}}{F_{N_2,in}} \times 100$ | % | Reactor efficiency | | 15 | Energy consumption (modern plant) | $\approx 28\text{–}32$ GJ/ton NH₃ | GJ/t | Benchmark | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why high pressure and moderate temperature in Haber process?"* — quote **Le Chatelier's Principle** ($\Delta n_{gas} = -1$, hence high P shifts right) and **kinetics** (rate ∝ e^(−E/RT), hence moderate T for acceptable rate despite lower equilibrium conversion). --- ### 2.3 The Iron Catalyst — Why Promoted? The magnetite-based catalyst is **doubly promoted**: - **K₂O (potassium oxide)** — electronic promoter, weakens N≡N bond - **Al₂O₃ (alumina)** — structural promoter, prevents sintering - **CaO (calcium oxide)** — additional structural promoter - **Fe (α-iron)** — active site > [!NOTE] > Without promoters, plain iron sinters within hours at 500 °C, losing > 90% of its surface area. With promoters, the catalyst lasts **10–15 years** in industrial service. ### 2.4 Why This Process Matters (Real-World Engineering) - **Food Security:** Haber–Bosch supports ~50% of the nitrogen atoms in the human body (Smil's estimate). - **Energy Benchmark:** Modern single-train plants (e.g., KBR, Topsoe, Uhde) reach 1,500–2,000 MTPD capacity with 28 GJ/ton energy. - **CO₂ Footprint:** ~1.8–2.0 tons CO₂ per ton NH₃ — driving the *green ammonia* (electrolysis-H₂) revolution. - **Safety Relevance:** NH₃ is toxic (IDLH = 300 ppm); hence the *Health, Safety, Environment* lens of PESFT633. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Equilibrium Conversion in Haber Process **Problem Setup:** A stoichiometric 3:1 H₂:N₂ feed enters a synthesis converter at P = 200 bar, T = 700 K. Determine the equilibrium mole fraction of NH₃. Let $\alpha$ = fractional approach to equilibrium (per mole of N₂). #### Step 1 — Define Initial Moles For 1 mole N₂ + 3 moles H₂, the total initial moles $n_0 = 4$. #### Step 2 — Define Change at Equilibrium $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Reacted:} \quad & N_2: -\tfrac{1}{2}\alpha; \quad H_2: -\tfrac{3}{2}\alpha; \quad NH_3: +\alpha \text{ (per mole basis)} \\ \text{Remaining moles at equilibrium:} \quad & n_{eq} = n_0 - \alpha \\ \text{Specifically:} \quad & n_{N_2} = 1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha \\ & n_{H_2} = 3 - \tfrac{3}{2}\alpha \\ & n_{NH_3} = \alpha \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3 — Express Mole Fractions $$ \begin{aligned} y_{N_2} &= \frac{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha}{4 - \alpha} \\ y_{H_2} &= \frac{3 - \tfrac{3}{2}\alpha}{4 - \alpha} \\ y_{NH_3} &= \frac{\alpha}{4 - \alpha} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4 — Write Kp Expression $$ K_p = \frac{p_{NH_3}}{p_{N_2}^{1/2} \cdot p_{H_2}^{3/2}} = \frac{y_{NH_3} \cdot P}{ \left(y_{N_2} \cdot P\right)^{1/2} \cdot \left(y_{H_2} \cdot P\right)^{3/2}} $$ Simplifying (the $P^{(1 - 1/2 - 3/2)} = P^{-1}$ term remains): $$ K_p = \frac{1}{P} \cdot \frac{y_{NH_3}}{y_{N_2}^{1/2} \cdot y_{H_2}^{3/2}} $$ #### Step 5 — Substitute Mole Fractions $$ K_p = \frac{1}{P} \cdot \frac{\alpha / (4-\alpha)}{ \left[(1 - \alpha/2)/(4-\alpha)\right]^{1/2} \cdot \left[(3 - 3\alpha/2)/(4-\alpha)\right]^{3/2} } $$ #### Step 6 — Simplify the Pressure-Independent Group $$ K_p = \frac{(4 - \alpha)}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{(4 - \alpha)^2 \cdot \alpha}{?} \cdots $$ After algebraic simplification (concentrating the $(4 - \alpha)$ terms): $$ K_p \cdot P = \frac{\alpha(4 - \alpha)}{\left(1 - \tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)^{1/2} \left(3 - \tfrac{3\alpha}{2}\right)^{3/2}} $$ #### Step 7 — Insert Numerical Values For T = 700 K, $K_p \approx 0.0086$ bar⁻¹; P = 200 bar; therefore $K_p \cdot P = 1.72$. The transcendental equation becomes: $$ \frac{\alpha(4 - \alpha)}{(1 - \alpha/2)^{1/2} (3 - 1.5\alpha)^{3/2}} = 1.72 $$ #### Step 8 — Solve Iteratively Starting with trial $\alpha = 0.30$: $$ \frac{0.30 \times 3.70}{(0.85)^{0.5} (2.55)^{1.5}} = \frac{1.110}{0.922 \times 4.072} = \frac{1.110}{3.755} = 0.296 $$ Trial $\alpha = 0.45$: $$ \frac{0.45 \times 3.55}{(0.775)^{0.5} (2.325)^{1.5}} = \frac{1.598}{0.880 \times 3.546} = \frac{1.598}{3.120} = 0.512 $$ Trial $\alpha = 0.50$: $$ \frac{0.50 \times 3.50}{(0.75)^{0.5} (2.25)^{1.5}} = \frac{1.750}{0.866 \times 3.375} = \frac{1.750}{2.923} = 0.599 $$ These do not match 1.72 — the theoretical limit is below 1.72 even at $\alpha \to 1$. The actual $\alpha$ at the *catalyst bed exit* (industrial converter) is **~0.21** (giving ~21% conversion per pass), with the rest recycled. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering interpretation:** Even at 200 bar and 700 K, the per-pass conversion to NH₃ is only about **15–25%** by volume. This is why a *recycle loop* with separation + recompression is essential — the unconverted H₂/N₂ must be sent back to the converter 4–6 times before overall conversion reaches ~95%. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Energy Audit of a 1,000 MTPD Ammonia Plant | Process Section | Energy Input (GJ/ton NH₃) | % of Total | |---|---|---| | Feedstock compression | 1.5 | 5.0 | | Primary reformer (fired duty) | 10.8 | 36.0 | | Secondary reformer air compression | 1.2 | 4.0 | | CO₂ removal & shift utilities | 1.0 | 3.3 | | Syngas compression | 4.5 | 15.0 | | Refrigeration (NH₃ separation) | 1.0 | 3.3 | | Steam/electricity integration losses | 10.0 | 33.4 | | **Total** | **30.0** | **100** | > [!NOTE] > The **primary reformer** is the largest single energy consumer — it is the prime target for energy optimisation and the focus of *green ammonia* research (replacing fired duty with electrified heating). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Modern Haber–Bosch Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Natural Gas Feed
CH4 + C2H6] --> B[Desulphurisation
ZnO Guard Beds] B --> C[Primary Reformer
Ni/Al2O3 Catalyst
700 to 850 C, 30 bar] D[Process Air
Air Compressor] --> E[Secondary Reformer
Ni Catalyst, 1200 C] C --> E E --> F[HT Shift Converter
Fe2O3 / Cr2O3
400 C] F --> G[LT Shift Converter
CuO / ZnO / Al2O3
220 C] G --> H[CO2 Removal
MDEA Scrubber] H --> I[Final Purification
Methanation
Ni Catalyst, 300 C] I --> J[Syngas Compressor
Centrifugal Multi-casing
to 200 bar] J --> K[Ammonia Synthesis Loop
Fe Catalyst Converter
450 C, 200 bar] K --> L[Refrigeration
Chiller and Condenser] L --> M[Liquid Ammonia Storage
Minus 33 C Cryogenic Tank] M --> N[Downstream Fertilisers
Urea / DAP / AN Plants] L --> O[Recycle Compressor
Unreacted H2 / N2] O --> K ``` ### 4.2 Synthesis Loop Internal Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Fresh Syngas Inlet] --> M1[Mixing with Recycle] M1 --> H1[Feed Effluent Exchanger H1] H1 --> H2[Feed Effluent Exchanger H2] H2 --> C1[Catalyst Bed 1
Promoted Iron] C1 --> C2[Catalyst Bed 2
Promoted Iron] C2 --> C3[Catalyst Bed 3
Promoted Iron] C3 --> H2 H1 --> WH[Waste Heat Boiler] WH --> S2[Steam Drum] S2 --> SEP[High Pressure Separator] SEP --> CR[Chiller Refrigeration] CR --> LR[Liquid NH3 Product] LR --> PROD[To Storage] SEP --> RC[Recycle Compressor] RC --> M1 ``` ### 4.3 Process State Summary ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[State 1: Natural Gas
30 bar, 40 C] --> P2[State 2: Reformer Outlet
30 bar, 850 C] P2 --> P3[State 3: Secondary Reformer
30 bar, 1200 C] P3 --> P4[State 4: HT Shift Exit
30 bar, 430 C] P4 --> P5[State 5: LT Shift Exit
30 bar, 230 C] P5 --> P6[State 6: CO2 Removed
29 bar, 70 C] P6 --> P7[State 7: Methanator Exit
28 bar, 320 C] P7 --> P8[State 8: Compressor Discharge
200 bar, 95 C] P8 --> P9[State 9: Converter Exit
200 bar, 510 C] P9 --> P10[State 10: NH3 Liquid
200 bar, 35 C] ``` ### 4.4 Biofertiliser–Ammonia Comparison Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD BIO[Biofertiliser Pathway] --> B1[Rhizobium / Azotobacter
Living Cells] B1 --> B2[Root Nodulation
Biological N Fixation] B2 --> B3[Slow Release Nitrogen
Organic Matrix] B3 --> CROP1[Crop Uptake
Low Intensity] CHEM[Chemical Ammonia Pathway] --> C1[Steam Reforming + Haber Bosch
Industrial Reactor] C1 --> C2[Catalytic Synthesis
200 bar, 450 C] C2 --> C3[Urea / DAP / CAN
Concentrated Salts] C3 --> CROP2[Crop Uptake
High Intensity] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define biofertiliser. List any four commonly used biofertiliser microorganisms and the crops they benefit.** **Model Answer:** A *biofertiliser* is a preparation containing living or latent cells of efficient strains of microorganisms (N-fixers, P-solubilisers, etc.) which, when applied to seed, soil, or compost, multiply and participate in nutrient cycling, enhancing soil fertility and crop yield. | Microorganism | Function | Associated Crops | |---|---|---| | *Rhizobium* | Symbiotic N₂ fixation | Pulses, soybean, groundnut | | *Azotobacter* | Free-living N₂ fixation | Cereals, vegetables | | *Azospirillum* | Associative N₂ fixation | Rice, maize, sugarcane | | *Phosphate-Solubilising Bacteria (PSB)* | Solubilise insoluble phosphates | All crops | *(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 marks for table)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **State the stoichiometric equation for ammonia synthesis and justify why the Haber–Bosch process operates at high pressure.** **Model Answer:** The ammonia synthesis reaction is: $$\tfrac{1}{2}N_2 + \tfrac{3}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons NH_3 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -45.9 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Justification for high pressure:** The reaction involves a *decrease in the number of gaseous moles* (Δn = 1 − 2 = **−1**). By **Le Chatelier's Principle**, increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles — i.e., toward NH₃. Industrial plants operate at **150–300 bar** to obtain acceptable per-pass conversion. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **manufacturing process of ammonia by the Haber–Bosch process** starting from natural gas as feedstock. Discuss the role of primary and secondary reforming, shift conversion, CO₂ removal, and methanation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **reasons for choosing the operating conditions** (temperature 400–500 °C, pressure 150–300 bar, iron catalyst with K₂O/Al₂O₃ promoters) in the ammonia synthesis converter. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Process Description (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Desulphurisation (½ mark):** Natural gas is passed through **activated carbon** and **ZnO guard beds** to reduce sulphur to < 0.1 ppm. Sulphur permanently poisons the Ni reforming and Fe ammonia catalysts. **Step 2 — Primary Reforming (1 mark):** Desulphurised CH₄ + steam (steam-to-carbon ratio ≈ 3.5) is passed through Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst in reformer tubes at **700–850 °C, 30–40 bar** in a fired furnace. $$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3 — Secondary Reforming (1 mark):** Process air is injected to combust residual CH₄ and supply the stoichiometric N₂. Exothermic; temperature rises to ~1200 °C. **Step 4 — Shift Conversion (1 mark):** Two stages: - *HTS (Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃)* at 400 °C: bulk CO conversion - *LTS (CuO–ZnO–Al₂O₃)* at 220 °C: final trace CO cleanup $$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$$ **Step 5 — CO₂ Removal (1 mark):** MDEA (aMDEA) scrubbing absorbs CO₂ at high pressure; rich solvent is regenerated by heating. CO₂ is recovered for urea manufacture. **Step 6 — Methanation (1 mark):** Final CO/CO₂ cleanup over Ni catalyst to < 10 ppm. $$CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$$ **Step 7 — Compression + Synthesis (1 mark):** Purified syngas (H₂:N₂ = 3:1) compressed to **200 bar** and passed over promoted Fe catalyst at 450 °C. Per-pass conversion ~20%; product NH₃ condensed and stored. **Step 8 — Recycle (½ mark):** Unreacted gas is recompressed and returned to the converter; overall conversion ~95%. **(b) Operating Conditions Justification (7 marks):** **1. Temperature: 400–500 °C (2 marks)** - *Thermodynamic view:* The reaction is exothermic ($\Delta H = -45.9$ kJ/mol). Equilibrium constant $K_p$ decreases with temperature (Van't Hoff). Low T favours high conversion. - *Kinetic view:* N≡N bond breakage requires high activation energy (~420 kJ/mol). Rate increases exponentially with T. - *Optimum:* A compromise is **400–500 °C**, where $K_p$ is still acceptable (0.0086 at 700 K) and the rate is industrially viable. **2. Pressure: 150–300 bar (2 marks)** - $\Delta n_{gas} = -1$. By Le Chatelier, high P shifts equilibrium to the right. - Modern plants use **centrifugal compressors**; 200 bar is the mechanical sweet spot. - Higher P (e.g., 600 bar) was used in old plants (Claude, Casale) but energy costs outweigh conversion gains. **3. Catalyst: Promoted Iron (2 marks)** - *Active phase:* α-Fe (reduced from magnetite Fe₃O₄). - *Electronic promoter:* K₂O donates electrons to Fe, weakening the N≡N bond on adsorption. - *Structural promoters:* Al₂O₃ and CaO prevent Fe crystallite sintering at 450 °C. - Without promoters, the catalyst deactivates within hours; with promoters, life is 10–15 years. **4. Recycle (1 mark)** Per-pass conversion is only 15–25%, so an economic recycle loop is essential. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often write *"high temperature increases conversion"* — this is **WRONG** for an exothermic equilibrium. Write *"high temperature increases **rate** but decreases equilibrium conversion."* > - Confusing *primary* vs. *secondary* reformer roles: primary produces H₂; secondary burns residual CH₄ and supplies N₂. > - Many students forget that **shift conversion** is **exothermic** (ΔH = −41.2 kJ/mol) — write the temperature trade-off correctly. > - Do **not** claim Haber received a Nobel for *ammonia synthesis* — he received it for *nitrogen fixation of nitrogen from air*; **Bosch** received the Nobel for *high-pressure chemistry*. --- #### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Compare **biofertilisers and chemical fertilisers** in terms of nutrient content, release pattern, environmental impact, cost, and soil health. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **health, safety, and environmental (HSE) aspects** of ammonia manufacturing, including major hazards, toxicity thresholds, and control measures. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Biofertiliser vs. Chemical Fertiliser (7 marks):** | Parameter | Biofertiliser | Chemical Fertiliser (e.g., Urea) | |---|---|---| | Nutrient content | Low (1–5% N in carrier) | High (Urea: 46% N) | | Release pattern | Slow, sustained | Quick, burst release | | Crop response | Long-term (multiple seasons) | Short-term, single season | | Soil health | Improves structure, microbial diversity | Can degrade structure, acidify | | Environmental impact | Eco-friendly, no leaching | Leaching → eutrophication, NO₃⁻ in groundwater | | Cost per kg N | High (small farmers may not adopt) | Low, subsidised | | GHG emissions | Near-zero | ~2 tons CO₂ per ton NH₃ | | Storage | Cold chain for live cells | Dry, stable | | Application frequency | Once per season | Multiple top dressings | | Risk of over-application | Negligible | Crop lodging, leaf burn | **Key takeaway:** Biofertilisers complement, not replace, chemical fertilisers. Integrated Nutrient Management (INM) uses both. **(b) HSE Aspects of Ammonia Manufacturing (7 marks):** **1. Toxicity & Exposure Limits (2 marks):** - **TLV-TWA:** 25 ppm (ACGIH) - **TLV-STEL:** 35 ppm - **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health):** 300 ppm - **LC₅₀ (rat, 4 h inhalation):** 2000 ppm - Anhydrous NH₃ is corrosive to skin, eyes, and respiratory tract. **2. Major Hazards (2 marks):** - *Toxic release* from storage/transfer (Bhopal-type scenarios, though the gas was MIC, not NH₃) - *Fire/explosion:* NH₃ has flammability limits 15–28% vol in air; auto-ignition 651 °C. - *High-pressure equipment failure* (reformer tubes, converter, refrigeration). - *Hydrogen embrittlement* of steel at > 200 bar. - *Cryogenic burns* from liquid NH₃ at −33 °C. **3. Control Measures (2 marks):** - *Process Safety Management (PSM):* HAZOP, LOPA studies - *Detection:* NH₃ gas detectors (electrochemical, IR) at 10 ppm alarm; 25 ppm evacuation - *PPE:* Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA), chemical-resistant suits - *Mitigation:* Water curtains, flares, emergency shut-down valves (ESDV) - *Relief:* Pressure safety valves (PSV), rupture discs on reactors **4. Environmental Aspects (1 mark):** - *CO₂ emissions:* ~1.8–2.0 tons/ton NH₃ — Kyoto Protocol concerns - *NOₓ emissions:* From reformer flue gas — low-NOx burners needed - *Effluent:* Process condensate contains NH₃, methanol — stripped and recycled - *Carbon capture:* Modern trend is CO₂ capture for urea synthesis, reducing net emissions > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > - Writing *"ammonia is flammable like petrol"* — No! NH₃ is *difficult* to ignite; its primary hazard is **toxicity**, not fire. Maximum flammability only 28% vol in air, requiring strong ignition. > - Confusing **TLV** (Threshold Limit Value) with **IDLH** — TLV is for chronic 8-h exposure; IDLH is the concentration above which irreversible health effects occur in 30 min. > - Forgetting that **liquid NH₃** expands **~850×** when vaporised — underestimating storage hazard. --- ### 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - [ ] **Biofertiliser** = living microbe preparation; key genera: *Rhizobium, Azotobacter, Azospirillum, PSB, Mycorrhiza* - [ ] **Ammonia (NH₃)** is the *most important* nitrogen intermediate; ~85% of global production → fertilisers - [ ] **Haber–Bosch equation:** $\tfrac{1}{2}N_2 + \tfrac{3}{2}H_2 \rightleftharpoons NH_3$, $\Delta H = -45.9$ kJ/mol - [ ] **Operating window:** 400–500 °C, 150–300 bar, **promoted iron** catalyst (Fe + K₂O + Al₂O₃ + CaO) - [ ] **Stoichiometric H₂:N₂ = 3:1** (molar) at converter inlet - [ ] **Per-pass conversion** ≈ 15–25%; recycle ratio ≈ 4–5; **overall conversion** ≈ 95% - [ ] **Eight unit operations:** Desulphurisation → Primary Reforming → Secondary Reforming → HTS → LTS → CO₂ Removal → Methanation → Compression → Synthesis - [ ] **Primary reformer:** 700–850 °C, 30–40 bar, Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst, endothermic, fired furnace - [ ] **Secondary reformer:** ~1200 °C, air injected for N₂ + heat; exothermic - [ ] **HTS:** Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃ at 400 °C; **LTS:** CuO–ZnO–Al₂O₃ at 220 °C - [ ] **MDEA** is the modern CO₂ removal solvent (energy-efficient) - [ ] **Methanation** reduces residual CO/CO₂ to < 10 ppm to protect ammonia catalyst - [ ] **Energy benchmark:** Modern plant ~ 28–32 GJ/ton NH₃ (best-in-class 27.5 GJ/ton) - [ ] **Green ammonia** uses electrolysis H₂ + renewable electricity → zero direct CO₂ - [ ] **HSE — Toxicity:** TLV 25 ppm; IDLH 300 ppm; corrosive to moist tissue - [ ] **Flammability range:** 15–28% vol in air; auto-ignition 651 °C - [ ] **Cryogenic hazard:** Liquid NH₃ at −33 °C; expansion ratio 1:850 - [ ] **CO₂ footprint:** ~1.8–2.0 tons CO₂ per ton NH₃ - [ ] **Ammonia → Urea:** Bosch–Meiser process, NH₃ + CO₂ → urea; **46% N** in urea - [ ] **Other downstream products:** NH₄NO₃ (CAN, 26% N), (NH₄)₂SO₄ (21% N), DAP, MAP - [ ] **Biofertiliser vs. Chemical:** bio = slow, eco-friendly, low N%; chemical = fast, high N%, risk of leaching > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark question on ammonia manufacture, always draw the **flow diagram first** (2 marks), then list the **eight unit operations** (4 marks), then justify the **converter conditions** with Le Chatelier + kinetics (4 marks), and finally add **HSE aspects** (2 marks) and **modern trends** (2 marks). This structure guarantees a complete answer within the time limit.

topics.title: Nitric Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Nitric Acid # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** is a highly corrosive, strong monoprotic mineral acid, also known historically as **"aqua fortis"** (strong water) or **"spirit of niter."** It is one of the most important industrial chemicals, classified as a **Class 8 Corrosive Substance** (UN No. 2031) under the UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods. In the fertilizer industry, nitric acid is the **primary feedstock for ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃)** and **calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN)** production, and indirectly for **nitro-phosphate** fertilizers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The manufacturing, handling, storage, health hazards, safety protocols, and environmental impact (NOx emissions, acid rain, greenhouse effect) of Nitric Acid form a **core weightage topic** in the End Semester Examination. ## 1.2 Key Physical and Chemical Properties | Property | Value (for concentrated HNO₃) | |----------|-------------------------------| | Molecular Weight | **63.01 g/mol** | | Density (at 20°C) | **1.512 g/cm³** (for 98% w/w) | | Boiling Point (azeotrope) | **122 °C** (at 68% w/w concentration) | | Melting Point | **-42 °C** (pure) | | Dissociation constant (Ka) | ≈ **23.5** (very strong acid) | | pKa | **-1.4** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of nitric acid as the **"aggressive elder sibling"** of vinegar (acetic acid). Where vinegar gently cleans a window, nitric acid aggressively dissolves metals, burns skin on contact, and produces toxic yellow-brown fumes. In the fertilizer industry, it acts like a **"nitrogen transporter"** — it captures atmospheric nitrogen (N₂), which is normally inert, and converts it into a water-soluble, plant-absorbable form (NO₃⁻). > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Nitric acid is the *bridge* between the inert atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) and reactive fertilizer nitrogen (NO₃⁻). The Ostwald process is the industrial "bridge builder." ## 1.4 Grades of Nitric Acid - **Weak Nitric Acid (WNA):** 50–70% HNO₃ by weight. - **Concentrated Nitric Acid (CNA):** 98% HNO₃ by weight (fuming, density ≈ **1.51 g/cm³**). - **Fuming Red Nitric Acid:** Contains dissolved NO₂ (nitrogen dioxide), giving a red-brown color. - **Anhydrous Nitric Acid:** 100% HNO₃ (highly unstable, rarely stored pure). > [!WARNING] > **Fuming nitric acid is a strong oxidizer and reacts EXPLOSIVELY with most organic materials. Never store near fuels, oils, or paper.** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Industrial Manufacturing: The Ostwald Process The Ostwald process (developed by **Wilhelm Ostwald in 1902**, Nobel Prize 1909) is the dominant industrial route for nitric acid manufacture. It involves **three sequential catalytic steps**. ### Step 1: Ammonia Oxidation (Primary Stage) Ammonia gas is oxidized with air over a **platinum-rhodium (Pt-Rh) gauze catalyst** at temperatures of **900–950 °C** and pressures of **1–10 bar**. $$4\text{NH}_3(g) + 5\text{O}_2(g) \xrightarrow{\text{Pt-Rh, 900°C}} 4\text{NO}(g) + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \quad \Delta H = -905 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ - The reaction is **highly exothermic** and **kinetically fast** (contact time ≈ 3 × 10⁻⁴ s). - Typical **conversion of NH₃** is **96–98%**. ### Step 2: Nitric Oxide Oxidation (Secondary Stage) The NO produced is cooled and further oxidized: $$2\text{NO}(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2(g) \quad \Delta H = -114 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ - This reaction is **exothermic**, **reversible**, and **third-order overall**. - Equilibrium favors NO₂ at **low temperatures**, but the rate is slow. Industrial practice: **50–200 °C** to balance rate and equilibrium. ### Step 3: Absorption (Tertiary Stage) $$3\text{NO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow 2\text{HNO}_3(aq) + \text{NO}(g)$$ - The **NO released** is recycled back to Step 2. - Achieves **acid strength of 50–70% (WNA)** in standard columns. - A **bleacher tower** with secondary air removes dissolved NO₂ to produce a colorless acid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Overall Reaction:** > $$\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ > The **theoretical stoichiometric yield** is **1 mole HNO₃ per 1 mole NH₃ (mass ratio ≈ 2.94 kg HNO₃/kg NH₃).** ## 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Value | Engineering Use | |-----------|-----------------|-----------------| | Stoichiometric HNO₃ from NH₃ | $1 \text{ mol NH}_3 \rightarrow 1 \text{ mol HNO}_3$ | Yield calculations | | Mass ratio (theoretical) | $\frac{M_{HNO_3}}{M_{NH_3}} = \frac{63.01}{17.03} = 3.70$ | Feedstock balance | | Practical mass ratio | ≈ **2.80** (per kg NH₃ fed) | Plant design | | NO oxidation rate (3rd order) | $\frac{d[\text{NO}_2]}{dt} = k[\text{NO}]^2[\text{O}_2]$ | Reactor design | | N₂O greenhouse equivalent | N₂O has **GWP = 273** (100-yr, IPCC AR6) | Emission accounting | | Acid dew point (Tdew) | $T_{dew} \approx 100 + 0.83 \cdot C_{HNO_3}$ (in °C) | Stack design (C in %) | | TLV-TWA (ACGIH) | **2 ppm (5.2 mg/m³)** | Workplace safety | | STEL (15 min) | **4 ppm (10 mg/m³)** | Acute exposure | | IDLH (NIOSH) | **25 ppm** | Emergency planning | | LC₅₀ (rat, 4h inhalation) | **65 ppm** | Toxicological data | > [!NOTE] > **Critical safety constant:** TLV-TWA = **2 ppm**. Any reading above this in a plant must trigger respiratory protection and engineering controls. ## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters (Real-World Engineering) - **Pt-Rh catalyst** is used because of its exceptional activity for NH₃ oxidation. A **palladium catchment gauze** is placed downstream to recover volatile Pt losses (saves up to **30–50%** of Pt cost). - **High pressure (mono-high or dual-pressure process)** increases absorption efficiency — 95% acid achievable in 10-bar plants. - **NOx abatement (SCR/SNCR)** is mandatory under KTU/Indian environmental norms (CPCB) — tail-gas temperature ≈ 200–250 °C. ## 2.4 Classification of Manufacturing Processes | Process Type | Pressure (bar) | Acid Strength | NH₃ Conversion | |--------------|----------------|---------------|----------------| | **Single Pressure (Mono-high)** | 8–10 | 60–70% | 96% | | **Dual Pressure** | 4 (oxidation) + 10–12 (absorption) | 68–75% | 98% | | **Low Pressure (obsolete)** | 1.1–3.5 | 50–55% | 92% | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical vs. Actual Yield of HNO₃ **Problem:** A nitric acid plant consumes **1000 kg/day of NH₃** and produces **2640 kg/day of 60% (w/w) HNO₃**. Calculate the **% yield** based on the stoichiometric reaction. ### Given Data - Mass of NH₃ fed = 1000 kg/day - Mass of 60% HNO₃ produced = 2640 kg/day - Molar mass of NH₃ = 17.03 g/mol - Molar mass of HNO₃ = 63.01 g/mol ### Step 1: Calculate Pure HNO₃ Produced Since the acid is 60% w/w: $$m_{\text{pure HNO}_3} = 2640 \times 0.60 = 1584 \text{ kg/day}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Moles of NH₃ Fed $$n_{NH_3} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{17.03 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 58.72 \text{ kmol/day}$$ ### Step 3: Theoretical Moles of HNO₃ (1:1 Stoichiometry) $$n_{HNO_3, \text{theo}} = 58.72 \text{ kmol/day}$$ ### Step 4: Theoretical Mass of HNO₃ $$m_{HNO_3, \text{theo}} = 58.72 \times 63.01 = 3700.0 \text{ kg/day}$$ ### Step 5: % Yield Calculation $$\% \text{Yield} = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{Yield} = \frac{1584}{3700} \times 100 = 42.81\%$$ > [!NOTE] > A 42.81% overall yield (based on pure acid mass) is **typical of a single-pressure Ostwald plant**. Yields of **95–97%** are common in modern dual-pressure plants — the difference is mostly the dilute acid and recycle losses. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: NOx Emission in Tail Gas (Environmental Compliance) **Problem:** A plant produces **100 tonnes/day of 100% HNO₃**. The tail gas contains **2000 ppmv of NOx (as NO₂ equivalent)**. The volumetric flow rate of tail gas is **25,000 Nm³/h** at STP. Calculate: 1. Mass flow rate of NOx in kg/h 2. Whether the plant meets the CPCB standard of **400 mg/Nm³**. ### Step 1: Convert ppmv to Volume Fraction $$x_{NO_x} = 2000 \text{ ppmv} = 2000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.002$$ ### Step 2: Volumetric Flow of NOx at STP $$V_{NO_x} = 0.002 \times 25{,}000 = 50 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$ ### Step 3: Molar Volume at STP = 22.414 Nm³/kmol $$n_{NO_x} = \frac{50}{22.414} = 2.231 \text{ kmol/h}$$ ### Step 4: Mass Flow (Molecular weight of NO₂ = 46.01 g/mol) $$\dot{m}_{NO_x} = 2.231 \times 46.01 = 102.65 \text{ kg/h}$$ ### Step 5: Concentration in mg/Nm³ $$C_{NO_x} = \frac{102.65 \times 10^6 \text{ mg/h}}{25{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}} = 4106 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$ > [!WARNING] > **4106 mg/Nm³ ≫ 400 mg/Nm³ limit. The plant FAILS the CPCB standard and requires NOx abatement (SCR unit with NH₃ injection, or a wet scrubbing tower).** --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Health Hazard Severity by Route | Exposure Route | Symptom Onset | Severity | First Aid | |----------------|---------------|----------|-----------| | **Inhalation (>2 ppm)** | Immediate (minutes) | **HIGH** | Move to fresh air; O₂; medical attention | | **Skin Contact (conc.)** | Seconds | **CRITICAL** (yellow burn, deep necrosis) | Flush 15+ min; do NOT neutralize | | **Eye Contact** | Seconds | **CRITICAL** (corneal damage) | Emergency eyewash 15+ min; ophthalmologist | | **Ingestion** | Minutes | **FATAL** (>5 mL conc.) | Do NOT induce vomiting; immediate medical | | **Chronic (occupational)** | Years | Moderate (bronchitis, dental erosion) | Periodic spirometry; PPE upgrades | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ostwald Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ammonia Storage
Pressure 8-10 bar] --> B[Vaporizer
Preheater 80 C] B --> C[Air Filter
Compressor 4-10 bar] C --> D[Mixer Unit
NH3 Air 1 to 9 ratio] D --> E[Converter Burner
Pt-Rh Gauze 900 C] E --> F[Waste Heat Boiler
Steam Generation] F --> G[CO NO Cooler
Secondary Air 200 C] G --> H[Absorption Tower
Packed Column 60-70 Acid] H --> I[Bleacher Tower
Secondary Air] I --> J[Acid Storage Tank
S30400 SS] H --> K[Tail Gas Heater
200 C preheat] K --> L[DeNOx SCR Unit
NH3 Injection] L --> M[Stack Emission
Limit 400 mg per Nm3] H --> N[Recycle NO Loop] N --> G J --> O[Fertilizer Plant
NH4NO3 / CAN] E --> P[Palladium Catchment Gauze
Pt Recovery] style E fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#fff style H fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#16a085,color:#fff style L fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#f39c12,color:#000 style M fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#16a085,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Hazard Identification Tree (HAZID) ```mermaid flowchart TD HNO3[Nitric Acid Plant] --> H1[Process Hazards] HNO3 --> H2[Health Hazards] HNO3 --> H3[Environmental Hazards] HNO3 --> H4[Storage Hazards] H1 --> H1a[High Temperature 900 C] H1 --> H1b[High Pressure 10 bar] H1 --> H1c[Pt Catalyst Poisoning] H1 --> H1d[Runaway Reaction] H2 --> H2a[NOx Inhalation] H2 --> H2b[Acid Burns Skin Eye] H2 --> H2c[Chronic Bronchitis] H2 --> H2d[Dental Erosion] H3 --> H3a[Acid Rain NOx SOx] H3 --> H3b[Greenhouse N2O] H3 --> H3c[Water Pollution Spill] H3 --> H3d[Photochemical Smog] H4 --> H4a[Corrosion of Tank] H4 --> H4b[Decomposition to NO2] H4 --> H4c[Incompatibility with Organics] H4 --> H4d[Vapor Cloud Explosion] ``` ## 4.3 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Worker in HNO3 Area] --> B[Respiratory Protection] A --> C[Body Protection] A --> D[Hand Protection] A --> E[Eye Face] B --> B1[Half-face APR
Acid gas cartridge] B --> B2[SCBA for 25 ppm IDLH] C --> C1[Acid-resistant suit
Saranex or Tyvek] C --> C2[Rubber boots PVC] D --> D1[Butyl rubber gloves 0.4 mm] D --> D2[Double gloving routine ops] E --> E1[Chemical splash goggles] E --> E2[Face shield during transfer] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Nitric Acid. List its two main industrial uses. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** **Model Answer:** Nitric acid (HNO₃) is a strong monoprotic mineral acid, also called *aqua fortis*, with a molecular weight of 63.01 g/mol. It is highly corrosive and a powerful oxidizing agent. **Two main industrial uses:** 1. Manufacture of **ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃)** fertilizer. 2. Manufacture of **adipic acid, nitroglycerin, TNT (explosives), and metal nitrates**. --- ### Question 2: State the TLV-TWA and IDLH values for nitric acid vapor. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **Model Answer:** - **TLV-TWA (ACGIH):** 2 ppm (5.2 mg/m³) — time-weighted average for 8-hour exposure. - **STEL (15 min):** 4 ppm (10 mg/m³). - **IDLH (NIOSH):** 25 ppm — Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health concentration. Exposure above TLV causes respiratory irritation; above IDLH, respiratory protective equipment (SCBA) is mandatory. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Ostwald process** for the manufacture of nitric acid with all three reaction steps, catalyst used, operating conditions, and a neat flow diagram. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The Ostwald process is the principal industrial method for producing nitric acid. It has three stages: **Stage 1 — Ammonia Oxidation:** $$4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt-Rh, 900°C}} 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -905 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ - Catalyst: **90% Pt + 10% Rh woven gauze** (8–12 layers). - Temperature: **900–950 °C**, Pressure: **1–10 bar**. - Conversion: **96–98% NH₃ to NO**. **Stage 2 — NO Oxidation:** $$2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -114 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ - Slow at low temperature; equilibrium-limited. - Carried out in **heat exchangers** at **50–200 °C**. **Stage 3 — Absorption:** $$3\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NO}$$ - The released NO is **recycled** back to Stage 2. - Acid of **60–70% (WNA)** is produced; concentrated to 98% via **sulfuric acid dehydration** or **Mg(NO₃)₂ extractive distillation**. **Flow:** NH₃ storage → vaporizer → air mixer → converter (Pt-Rh) → waste heat boiler → cooler → absorber → bleacher → storage. Tail gas is treated by **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** to bring NOx below 400 mg/Nm³. **(b)** Discuss the **health hazards** of nitric acid exposure. Mention the **first aid measures** for skin contact and inhalation. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Health Hazards:** 1. **Inhalation:** NO₂ (formed when HNO₃ vapor reacts with air) causes **pulmonary edema, bronchitis, and emphysema**. Symptoms may be delayed 24–72 hours. 2. **Skin Contact:** Concentrated HNO₃ produces **severe yellow burns** (xanthoproteic reaction with skin proteins), with deep necrosis. 3. **Eye Contact:** Causes **permanent corneal damage** and blindness. 4. **Chronic Exposure:** **Dental erosion**, chronic bronchitis, reduced lung function. 5. **Ingestion:** **Fatal**; causes GI tract perforation. **First Aid Measures:** - **Skin Contact:** Immediately flush with **copious water for at least 15 minutes**. Remove contaminated clothing. **DO NOT** apply neutralizing agents (heat of neutralization worsens burn). Cover with sterile dressing. Seek medical aid. - **Inhalation:** Move victim to **fresh air**. Administer **oxygen** if breathing is difficult. Keep warm and at rest. **Do not perform mouth-to-mouth** if victim has inhaled NO₂. Get immediate medical attention — observe for delayed pulmonary edema. - **PPE Used:** Acid-resistant suit (Saranex), butyl rubber gloves, full-face respirator with acid gas cartridge, SCBA above IDLH. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** Describe the **environmental impact** of a nitric acid plant. Discuss **NOx abatement techniques** and the **greenhouse effect of N₂O**. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Environmental Impacts:** 1. **Acid Rain:** NOx emissions (NO, NO₂) react with atmospheric water to form HNO₃, which contributes to acid rain (pH < 5.6), damaging forests, soil, and aquatic life. 2. **Photochemical Smog:** NO₂ absorbs UV light and forms **ozone (O₃)** and **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)**, causing respiratory illness and crop damage. 3. **Greenhouse Effect — N₂O:** A byproduct of ammonia oxidation (about **1–3%** of N input becomes N₂O). **GWP = 273** (CO₂ equivalent) over 100 years per IPCC AR6. N₂O also depletes the **stratospheric ozone layer**. 4. **Water Pollution:** Accidental spills of acid waste streams lower water pH and kill aquatic life. 5. **Soil Contamination:** Settled NO₃⁻ causes **eutrophication** in water bodies. **NOx Abatement Techniques:** 1. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** Inject NH₃ into tail gas (200–250 °C) over a V₂O₅/TiO₂ catalyst: $4\text{NH}_3 + 4\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Removes 80–95% NOx. 2. **Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR):** Operates at 800–1000 °C without catalyst. Less efficient (40–70%). 3. **Extended Absorption:** High-pressure absorption (10–12 bar) increases absorption efficiency and reduces tail-gas NOx. 4. **Tertiary Treatment:** Using H₂O₂ or NaOH scrubbing as a polishing step. **(b)** Explain the **safe storage and handling** of nitric acid in a fertilizer plant. What are the **incompatible materials**? **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Safe Storage:** 1. **Material of Construction:** **Stainless Steel S30400 / S31600** (NOT carbon steel, NOT aluminum, NOT copper). Concrete tanks require an acid-resistant lining (PVC, PTFE, or rubber). 2. **Temperature:** Store below **30 °C** in well-ventilated area. Concentrated acid freezes at -42 °C, so winter storage needs steam tracing. 3. **Segregation:** Store in **dedicated acid room** with **secondary containment (bund) capacity = 110% of largest tank**. 4. **Ventilation:** Mechanical exhaust with **acid-resistant ducts**; minimum **6 air changes per hour**. 5. **Venting:** Tanks must have **pressure-vacuum relief valves** to handle NO₂ evolution from slow decomposition. **Handling Precautions:** - Use **mechanical pumps (centrifugal, S.S. body)** for transfer; never pressurize with air (use N₂ pad if needed). - **Grounding and bonding** for all transfer lines to prevent static sparks. - **Eye wash and emergency shower** within 10 seconds walking distance. - **Spill kit** (neutralizer = soda ash / lime) must be readily available. - **Hoses** must be acid-resistant (PTFE-lined), inspected weekly, and replaced every 6 months. **Incompatible Materials (NEVER MIX with HNO₃):** 1. **Organic materials:** Oil, gasoline, paper, sawdust, wood, rags (fire/explosion risk). 2. **Metals:** Aluminum, copper, brass, zinc (produces flammable H₂ gas). 3. **Reducing agents:** Sulfides, cyanides, carbides (violent reaction, toxic gas release). 4. **Bases / Caustics:** NaOH, KOH, ammonia (exothermic neutralization, splattering). 5. **Other acids:** Sulfuric acid (exothermic mixing, possible decomposition). 6. **Permanganates / Chlorates / Peroxides** (oxidizer + oxidizer → explosion). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write the Pt-Rh catalyst as "platinum only" — Rh is essential for **mechanical strength** at 900 °C. (-1 mark) > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusion between **NO and NO₂** in tail gas — remember, **NO is the desired intermediate**, **NO₂ is the absorber feed and the toxic tail gas**. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to **recycle NO** from the absorber — the plant is a *closed-loop* for NO. (-1 mark) > - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "aluminum tanks" for HNO₃ storage. **WRONG** — aluminum is violently attacked. Always specify **stainless steel** or **lined carbon steel**. > - **Common Mistake 5:** Missing **TLV-TWA = 2 ppm** in safety questions. Examiners explicitly test this numerical value. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Chemical Formula:** HNO₃, M.W. = **63.01 g/mol**, Density of 98% acid = **1.51 g/cm³**. - **Industrial Route:** **Ostwald Process** (3 stages — NH₃ oxidation → NO oxidation → absorption). - **Catalyst:** **Pt-Rh (90:10) gauze** at 900 °C; **Pd catchment gauze** for Pt recovery. - **Overall Reaction:** NH₃ + 2O₂ → HNO₃ + H₂O. - **Acid Strength:** 50–70% (WNA), 98% (CNA), 100% (anhydrous — unstable). - **Health TLV-TWA:** **2 ppm**, IDLH = **25 ppm**, STEL = **4 ppm**. - **Storage Material:** **Stainless Steel (S30400)** — NEVER aluminum, copper, or carbon steel. - **Incompatible:** Organics, reducing agents, metals (Al, Cu, Zn), bases. - **NOx Abatement:** **SCR with NH₃ over V₂O₅/TiO₂** catalyst — most effective. - **N₂O GWP:** **273** (IPCC AR6, 100-yr) — major greenhouse gas byproduct. - **Yield Indicator:** Modern dual-pressure plant achieves **95–97%** NH₃ conversion to HNO₃. - **Key Safety Equipment:** SCBA above IDLH; butyl rubber gloves; full-face respirator with acid cartridge; emergency eyewash/shower within 10 seconds. - **Spill Neutralizer:** **Soda ash (Na₂CO₃) or lime (CaO)** — never sawdust or organic absorbents. - **First Aid:** **Flush with water 15+ min**, **do not neutralize** skin contact, **do not induce vomiting** if ingested. - **Environmental Compliance:** Tail gas NOx limit = **400 mg/Nm³** (CPCB norm). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students:** Always cite the **Pt-Rh catalyst temperature (900 °C)**, the **TLV-TWA (2 ppm)**, and **Stainless Steel S30400** for storage in any 14-mark answer — these are the three "must-mention" keywords that examiners explicitly scan for during valuation.

topics.title: Sulphuric Acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sulphuric Acid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sulphuric Acid (Chemical Formula: H₂SO₄, Molecular Weight: 98.079 g/mol)** is a strong, highly corrosive mineral acid composed of two hydrogen atoms, one sulphur atom, and four oxygen atoms. It is often referred to as the **"King of Chemicals"** or **"Oil of Vitriol"** because of its widespread industrial applications, particularly in the manufacture of fertilizers (e.g., Single Super Phosphate, Ammonium Sulphate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sulphuric Acid is the foundational raw material for **Phosphoric Acid** production (via the Wet Process), which in turn produces **DAP, MAP, and TSP** fertilizers. Hence, understanding its HSE profile is a **mandatory CO1 outcome**.

### Key Physical & Chemical Properties

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Molecular Formula | H₂SO₄ |
| Molecular Weight | **98.079 g/mol** |
| Density (at 25°C) | **1.84 g/cm³** |
| Boiling Point (98.3%) | **337 °C** |
| Melting Point | **10.37 °C** |
| Viscosity (at 20°C) | **26.7 cP** |
| pKa1 (first dissociation) | **−3.0** |
| pKa2 (second dissociation) | **1.99** |
| Appearance | Colourless, oily, viscous liquid |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for KTU Students:**
> Think of sulphuric acid as the **"industrial version of a pressure cooker on maximum heat"**:
> - Just as a pressure cooker can instantly tenderize tough food (or violently explode if mishandled), H₂SO₄ can instantly dissolve metals, dehydrate organic tissue, and cause severe chemical burns.
> - If you spill vinegar (a weak acid) on your hand — it stings mildly. Spilling H₂SO₄ is equivalent to that same spill but with the heat of a hot frying pan AND the corrosive power to eat through your skin down to the bone.
> - In fertilizer industry, it acts as the **"acidic backbone"** — almost every phosphate fertilizer ultimately traces back to sulphuric acid reacting with phosphate rock.

### Industrial Importance in Fertilizer Industry

Sulphuric acid is the **single most-produced industrial chemical in the world** (≈ **260 million tonnes/year globally**), with **≈ 50–60% of production consumed by the fertilizer industry** alone. This makes its safe handling a **critical HSE concern** in any fertilizer plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Chain:**
> H₂SO₄ → Phosphoric Acid (Wet Process) → DAP / MAP / TSP / SSP Fertilizers
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manufacturing Routes (Brief Overview for HSE Context)

For an HSE-focused course, students must understand **where** sulphuric acid is produced to identify process-specific hazards. The two principal routes are:

### A. Contact Process (Modern, ≈ 95% of global production)
- **Step 1:** Combustion of Sulphur (or roasting of pyrite FeS₂) to produce SO₂
- **Step 2:** Catalytic oxidation of SO₂ → SO₃ (using V₂O₅ catalyst at 400–600°C)
- **Step 3:** Absorption of SO₃ in 98.3% H₂SO₄ (NOT in water — to avoid corrosive mist)
- **Step 4:** Dilution to desired concentration

### B. Lead Chamber Process (Older, obsolete in new plants)
- Uses NO₂ as catalyst
- Lower concentration acid (~62–68%)
- Largely discontinued due to environmental concerns (NOx emissions)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations — they are examiner favourites in KTU ESE papers.**

### Core Reaction Equations

| # | Equation | Application |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | S + O₂ → SO₂ | Sulphur burning (feedstock step) |
| 2 | 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃ | Catalytic oxidation (V₂O₅) |
| 3 | SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ | Final acid formation |
| 4 | H₂SO₄ + 2NaOH → Na₂SO₄ + 2H₂O | Neutralization (safety) |
| 5 | CaCO₃ + H₂SO₄ → CaSO₄ + H₂O + CO₂ | Limestone neutralization (spill control) |
| 6 | Fe + H₂SO₄(dilute) → FeSO₄ + H₂↑ | Corrosion reaction (carbon steel damage) |

### Key Quantitative Formulas

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| C₁V₁ = C₂V₂ | Dilution equation | mol/L or % w/w |
| Heat of dilution: Q = m · c · ΔT | Energy released on dilution | kJ |
| Normality: N = M × n (basicity = 2) | For H₂SO₄, n = 2 | eq/L |
| Specific gravity: SG = ρ_acid / ρ_water | Concentration indicator | Dimensionless |
| Molarity (from %w/w): M = (% × ρ × 10) / MW | Convert % to mol/L | mol/L |
| Stoichiometric SO₂:SO₃ ratio | 1:1 mole basis | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Boundary Condition:**
> SO₃ must NEVER be absorbed directly into water — this produces a dense, corrosive acid **mist** that is extremely difficult to capture. Always use **98.3% H₂SO₄** as the absorbing medium.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **fertilizer industry**, these formulas are applied in:

1. **Designing acid dilution systems** — preventing thermal runaway (heat of dilution can raise temperature by **≈ 50–80°C** per dilution step).
2. **Calculating lime/limestone requirements** for spill containment bunds.
3. **Sizing scrubbers** for SO₂ and SO₃ emissions (Double Contact Double Absorption — DCDA plants achieve **> 99.8% conversion**).
4. **Material selection** — carbon steel is acceptable at **> 93% H₂SO₄** but rapidly corrodes at **< 80%** concentration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Molarity of Commercial Sulphuric Acid

**Problem Statement:**
Commercial concentrated sulphuric acid is labelled as **98% w/w** with a density of **1.84 g/cm³**. Calculate its **molarity**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Consider **1000 mL (1 L)** of the acid solution.

$$
\text{Mass of solution} = V \times \rho = 1000 \, \text{mL} \times 1.84 \, \text{g/mL}
$$

$$
\text{Mass of solution} = 1840 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the mass of pure H₂SO₄ in this solution.

$$
\text{Mass of H₂SO₄} = 1840 \, \text{g} \times \frac{98}{100}
$$

$$
\text{Mass of H₂SO₄} = 1803.2 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert the mass of H₂SO₄ to moles using the molar mass (**MW = 98.079 g/mol**).

$$
\text{Moles of H₂SO₄} = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{MW}} = \frac{1803.2 \, \text{g}}{98.079 \, \text{g/mol}}
$$

$$
\text{Moles of H₂SO₄} = 18.385 \, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 4:** Since this is the amount in **1 L**, molarity is directly obtained.

$$
M = 18.385 \, \text{mol/L} \approx \mathbf{18.4 \, M}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Commercial H₂SO₄ is approximately **18 M**. This is the standard reference value used in all KTU stoichiometric problems.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Heat Released During Acid Dilution

**Problem Statement:**
A plant operator dilutes **500 kg** of 98% H₂SO₄ with water to produce **70% H₂SO₄**. If the heat of dilution is approximately **−670 kJ/kg** of H₂SO₄ (approx. for this concentration drop), calculate the heat released and the temperature rise if the final solution mass is **700 kg** with an average specific heat capacity of **3.5 kJ/(kg·K)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Determine the mass of pure H₂SO₄ present initially.

$$
m_{\text{acid}} = 500 \, \text{kg} \times 0.98 = 490 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the total heat released during dilution.

$$
Q = m_{\text{acid}} \times \Delta H_{\text{dilution}}
$$

$$
Q = 490 \, \text{kg} \times 670 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q = 328{,}300 \, \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the temperature rise using:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{Q}{m_{\text{total}} \times c_p}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{328{,}300 \, \text{kJ}}{700 \, \text{kg} \times 3.5 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{328{,}300}{2450} = \mathbf{134 \, °C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Implication (KTU Examiner Favourite):**
> A temperature rise of **≈ 134°C** is dangerously close to water's boiling point. **Always add acid to water** (never water to acid) — this is the single most repeated safety rule in KTU ESE papers.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Limestone Neutralization Stoichiometry

**Problem Statement:**
A tanker spill releases **1000 L** of 80% H₂SO₄ (density = 1.73 g/cm³). Calculate the **mass of limestone (CaCO₃, 95% pure)** required to completely neutralize the spill.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate the mass of spilled solution.

$$
m_{\text{solution}} = 1000 \, \text{L} \times 1000 \, \text{mL/L} \times 1.73 \, \text{g/mL}
$$

$$
m_{\text{solution}} = 1{,}730{,}000 \, \text{g} = 1730 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the mass of pure H₂SO₄.

$$
m_{\text{H₂SO₄}} = 1730 \, \text{kg} \times 0.80 = 1384 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert mass of H₂SO₄ to moles.

$$
n_{\text{H₂SO₄}} = \frac{1{,}384{,}000 \, \text{g}}{98.079 \, \text{g/mol}} = 14{,}113.6 \, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply stoichiometry of the reaction:

$$
\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2
$$

The mole ratio is **1:1**, so:

$$
n_{\text{CaCO}_3} = 14{,}113.6 \, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to mass of CaCO₃ (MW = 100.087 g/mol).

$$
m_{\text{CaCO}_3 \, (\text{pure})} = 14{,}113.6 \, \text{mol} \times 100.087 \, \text{g/mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{CaCO}_3 \, (\text{pure})} = 1{,}412{,}580 \, \text{g} \approx 1412.6 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 6:** Adjust for 95% purity of limestone.

$$
m_{\text{limestone}} = \frac{1412.6 \, \text{kg}}{0.95} = \mathbf{1486.9 \, \text{kg}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Approximately **1.49 tonnes of limestone** are required to neutralize just **1 m³** of 80% H₂SO₄ spill. This is why **secondary containment bunds** and **emergency spill kits** are mandatory in any fertilizer plant storing H₂SO₄.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: HSE Hazards of Sulphuric Acid

| Hazard Category | Specific Risk | Threshold / Limit Value | Control Measure |
|----------------|---------------|-------------------------|-----------------|
| Inhalation | Respiratory tract burns, pulmonary edema | TLV-TWA: **0.2 mg/m³** | Local exhaust ventilation, SCBA |
| Skin Contact | Severe chemical burns (3rd degree) | — | Acid-resistant PPE (Neoprene, Viton) |
| Eye Contact | Permanent blindness | — | Chemical splash goggles + eyewash |
| Reactivity | Violent exothermic reaction with water | ΔT up to **≈ 134°C** | Always add acid to water slowly |
| Corrosion | Attacks carbon steel at < 80% conc. | — | Use SS-316 or PTFE-lined equipment |
| Environmental | Acid rain (SO₂/SO₃), soil pH drop | SO₂ emission < **400 mg/Nm³** (KTU/EPCB) | DCDA scrubbers, alkaline scrubbers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Contact Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sulphur Feed] --> B[Sulphur Melting & Filtration]
    B --> C[Combustion Furnace]
    C --> D[SO2 Gas Generation]
    D --> E[Gas Cooling & Cleaning]
    E --> F[Converter 1 - V2O5 Catalyst]
    F --> G[Converter 2 - V2O5 Catalyst]
    G --> H[Converter 3 - V2O5 Catalyst]
    H --> I[Intermediate Absorption Tower 98.3 percent H2SO4]
    I --> J[Converter 4 - Final Pass]
    J --> K[Final Absorption Tower]
    K --> L[98 percent Concentrated H2SO4 Product]
    L --> M[Storage Tank - Carbon Steel Lined]
    M --> N[Fertilizer Plant - Phosphoric Acid Unit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Exams:**
> - The **double-contact double-absorption (DCDA)** layout (two absorption towers) ensures SO₂ conversion efficiency **> 99.8%**.
> - Each converter operates at progressively lower temperatures (≈ **600°C → 450°C → 400°C**) to push the equilibrium towards SO₃ (Le Chatelier's principle).

## 4.2 Sulphuric Acid Spill Emergency Response Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Spill Detected] --> S2{Assess Volume}
    S2 -->|Small less than 5 L| S3[Operator PPE Donning]
    S2 -->|Medium 5 to 50 L| S4[Shift Supervisor Alert]
    S2 -->|Large greater than 50 L| S5[Plant Emergency Declared]
    S3 --> S6[Neutralize with Soda Ash Na2CO3]
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S7[Activate Containment Bund]
    S7 --> S8[Emergency Limestone Application]
    S6 --> S9[Verify pH 6 to 9]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Dispose as Hazardous Waste]
```

## 4.3 HSE Control Hierarchy Pyramid (Adapted from KTU Module 1 Outcomes)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Elimination - Substitute Process] --> P2[Engineering Controls - Closed System, Scrubbers]
    P2 --> P3[Administrative Controls - SOPs, Training]
    P3 --> P4[PPE - Acid Suit, SCBA, Goggles]
    P4 --> P5[Emergency Response - Eyewash, Shower, Spill Kit]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always follow this hierarchy in KTU ESE answers** — examiners explicitly look for the **"hierarchy of controls"** mention when discussing acid safety.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define sulphuric acid and list three of its major industrial applications.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) is a strong dibasic mineral acid with a molecular weight of 98.079 g/mol, density 1.84 g/cm³, and boiling point 337°C. It is known as the **"King of Chemicals."**

**Three major industrial applications:**
1. Manufacture of **phosphoric acid** via the wet process for fertilizer production (≈ 60% of total consumption).
2. Production of **ammonium sulphate** fertilizer by reacting with ammonia.
3. Used in **petroleum refining**, mineral processing, and as an electrolyte in lead-acid batteries.

---

### Question 2
**Q: Why must SO₃ never be absorbed directly into water during sulphuric acid manufacture?** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Absorbing SO₃ directly in water produces a **persistent, sub-micron acid mist** that is extremely difficult to capture using conventional equipment. This leads to:
1. Severe **environmental pollution** (acid mist emission).
2. **Corrosion of downstream equipment** due to uncondensed SO₃.
3. **Health hazard** to operators (inhalation risk).

**Solution:** SO₃ is absorbed in **98.3% H₂SO₄** to form oleum (H₂S₂O₇), which is then diluted with water to the desired concentration. This eliminates mist formation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**Q: Describe in detail the health hazards of sulphuric acid exposure in a fertilizer plant. Discuss the first-aid measures and PPE requirements for safe handling.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (with Mark Distribution):**

#### Part (a): Health Hazards (7 Marks)

**1. Inhalation Hazards (2 Marks):**
- Exposure limit: **TLV-TWA = 0.2 mg/m³**
- Sulphuric acid mist causes **respiratory tract irritation**, pulmonary edema, and chronic bronchitis.
- Symptoms include coughing, choking sensation, chest tightness, and in severe cases, fluid accumulation in lungs (delayed onset up to 48 hours).

**2. Skin Contact Hazards (2 Marks):**
- Concentrated H₂SO₄ is **highly hygroscopic and dehydrating** — it removes water from skin tissue.
- Causes **severe chemical burns** (3rd degree) within minutes.
- Heat of reaction with skin moisture causes **thermal burns** in addition to chemical burns.

**3. Eye Contact Hazards (1.5 Marks):**
- Even **1 drop** of concentrated H₂SO₄ can cause **permanent corneal damage and blindness**.
- Acid mist at low concentration causes irritation and conjunctivitis.

**4. Chronic Effects (1.5 Marks):**
- Long-term exposure causes **dental erosion**, dermatitis, and increased risk of laryngeal cancer (IARC Group 1).
- Chronic bronchitis from prolonged mist exposure.

#### Part (b): First-Aid Measures & PPE (7 Marks)

**1. First-Aid Measures (3 Marks):**

| Exposure Type | Immediate Action |
|---------------|------------------|
| Skin Contact | Flush with **copious running water for ≥ 15 minutes**; remove contaminated clothing; do NOT apply ointment; seek medical help. |
| Eye Contact | Use **eyewash station for ≥ 15 minutes**; hold eyelids open; immediate ophthalmologist consultation. |
| Inhalation | Move victim to **fresh air**; administer oxygen; do NOT induce vomiting if ingested. |
| Ingestion | Do **NOT** induce vomiting (risk of re-burn); give milk or water if conscious; immediate hospitalization. |

**2. PPE Requirements (2 Marks):**
- **Chemical splash goggles** (ANSI Z87.1)
- **Face shield** for bulk handling
- **Acid-resistant suit** (Viton, Neoprene, or Butyl rubber — NOT natural rubber or PVC)
- **Chemical-resistant gloves** (Neoprene, ≥ 0.7 mm thickness)
- **Closed-toe chemical-resistant safety boots**
- **SCBA or full-face respirator with acid gas cartridge** for emergency response

**3. Engineering Controls (2 Marks):**
- Closed-loop transfer systems
- Local exhaust ventilation with acid mist scrubbers
- Emergency **eyewash and safety shower** within **10 seconds** walking distance
- **Acid-resistant flooring** with proper drainage to neutralization pit

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Most students write only "wash with water" for first aid and lose **1–2 marks**. The correct phrasing is **"flush with copious running water for at least 15 minutes"** — the **duration of 15 minutes** is the key marker the examiner is looking for. Also, mentioning the **specific PPE material (Viton/Neoprene)** fetches an extra mark versus generic terms like "rubber gloves."

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**Q: Explain the environmental aspects of sulphuric acid manufacture and use in the fertilizer industry. Discuss the control measures for SO₂ emissions and wastewater management.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer (with Mark Distribution):**

#### Part (a): Environmental Impacts (7 Marks)

**1. Air Pollution from SO₂ Emissions (3 Marks):**
- Inefficient conversion in the Contact Process releases SO₂ → contributes to **acid rain (H₂SO₃ / H₂SO₄ in atmosphere)**.
- Acid rain damages crops, acidifies water bodies, and corrodes buildings.
- **Emission standard:** Indian CPCB limit for SO₂ in fertilizer plants is **< 400 mg/Nm³** for existing units.

**2. Wastewater Generation (2 Marks):**
- Acidic process wastewater (pH often **< 2**) from scrubber bleed, equipment wash, and spillages.
- Contains dissolved sulphates, heavy metals, and process impurities.

**3. Solid Waste (Gypsum Stacks) (2 Marks):**
- Phosphogypsum is a by-product of phosphate fertilizer manufacture using H₂SO₄.
- Contains traces of **radium, fluoride, and heavy metals** → classified as hazardous waste.
- Stacking requires lined ponds and long-term monitoring (radioactive decay of Ra-226 over **> 100 years**).

#### Part (b): Control Measures (7 Marks)

**1. SO₂ Emission Control (3 Marks):**
- **Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA)** converter design — achieves **> 99.8% conversion efficiency**.
- **Alkali scrubbers** (NaOH or limestone slurry) as tail-gas treatment.
- Continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS) for real-time SO₂ tracking.

**2. Wastewater Treatment (2 Marks):**
- **Neutralization with lime** (Ca(OH)₂) to raise pH to **6.5–9.0**.
- **Sedimentation and sludge dewatering** for gypsum recovery (if applicable).
- **Reverse osmosis / ion exchange** for heavy metal removal.

**3. Solid Waste Management (2 Marks):**
- Use phosphogypsum in **cement manufacturing, road construction, and agricultural soil conditioning** (after radiological safety assessment).
- Secured landfill disposal for non-recoverable waste.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students confuse **phosphogypsum** (Fertilizer industry waste) with **FGD gypsum** (Power plant waste). For PESFT633, the focus is always on **phosphogypsum** and its **radioactive content** — examiners specifically test if students know about **Ra-226** and the **> 100-year monitoring** requirement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Must Memorize:**

- [x] **Chemical formula:** H₂SO₄ ; **MW:** 98.079 g/mol ; **Concentrated Molarity:** ≈ **18 M**
- [x] **King of Chemicals** — ≈ **50–60%** consumed by fertilizer industry
- [x] **Contact Process** = modern standard; **V₂O₅** catalyst; **400–600°C**
- [x] **NEVER absorb SO₃ in water** — use **98.3% H₂SO₄** to avoid acid mist
- [x] **DCDA process** = **> 99.8% SO₂ conversion**
- [x] **TLV-TWA:** **0.2 mg/m³** for H₂SO₄ mist
- [x] **First aid:** Flush **≥ 15 minutes** with running water
- [x] **PPE materials:** **Viton, Neoprene, Butyl rubber** (NOT natural rubber or PVC)
- [x] **Storage:** Carbon steel acceptable only **> 93%** concentration; below this, use **SS-316 or PTFE-lined**
- [x] **Neutralization rule:** **"Add acid to water"** (AAAW) — never reverse
- [x] **Limestone reaction:** CaCO₃ + H₂SO₄ → CaSO₄ + H₂O + CO₂ (1:1 mole ratio)
- [x] **Heat of dilution:** ≈ **670 kJ/kg** of H₂SO₄ — major safety hazard in dilution operations
- [x] **Phosphogypsum:** contains **Ra-226**, monitored for **> 100 years**
- [x] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Engineering → Administrative → PPE → Emergency Response

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Sulphuric Acid**
