topics.title: Major Pollutants Generated in Fertiliser Manufacturing Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Major Pollutants Generated in Fertiliser Manufacturing Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pollutant (KTU 2024 Definition):** A pollutant generated in fertiliser manufacturing is any gaseous, liquid, or solid substance released into the environment (air, water, or soil) as a direct or indirect by-product of the chemical conversion of raw materials (such as natural gas, naphtha, sulphur, phosphate rock, and potash) into nitrogenous, phosphatic, or potassic nutrient products, in concentrations that exceed the permissible limits prescribed by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, major pollutants from the fertiliser industry are classified into three principal environmental compartments:

| Pollutant Stream | Common Name in Industry | Typical Sources |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Pollutants** | Stack emissions & fugitives | Reformer stacks, acid plants, prilling towers |
| **Water Pollutants** | Liquid effluents | Process condensate, cooling blowdown, gypsum pond water |
| **Solid Pollutants** | Hazardous & non-hazardous waste | Phosphogypsum, spent catalyst, slag, sludge |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fertiliser Plant as a "Kitchen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Think of a Fertiliser Plant Like a Pressure Cooker Kitchen:**
> Imagine you are cooking a five-dish meal in a single busy kitchen. To prepare the main dish (urea or DAP), you must first pre-cook the ingredients (steam reforming, acidulation, neutralisation). During this pre-cooking:
> - **Smoke** rises from the burner (analogous to **NOx, SO2, CO2** from reformer and acid stacks).
> - **Steam** leaks from the lid (analogous to **ammonia slip** and **acid mist**).
> - **Spilled water** collects on the floor (analogous to **process condensate with ammonia and urea**).
> - **Peelings and bones** are thrown away (analogous to **phosphogypsum** and **spent catalyst**).
> Just as a chef must manage smoke, steam, spillage, and waste, an environmental engineer must identify, quantify, and treat every pollutant stream — otherwise the kitchen (environment) becomes polluted.

### 1.3 Categorical Hierarchy of Pollutants

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to master **seven** primary pollutant categories emitted across the fertiliser value chain:

1. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NO, NO2, N2O)** — collectively termed **NOx**.
2. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO2)** and **Sulphur Trioxide (SO3)**.
3. **Ammonia (NH3)** — both gaseous slip and dissolved.
4. **Fluorides (HF, SiF4)** — specific to phosphatic fertilisers.
5. **Particulate Matter (PM10, PM2.5)** — dust from rock grinding, prilling, granulation.
6. **Greenhouse Gases — CO2 and N2O.**
7. **Process Liquid Effluents** — containing ammonia, urea, nitrates, phosphates, fluorides, and **Heavy Metals (Cd, Pb, Hg, As, Cr)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners in Dec 2024 and June 2025 consistently tested **process-wise identification of pollutants**. Memorise at least **two specific pollutants per major process** (ammonia, urea, nitric acid, sulphuric acid, phosphoric acid, DAP/NPK).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Process-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Ammonia Plant (Steam Reforming of Natural Gas / Naphtha)

**Core Reaction Sequence:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Ni catalyst, } 850^\circ\text{C}} \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\text{Primary Reformer})$$

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Shift converter}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (\text{Shift Conversion})$$

$$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{High pressure}} 2\text{NH}_3 \quad (\text{Ammonia Synthesis Loop})$$

**Pollutants Generated:**

| Pollutant | Source Point | Why It Forms |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** (high volume) | Shift converter & methanator outlet | Inherent to the process for H2 purification |
| **CO** (trace) | Reformer furnace, partial oxidation | Incomplete combustion of fuel |
| **NOx** | Reformer furnace burners | Thermal NOx at **>1300 °C** flame temperature |
| **H2S** | Feed gas desulphurisation | Sulphur impurity in natural gas |
| **NH3 slip** | Synthesis loop purges | Incomplete conversion (typical 15–20% per pass) |
| **Cyanide (CN⁻)** | Wastewater from CO2 scrubber | Forms in alkaline scrubbers |
| **Particulates** | Catalyst dust, fly ash | Attrition in reformer/secondary reformer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **CO2 released from an ammonia plant** is the **single largest greenhouse gas emission** in nitrogen fertiliser manufacture. A 1000 MTPD ammonia plant emits approximately **1700–2000 MT/day of CO2** — this is the foundation of **Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS)** discussions in the industry.

---

#### 2.1.2 Nitric Acid Plant (Ostwald Process)

**Core Reactions:**

$$4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt-Rh gauze, } 900^\circ\text{C}} 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2$$

$$3\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NO}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

- **Nitric Oxide (NO)** and **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2)** — collectively **NOx**, the dominant stack emission.
- **Nitrous Oxide (N2O)** — a potent greenhouse gas with **GWP ≈ 298** (over 100 years).
- **Ammonia slip** — unconverted NH3 from the converter (typically < 10 ppm).
- **Nitrate-laden wastewater** from absorber tail gas scrubbing.

---

#### 2.1.3 Sulphuric Acid Plant (Contact Process)

**Core Reactions:**

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$$

$$2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{V}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ catalyst}} 2\text{SO}_3$$

$$\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_7 \text{ (oleum)}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

- **SO2** — primary stack emission (must be reduced via **Double Contact Double Absorption — DCDA** to < **400 mg/Nm3**).
- **SO3 / Acid Mist** — forms sub-micron sulphuric acid droplets.
- **Spent V2O5 Catalyst** — solid hazardous waste.

---

#### 2.1.4 Urea Plant

**Core Reaction:**

$$2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

- **NH3 in vent gases** from carbamate decomposer.
- **CO2 vent losses** from purge.
- **Urea dust** from prilling tower.
- **Process condensate** containing **NH3 (0.5–2%)** and **urea (0.1–1%)** — must be hydrolysed before discharge.

---

#### 2.1.5 Phosphoric Acid Plant (Wet Process)

**Core Reaction:**

$$\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 10\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 10\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{HF}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

| Pollutant | Quantity per MT of P2O5 | Toxicity |
|---|---|---|
| **Phosphogypsum** | **4.5–5.5 MT** | Solid waste (radioactive due to Ra-226) |
| **Fluorides (HF, SiF4)** | **20–60 kg** | Severe respiratory irritant |
| **Phosphate dust** | Variable | Particulate matter |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | 5–100 g | Heavy metal, carcinogen |
| **Process water with P2O5** | 5–15 m³ | Eutrophication agent |

---

#### 2.1.6 DAP / NPK / Complex Fertiliser Plant

**Reactions:**

$$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4 \text{ (MAP)}$$

$$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4 \text{ (DAP)}$$

**Major Pollutants:**

- **NH3 slip** from granulator and dryer.
- **Fluorides** evolved during acidulation.
- **Particulate dust** from granulation.
- **HCl fumes** when MOP is added.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU Board Examinations — All Major Pollutant Equations**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_i = Q \times C_i \times 10^{-6}$ | Emission rate of pollutant *i* from stack gas | kg/hr |
| 2 | $C_i = \dfrac{E_i}{Q}$ | Pollutant concentration in stack (back-calculated) | mg/Nm³ |
| 3 | $E_{\text{NOx}} = 1.57 \times F_{\text{NH}_3} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{NOx}}}{M_{\text{NH}_3}}$ | NOx emission from nitric acid plant per tonne NH3 oxidised | kg NOx/MT HNO3 |
| 4 | $E_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.05 \times F_{\text{NH}_3}$ | N2O emission factor (modern plant) | kg N2O/MT HNO3 |
| 5 | $E_{\text{SO}_2} = 2 \times S \times \dfrac{M_{\text{SO}_2}}{M_{\text{S}}} \times (1-\eta_{\text{conv}})$ | SO2 emission from sulphur content in feed | kg SO2/hr |
| 6 | $E_{\text{CO}_2} = 1.73 \times \text{NH}_3 \text{ production (MTPD)}$ | CO2 emission factor for ammonia plant (average) | MTCO2/day |
| 7 | $M_{\text{gypsum}} = 5 \times \text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ production (MTPD)}$ | Phosphogypsum generated per MT P2O5 | MT/day |
| 8 | $F_{\text{fluoride}} = 0.04 \times \text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ production}$ | Fluoride emission factor (uncontrolled) | kg F/MT P2O5 |
| 9 | $L_{\text{eff}} = Q \times (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})$ | Mass loading removed by effluent treatment plant | kg/day |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{control}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency of control equipment | % |

Where:
- $E_i$ = emission rate of pollutant *i*
- $Q$ = flue gas volumetric flow rate (Nm³/hr)
- $C_i$ = concentration of pollutant (mg/Nm³)
- $F_{\text{NH}_3}$ = mass flow rate of NH3 oxidised (kg/hr)
- $M_{\text{NOx}} = 46$ g/mol; $M_{\text{NH}_3} = 17$ g/mol
- $M_{\text{SO}_2} = 64$ g/mol; $M_{\text{S}} = 32$ g/mol
- $\eta_{\text{conv}}$ = conversion efficiency of SO2 → SO3
- $\eta_{\text{control}}$ = removal efficiency

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These pollutant-generation equations are not academic abstractions — they are the **backbone of**:

1. **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** reports submitted to **MoEFCC** before plant expansion.
2. **Consent to Operate** applications under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**.
3. **Carbon credit calculations** under **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme for ammonia plants.
4. **Pollution control equipment sizing** — for example, **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** for NOx abatement requires the exact emission rate.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Stoichiometric Derivation: NOx Emission from a Nitric Acid Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 — KTU Board Frequency: HIGH**
> A nitric acid plant oxidises **10,000 kg/hr of ammonia** in the Ostwald converter. The conversion efficiency of NH3 to NO is **96%**, and 90% of the resulting NO is oxidised to NO2. The tail gas leaving the absorption tower contains unabsorbed NO and NO2 totalling **1200 ppm by volume** (dry basis, at STP). Calculate:
> (a) The mass of NO emitted per hour.
> (b) The volume of NOx (as NO2 equivalent) in Nm³/hr if the flue gas flow is **50,000 Nm³/hr**.
> (c) Whether the plant complies with the CPCB standard of **400 mg/Nm3** as NO2.

#### Step 1 — Determine NH3 actually converted to NO

The Ostwald reaction requires **17 g NH3 to produce 30 g NO**:

$$4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

So the stoichiometric ratio is:

$$\dfrac{M_{\text{NO}}}{M_{\text{NH}_3}} = \dfrac{30}{17}$$

NH3 converted to NO:

$$m_{\text{NH}_3,\text{converted}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.96 = 9{,}600 \text{ kg/hr}$$

NO produced (if fully absorbed):

$$m_{\text{NO, theoretical}} = 9{,}600 \times \dfrac{30}{17} = 16{,}941.18 \text{ kg/hr}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Real-World NO to NO2 Oxidation

90% of NO becomes NO2:

$$m_{\text{NO}_2,\text{ formed}} = 16{,}941.18 \times 0.90 = 15{,}247.06 \text{ kg/hr}$$

The remaining 10% remains as NO:

$$m_{\text{NO, unoxidised}} = 16{,}941.18 \times 0.10 = 1{,}694.12 \text{ kg/hr}$$

#### Step 3 — Convert NO to NO2-equivalent for emission reporting

Using molecular weight ratio (46/30):

$$m_{\text{NO}, \text{as NO}_2} = 1{,}694.12 \times \dfrac{46}{30} = 2{,}597.65 \text{ kg/hr as NO}_2 \text{ equivalent}$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate Total NOx as NO2 Equivalent

$$\text{Total NOx (as NO}_2\text{)} = 15{,}247.06 + 2{,}597.65 = 17{,}844.71 \text{ kg/hr}$$

#### Step 5 — Calculate Concentration in mg/Nm³ (Using Given Flue Gas Flow)

Given flue gas flow $Q = 50{,}000$ Nm³/hr, and concentration in emission = **1200 ppm**:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{NOx (as NO}_2\text{)}} &= \text{ppm} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{NO}_2}}{22.4} \\
&= 1200 \times \dfrac{46}{22.4} \\
&= 1200 \times 2.0536 \\
&= 2{,}464.29 \text{ mg/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 6 — Compliance Check Against CPCB Standard

$$\text{CPCB Standard} = 400 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

$$\text{Actual} = 2{,}464.29 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 > 400 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

**Result:** The plant **DOES NOT comply** with CPCB norms. A **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** or **Extended Absorption** system must be installed to reduce NOx levels.

---

### 3.2 Stoichiometric Derivation: CO2 Emission from an Ammonia Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 — KTU Board Frequency: MODERATE**
> An ammonia plant produces **1500 MTPD (Metric Tonnes Per Day)** using steam reforming of natural gas. Calculate:
> (a) Daily CO2 emission in MT/day.
> (b) Annual CO2 emission in tonnes.
> (c) The number of carbon credits (1 credit = 1 tCO2) earned if the plant reduces emissions by **20%** via CCS.

#### Step 1 — Use the Standard CO2 Emission Factor for Ammonia

$$\text{Emission Factor (EF)} = 1.73 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{tNH}_3$$

#### Step 2 — Daily CO2 Emission

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{CO}_2, \text{daily}} &= \text{Production} \times \text{EF} \\
&= 1500 \text{ MT/day} \times 1.73 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{tNH}_3 \\
&= 2{,}595 \text{ MT/day}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 3 — Annual CO2 Emission (assuming 330 operating days)

$$E_{\text{CO}_2, \text{annual}} = 2{,}595 \times 330 = 856{,}350 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

#### Step 4 — Carbon Credits from 20% Reduction

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ reduced} = 856{,}350 \times 0.20 = 171{,}270 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

$$\text{Carbon Credits} = 171{,}270 \text{ credits/year (since 1 credit} = 1 \text{ tCO}_2\text{)}$$

At a market price of approximately **₹800–1500 per credit**, this translates to ₹13.7–25.7 crores/year in revenue — a major economic incentive for CCS adoption.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant-to-Process Mapping

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Table — Memorise for 14-Mark Questions**

| Process | Primary Air Pollutant | Secondary Air Pollutant | Liquid Effluent | Solid Waste | Critical Control Technology |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ammonia (Reforming)** | CO2 (high volume) | NOx, CO, NH3 slip | Cyanide, NH3 wastewater | Spent catalyst (NiO) | MEA / Benfield CO2 removal |
| **Nitric Acid** | NOx (NO, NO2) | N2O (potent GHG), NH3 | Nitrate-rich water | Spent Pt-Rh gauze | SCR + Extended Absorption |
| **Sulphuric Acid** | SO2 | SO3 / acid mist | Dilute acid | Spent V2O5 catalyst | DCDA, wet scrubber |
| **Urea** | NH3, CO2 | Urea dust | Process condensate | Carbamate residue | Hydrolyser stripper |
| **Phosphoric Acid** | Fluorides (HF, SiF4) | Particulate dust | P2O5-laden water | **Phosphogypsum (4.5 MT/MT P2O5)** | Wet scrubber, gypsum pond |
| **DAP / NPK** | NH3, Fluorides | HCl fume, dust | Phosphate + ammonia | Granulation fines | Cyclone + wet scrubber |
| **Complex Fertilisers** | NH3 | Particulates | Mixed nutrient liquor | Various | Bag filter + scrubber |

### 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Notes

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

> List **any six major air pollutants** generated in fertiliser manufacturing processes and name one process that generates each.

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| S.No | Pollutant | Generating Process |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | NO / NO2 (NOx) | Nitric acid plant (Ostwald process) |
| 2 | SO2 | Sulphuric acid plant (Contact process) |
| 3 | NH3 slip | Urea plant (carbamate decomposer) |
| 4 | Fluorides (HF, SiF4) | Phosphoric acid plant (acidulation of rock) |
| 5 | CO2 | Ammonia plant (CO2 removal section) |
| 6 | Particulate dust | DAP / NPK plant (granulator / dryer) |

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Supplementary]`

> What is **phosphogypsum**? State its environmental significance and two methods of safe disposal or utilisation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Phosphogypsum (1 mark):** Phosphogypsum is the calcium sulphate dihydrate (CaSO4·2H2O) by-product generated when phosphate rock is acidulated with sulphuric acid in the wet-process manufacture of phosphoric acid. Approximately **4.5–5.5 tonnes** of phosphogypsum are produced per tonne of P2O5 manufactured.

**Environmental Significance (1 mark):**
- Contains trace radioactivity (Radium-226) from the phosphate rock.
- Generates acidic leachate if improperly stored, contaminating groundwater.
- Requires large land area for stack disposal (about 1 hectare per 100 TPD P2O5).

**Disposal / Utilisation Methods (1 mark):**
1. **Cement Industry Use** — as a retarder in cement clinker manufacture (replacing natural gypsum).
2. **Agricultural Use** — after purification, as a soil conditioner / sulphur fertiliser.
3. **Backfilling of abandoned mines.**
4. **Construction material** — in plaster of Paris, gypsum boards, and bricks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by listing pollutants without naming the **specific process unit** that generates them. Always pair the pollutant with the source (e.g., "NH3 slip from urea carbamate decomposer" — NOT just "NH3").
> - Students forget to mention **N2O** as a greenhouse gas from nitric acid plants — this is a frequently-missed 1-mark point.
> - In numerical problems, students often **mix up the molecular weights** of NO (30) and NO2 (46). Memorise: NO = 30, NO2 = 46, NH3 = 17, SO2 = 64, CO2 = 44.
> - Students present **NOx concentration in ppm** without converting to **mg/Nm³** using the ideal gas equation at STP — examiners deduct 1 mark for this.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2025 - Expected Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat flow diagrams, explain the major **air pollutants generated** in **(i) Ammonia plant**, **(ii) Nitric acid plant**, and **(iii) Phosphoric acid plant**. For each plant, identify **at least three pollutants** with their **source points** and **control technologies**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sulphuric acid plant burns **50 tonnes/day of sulphur** to produce H2SO4. The conversion efficiency of SO2 to SO3 in the converter is **98%**. The stack gas flow rate is **20,000 Nm³/hr** measured at STP. Calculate:
   (i) The mass of SO2 emitted per day in kg/day.
   (ii) The concentration of SO2 in the stack gas in mg/Nm³ and ppm (by volume).
   (iii) Whether the plant meets the CPCB emission standard of **400 mg/Nm³**.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Ammonia Plant Air Pollutants (2.5 marks):**

| Pollutant | Source Point | Control Technology |
|---|---|---|
| CO2 | Shift converter & CO2 removal system | MDEA / Benfield absorption (recovered for urea feedstock) |
| NOx | Primary reformer furnace burners | Low-NOx burners, SCR |
| NH3 slip | Synthesis loop purge | Recovery as ammonium nitrate / urea |
| CO | Reformer flue gas | Complete combustion with excess O2 |
| H2S | Feed gas desulphuriser | ZnO adsorber beds |

**(ii) Nitric Acid Plant Air Pollutants (2.5 marks):**

| Pollutant | Source Point | Control Technology |
|---|---|---|
| NO / NO2 | Converter tail gas (main stack) | Extended absorption + SCR |
| N2O | Converter catalyst bed | High-temperature thermal decomposition (secondary catalyst) |
| NH3 slip | Converter inlet (unreacted) | Optimised Pt-Rh gauze operation |
| Acid mist | Absorber & bleaching section | Mist eliminators / demisters |

**(iii) Phosphoric Acid Plant Air Pollutants (2 marks):**

| Pollutant | Source Point | Control Technology |
|---|---|---|
| HF, SiF4 | Reaction tank & filter | Wet scrubber with silica recirculation |
| Particulate dust | Rock grinding, conveying | Bag filters / cyclones |
| Phosphate mist | Evaporator section | Mist eliminators |

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Sulphur burnt = 50 tonnes/day = 50,000 kg/day
- Conversion efficiency η = 98% = 0.98
- Stack gas flow Q = 20,000 Nm³/hr
- CPCB standard = 400 mg/Nm³

**Step 1 — Mass of SO2 theoretically produced from 50 tonnes of S**

Reaction: S + O2 → SO2

Molecular weight ratio:

$$\dfrac{M_{\text{SO}_2}}{M_{\text{S}}} = \dfrac{64}{32} = 2$$

Theoretical SO2 production:

$$m_{\text{SO}_2, \text{theoretical}} = 50{,}000 \times 2 = 100{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of SO2 actually emitted (unconverted)**

Emitted SO2 fraction = (1 − η) = 1 − 0.98 = 0.02

$$m_{\text{SO}_2, \text{emitted}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.02 = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Per-hour emission rate**

$$E_{\text{SO}_2} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{24} = 83.33 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Concentration in mg/Nm³**

$$C_{\text{SO}_2} = \dfrac{E_{\text{SO}_2} \times 10^6}{Q} = \dfrac{83.33 \times 10^6}{20{,}000} = 4{,}166.5 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Concentration in ppm (using ideal gas: 1 ppm = M/22.4 mg/Nm³)**

$$\text{ppm} = \dfrac{C}{M_{\text{SO}_2}/22.4} = \dfrac{4{,}166.5}{64/22.4} = \dfrac{4{,}166.5}{2.857} = 1{,}458.4 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 6 — Compliance Check**

$$C_{\text{actual}} = 4{,}166.5 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 > 400 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 \text{ (CPCB limit)}$$

**Result:** The plant **DOES NOT comply** with CPCB norms. It must install a **Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA)** system to improve conversion to >99.7% and reduce SO2 emissions below 400 mg/Nm³.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **major water pollutants** generated in fertiliser industries. Discuss the characteristics, treatment methods, and **CPCB effluent discharge standards** for the following:
   (i) Ammonia plant process condensate
   (ii) Phosphoric acid plant gypsum pond water
   (iii) Nitric acid plant weak acid effluent

**(b) [7 Marks]** A phosphoric acid plant produces **300 MTPD of P2O5** by the wet process. Calculate:
   (i) The quantity of **phosphogypsum** generated per day (assume 5 MT gypsum per MT P2O5).
   (ii) The **fluoride emissions** in kg/day, if the fluoride generation factor is **0.04 kg F per kg P2O5** and 90% is recovered by wet scrubbing.
   (iii) The land area required for stacking the gypsum for **365 days** at a stack height of **10 metres** (assume gypsum bulk density = 1.5 tonnes/m³).

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Ammonia Plant Process Condensate (2.5 marks):**

- **Sources:** Condensate from CO2 regeneration, process steam drains, urea synthesis purge (if integrated).
- **Key pollutants:** NH3 (50–2000 mg/L), methanol, suspended solids, low BOD/COD.
- **Treatment:** Steam stripping → recovered NH3 recycled; biological polishing; multi-effect evaporation.
- **CPCB Standard (for fertiliser industry):** NH3 ≤ 30 mg/L, pH 6.5–8.5, BOD ≤ 30 mg/L, COD ≤ 250 mg/L, SS ≤ 100 mg/L.

**(ii) Phosphoric Acid Plant Gypsum Pond Water (2.5 marks):**

- **Sources:** Wash water from gypsum filters, pond overflow.
- **Key pollutants:** P2O5 (0.5–2% as H3PO4), fluorides (800–2000 mg/L as F), low pH (1.5–3.0), suspended solids.
- **Treatment:** Lime neutralisation (pH to 6–7), fluoride precipitation as CaF2 or adsorption on activated alumina, settling in clarifier.
- **CPCB Standard:** Total P (as P) ≤ 5 mg/L, fluoride ≤ 2 mg/L, pH 6.5–8.5, SS ≤ 100 mg/L.

**(iii) Nitric Acid Plant Weak Acid Effluent (2 marks):**

- **Sources:** Absorber weak acid stream, bleaching section condensate.
- **Key pollutants:** HNO3 (1–5%), nitrates (5000–15000 mg/L), fluorides (trace).
- **Treatment:** Alkaline neutralisation with lime or caustic soda; biological denitrification (rare in industry); recycling back to absorption tower.
- **CPCB Standard:** Total N as NO3 ≤ 10 mg/L (drinking water), pH 6.5–8.5.

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- P2O5 production = 300 MTPD
- Gypsum factor = 5 MT per MT P2O5
- Fluoride generation factor = 0.04 kg F per kg P2O5
- Scrubber recovery = 90%
- Stack height = 10 m
- Bulk density of gypsum = 1.5 tonnes/m³
- Period = 365 days

**(i) Phosphogypsum Generated per Day (2 marks):**

$$M_{\text{gypsum, daily}} = 300 \times 5 = 1{,}500 \text{ MT/day}$$

**(ii) Fluoride Emissions per Day (3 marks):**

Total fluoride generated:

$$F_{\text{generated}} = 0.04 \times 300{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg F/day}$$

Fluoride emitted (after 90% recovery = 10% emitted):

$$F_{\text{emitted}} = 12{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1{,}200 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(iii) Land Area Required for 365-Day Gypsum Stack (2 marks):**

Annual gypsum generated:

$$M_{\text{gypsum, annual}} = 1{,}500 \times 365 = 547{,}500 \text{ MT/year}$$

Volume of stack (at bulk density 1.5 t/m³):

$$V_{\text{stack}} = \dfrac{M}{\rho} = \dfrac{547{,}500}{1.5} = 365{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

At stack height h = 10 m:

$$\text{Land area} A = \dfrac{V}{h} = \dfrac{365{,}000}{10} = 36{,}500 \text{ m}^2 = 3.65 \text{ hectares}$$

This massive land requirement (~3.65 hectares/year per plant) is why **phosphogypsum utilisation** is a national priority under the **Department of Fertilizers** policy framework.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Major Pollutants Generated in Fertiliser Manufacturing**

- [ ] **Ammonia Plant** → CO2 (largest GHG), NOx, CO, NH3 slip, cyanide in effluent, spent Ni catalyst.
- [ ] **Nitric Acid Plant** → NOx (NO + NO2), N2O (GWP = 298), NH3 slip, weak acid effluent.
- [ ] **Sulphuric Acid Plant** → SO2 (dominant), SO3 / acid mist, spent V2O5 catalyst.
- [ ] **Urea Plant** → NH3 vent, CO2 vent, urea dust, process condensate (NH3 + urea).
- [ ] **Phosphoric Acid Plant** → HF / SiF4, particulate dust, **phosphogypsum (4.5–5.5 MT/MT P2O5)**, Cd / Pb / Hg traces, radioactive Ra-226.
- [ ] **DAP / NPK Plant** → NH3 slip, fluorides, dust, HCl fume.
- [ ] **Key Emission Factors to Memorise:** CO2 = 1.73 t/tNH3; Fluoride = 0.04 kg F/kg P2O5; NOx (modern plant) ≈ 0.2–0.5 kg NO2/tHNO3 after abatement.
- [ ] **CPCB Standards (must remember):** NOx (as NO2) ≤ 400 mg/Nm³; SO2 ≤ 400 mg/Nm³; NH3 (stack) ≤ 175 mg/Nm³; Fluoride (stack) ≤ 25 mg/Nm³; PM ≤ 150 mg/Nm³.
- [ ] **Conversion Formula to remember:** ppm (vol) → mg/Nm³ = ppm × M / 22.4 (at STP).
- [ ] **Control Technologies:** SCR for NOx; DCDA for SO2; Wet scrubber for HF; Hydrolyser-stripper for urea effluent; Gypsum stack + cement reuse for phosphogypsum.
- [ ] **Statutory Framework:** Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EPA 1986, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules 1989.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Strategy:** In any KTU 2024 question on "major pollutants," always structure your answer in **four blocks**: (1) Pollutant list with source point, (2) Stoichiometric / numerical basis, (3) Health and environmental impact, (4) Control technology. Examiners reward structured answers with full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollution Control Measures in Fertiliser Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pollution Control Measures in Fertiliser Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pollution Control Measures in the Fertiliser Industry** refer to the integrated set of engineering technologies, process modifications, and management practices deployed at various unit operations (ammonia synthesis, urea prilling, phosphoric acid production, granulation, etc.) to prevent, reduce, or treat the release of harmful gaseous, liquid, and solid effluents into the environment. These measures operate on the **3R hierarchy** — *Reduce* (process modification), *Reuse* (in-plant recycling), and *Recover* (end-of-pipe treatment) — and form the technical backbone of any Environmental Management System (EMS) conforming to **ISO 14001:2015** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A fertilizer plant is classified as a **"Red Category"** highly polluting industry by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India. Therefore, pollution control is not optional — it is a statutory requirement under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Kitchen"

Imagine a large commercial kitchen where a chef (the chemical reaction) cooks food (fertiliser product). The kitchen naturally produces:
- **Steam & smoke** (air emissions — NOx, SOx, NH₃, HF, particulates)
- **Dirty dishwater** (liquid effluent — acidic/alkaline streams, ammonia, phosphate, fluoride)
- **Vegetable peels & waste** (solid waste — phosphogypsum, catalyst, slag, sludge)
- **Noise from exhaust fans and grinders** (acoustic pollution)

Now, a clean kitchen would use a **chimney hood with filters** (scrubber/ESP), a **water recycling sink with grease trap** (ETP/STP), a **compost bin** (gypsum recycling), and **silent exhaust fans** (acoustic dampers). **Pollution control in a fertiliser plant is exactly this — installing the right "filters and traps" at every dirty step of the process.** The goal is to make the kitchen so clean that the surrounding neighbourhood (environment) never smells, sees, or tastes the cooking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Environmental Metrics to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - **PM (Particulate Matter):** measured in mg/Nm³ (normal cubic metre of dry flue gas)
> - **SO₂ emission standard:** < **50 mg/Nm³** for new fertilizer plants
> - **NOx emission standard:** < **100 mg/Nm³**
> - **Effluent pH range (discharge norm):** **6.5 to 8.5**
> - **Fluoride in effluent:** < **2 mg/L**
> - **Ammoniacal Nitrogen (NH₃-N):** < **50 mg/L**

### 1.3 Classification of Pollutants in Fertiliser Industry

| Pollutant Stream | Primary Source Unit | Major Pollutants |
|---|---|---|
| **Gaseous (Stack)** | Urea prill tower, ammonia reformer, granulator | NH₃, NOx, SO₂, HF, urea dust, PM |
| **Liquid (Effluent)** | Cooling tower blowdown, phosphoric acid plant, urea hydrolysis | Fluorides, phosphates, ammonia, urea, heavy metals |
| **Solid (Waste)** | Phosphoric acid plant, slag handling, spent catalyst | Phosphogypsum, slag, spent Ni-catalyst, sludge |
| **Noise** | Compressors, grinding mills, prill tower fans | > 85 dB(A) continuous |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Air Pollution Control Measures

#### 2.1.1 Control of Particulate Matter (PM)

Particulate matter is generated primarily in:
- **Urea prill towers** (urea dust carryover)
- **DAP/NPK granulators** (dust from solid fertilizer handling)
- **Phosphate rock grinding & handling**

**Control Equipment Hierarchy (in increasing efficiency):**

1. **Cyclone Separators** (Efficiency: 70–90% for >10 µm particles)
   - Centrifugal force throws particles to the wall; collected via hopper.
   - Used as **pre-cleaner** before higher-efficiency devices.

2. **Wet Scrubbers** (Efficiency: 90–99% for >5 µm)
   - Liquid (water or reagent solution) contacts dust-laden gas.
   - Examples: **Venturi scrubber**, **packed-bed scrubber**, **impingement plate scrubber**.
   - **Urea dust** is recovered by wet scrubbing with water → recycled as liquor.

3. **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** (Efficiency: >99.5% for fine dust)
   - Particles are charged by a discharge electrode and collected on grounded plates.
   - Widely used in **coal-fired boilers** in fertilizer complexes for power generation.

4. **Bag Filters / Fabric Filters** (Efficiency: 99.9%)
   - Porous fabric (Nomex, polyester, PTFE) captures dust on the surface.
   - Periodic reverse-pulse cleaning regenerates the filter.
   - **Standard design emission:** < **30 mg/Nm³**.

#### 2.1.2 Control of NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen)

Sources: **Primary reformer furnace** (ammonia plant), **high-temperature combustion**.

**Control Techniques:**
- **Low-NOx Burners (LNB):** Staged combustion reduces peak flame temperature.
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** Inject NH₃ over a V₂O₅/TiO₂ catalyst at **300–400 °C** to convert NOx → N₂ + H₂O.
- **Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR):** Urea or NH₃ injected at **800–1100 °C** without catalyst.

**SCR Core Reaction:**

$$4\,\text{NH}_3 + 4\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

#### 2.1.3 Control of SO₂ and Fluorides

**SO₂** is generated in **sulphuric acid plants** (H₂SO₄ manufacturing) and from sulphur in fuel.

- **Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA)** in H₂SO₄ plant achieves **>99.7% SO₂ conversion** — the *cleanest* process inherently.
- **Flue Gas Desulphurization (FGD)** using **Lime / Limestone slurry**:

$$\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$

**Fluorides (HF, SiF₄)** — characteristic of **phosphoric acid** and **SSP plants**.

- **Wet scrubbing with water** forms weak **HF/H₂SiF₆** solution.
- **Scrubbing with SiO₂ / alumina** captures SiF₄.
- Recovered fluoride is processed into **AlF₃, NaF, or fluorosilicic acid (H₂SiF₆)** — a useful by-product.

#### 2.1.4 Control of Ammonia and Urea Dust

- **Urea prill tower** emits urea dust + NH₃. Modern plants use:
  - **Bulk Blending & Granulation (vs Prilling):** Eliminates tower emissions.
  - **Prill Tower Wet Scrubbing:** A water-spray ring at the tower top collects dust → recycled to melt section.

- **Ammonia recovery** from vent gas using **Absorption in water** (forming NH₄OH) or **acid scrubbing** (forming (NH₄)₂SO₄).

### 2.2 Water Pollution Control — Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)

A typical fertilizer complex ETP operates in **3 stages**:

| Stage | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Equalization, neutralization, sedimentation | pH correction (6.5–8.5), grit removal |
| **Secondary** | Biological treatment (ASP, SBR, MBBR) | Organic load reduction (BOD/COD removal) |
| **Tertiary** | RO, ion exchange, activated carbon polishing | Nutrient removal (N, P, F) |

**Key Formulas Used in Effluent Design:**

- **BOD Removal Efficiency (%):**

$$\eta_{\text{BOD}} = \frac{\text{BOD}_{\text{in}} - \text{BOD}_{\text{out}}}{\text{BOD}_{\text{in}}} \times 100$$

- **F/M Ratio (Food to Microorganism ratio) for Activated Sludge Process:**

$$\text{F/M} = \frac{\text{BOD load per day (kg/day)}}{\text{Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids (kg)}}$$

- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):**

$$\text{HRT} = \frac{V}{Q}$$

where $V$ = reactor volume (m³), $Q$ = flow rate (m³/hr).

**Specific Fertiliser Industry Effluent Streams:**

1. **Phosphogypsum Pond Water (Pond Return Water):** Contains P₂O₅, F⁻, low pH. Recycled to plant for **re-acidulation** rather than discharged.
2. **Ammonia Plant Process Condensate:** Stripped of NH₃ in a **steam-stripping column** → NH₃ recovered as aqueous ammonia.
3. **Cooling Tower Blowdown:** High TDS, treated in **demineralization plant** or **RO** for reuse.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Zero Liquid Discharge" (ZLD)** is a key concept often asked in KTU exams. It implies recycling 100% of the treated effluent via **multiple-effect evaporators (MEE)** + **crystallizer**, ensuring no liquid leaves the plant boundary.

### 2.3 Solid Waste Management

| Solid Waste | Source | Utilization / Disposal |
|---|---|---|
| **Phosphogypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** | Phosphoric acid plant (4–5 t per t of P₂O₅) | Cement retarder, plaster of Paris, agriculture soil conditioner, road construction |
| **Spent Reformer Catalyst (NiO/Al₂O₃)** | Ammonia plant | Sent to authorised recyclers for Ni recovery |
| **Slag** from coal/oil-fired boilers | Boiler plant | Road sub-base, cement clinker manufacture |
| **ETP Sludge** | Wastewater treatment | Sent to secured landfill or used as manure filler |
| **Spent Molecular Sieves** | Drying section | Sold to metal reclaimers |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Collection Efficiency of Cyclone** | $\eta = 1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{d_{50}}\right)^2$ for $d \le d_{50}$ | Cyclone design |
| 2 | **SCR Reaction** | $4\text{NH}_3 + 4\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \to 4\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | NOx removal |
| 3 | **FGD Stoichiometry** | $\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{CO}_2$ | SO₂ removal |
| 4 | **BOD Removal Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{BOD}} = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100$ | Effluent design |
| 5 | **HRT** | $\text{HRT} = V/Q$ | Reactor design |
| 6 | **F/M Ratio** | $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | ASP design |
| 7 | **ESP Migration Velocity (Deutsch Equation)** | $w = \dfrac{d \cdot \ln(1-\eta)}{L}$ | ESP design |
| 8 | **Phosphogypsum Generation** | ~4–5 t per t of P₂O₅ produced | Mass balance |
| 9 | **Acoustic Power Reduction** | $\text{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ | Noise control |
| 10 | **Ammonia Stripping Factor** | $\dfrac{\text{NH}_3(\text{g})}{\text{NH}_4^+(\text{aq})} = K \cdot 10^{\text{pH}}$ | Stripper design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 14-mark problems, you will almost always need a **mass balance** plus a **pollution load calculation** like:
> $$\text{Pollution Load (kg/day)} = \text{Flow rate (m}^3\text{/day)} \times \text{Concentration (mg/L)} \times 10^{-3}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deutsch Equation for ESP Collection Efficiency

The **Deutsch Equation** is the cornerstone of electrostatic precipitator design and is a high-yield KTU topic.

**Step 1 — Define Variables**
- $w$ = migration velocity of particle (m/s) — drift velocity of charged particle towards the collection plate
- $A_c$ = effective collection area of plates (m²)
- $Q$ = volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)
- $\eta$ = fractional collection efficiency

**Step 2 — Write the First-Order Differential Equation**

Consider a control volume of gas flowing through the ESP. A particle drifting at velocity $w$ over a plate of length $dx$ has a probability $w \cdot dx / v$ of being collected, where $v = Q/A_c$ is the gas velocity.

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\frac{w \cdot dA_c}{Q}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Across the Entire ESP**

Integrating from inlet ($N_0$) to outlet ($N$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\frac{w}{Q} \int_0^{A_c} dA_c$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{w \cdot A_c}{Q}$$

**Step 4 — Define Collection Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0} = 1 - \frac{N}{N_0}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{w \cdot A_c}{Q}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to Solve for Plate Area (Design Problem)**

For a given efficiency $\eta$ and known $w, Q$:

$$A_c = -\frac{Q}{w} \cdot \ln(1-\eta)$$

This is the **design equation** used to size an ESP.

> [!NOTE]
> The Deutsch equation **assumes** (a) uniform gas flow, (b) uniform particle charging, (c) constant $w$. In practice, the **effective migration velocity** $w_e$ is empirically derived (typically **0.06–0.13 m/s** for fly-ash ESPs).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — ESP Sizing

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A fertilizer complex power plant burns 50 t/h of coal with an ash content of 8%. The flue gas flow is 1,20,000 Nm³/h, and the inlet dust loading is 15 g/Nm³. Design an ESP to achieve **99.5% efficiency**. Assume effective migration velocity $w = 0.10$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Inlet Dust Load**

$$\text{Dust load} = 1,20,000 \times 15 = 1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ g/h} = 1800 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Deutsch Equation**

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-wA_c/Q}$$

$$0.995 = 1 - e^{-0.10 \cdot A_c / (1,20,000/3600)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Q to m³/s**

$$Q = \frac{1,20,000}{3600} = 33.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A_c$**

$$1 - 0.995 = e^{-0.10 \cdot A_c / 33.33}$$

$$0.005 = e^{-0.10 \cdot A_c / 33.33}$$

Take natural log on both sides:

$$\ln(0.005) = -\frac{0.10 \cdot A_c}{33.33}$$

$$-5.298 = -\frac{0.10 \cdot A_c}{33.33}$$

$$A_c = \frac{5.298 \times 33.33}{0.10} = 1766 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer**

$$\boxed{A_c \approx 1766 \text{ m}^2}$$

For a 2-field ESP with plates 10 m tall, plate length required ≈ **88 m per pass.**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Pollution Control Devices

| Control Device | Target Pollutant | Working Principle | Removal Efficiency | Key Operating Cost | Merits / Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cyclone** | PM > 10 µm | Centrifugal force | 70–90% | Low | Simple, no moving parts; poor for fine dust |
| **Scrubber (Venturi)** | PM, SO₂, HF, NH₃ | Liquid–gas contact | 90–99% | Medium-High | Handles gases + particles; creates wastewater |
| **ESP** | PM (fine) | Electrostatic attraction | 99.5%+ | Medium | High capex, low opex; sensitive to resistivity |
| **Bag Filter** | PM (very fine) | Fabric filtration | 99.9% | Low | Excellent; bag wear; not for wet gases |
| **SCR** | NOx | Catalytic reduction | 80–95% | High (NH₃ cost) | Best for large plants; needs reagent |
| **SNCR** | NOx | Thermal reduction | 30–70% | Medium | Lower cost; lower efficiency |
| **FGD (Wet Lime)** | SO₂ | Alkaline scrubbing | 90–98% | High | Generates gypsum; large footprint |
| **Biofilter** | VOC, Odour | Microbial oxidation | 70–90% | Low | Eco-friendly; large area needed |
| **Reverse Osmosis** | TDS, F⁻, heavy metals | Membrane separation | 95%+ | High | Recovers water; membrane fouling |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Stripping Factor for Ammonia Removal

Ammonia in wastewater exists in two forms — molecular NH₃ and ionic NH₄⁺. The stripping efficiency depends on pH.

**Equilibrium Reaction:**

$$\text{NH}_4^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+$$

**Equilibrium constant expression:**

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{NH}_3][\text{H}^+]}{[\text{NH}_4^+]}$$

**Rearranging:**

$$\frac{[\text{NH}_3]}{[\text{NH}_4^+]} = \frac{K_a}{[\text{H}^+]} = K_a \cdot 10^{\text{pH}}$$

This ratio is the **Stripping Factor**. At pH > 11, >99% ammonia is in free NH₃ form and can be air-stripped efficiently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **Ammonia Stripper Column** in a fertilizer ETP: L/G (liquid-gas mass flow ratio) is the design parameter. Higher L/G → better mass transfer but larger column. Typical design L/G = **2000–5000**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Mass Balance — Phosphogypsum Generation

**Problem:** A DAP plant produces 300 t/day of phosphoric acid (as P₂O₅ equivalent). Calculate phosphogypsum generation per day.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric Generation**

Per tonne of P₂O₅ from rock, the reaction:

$$\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 10\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \to 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 10\text{CaSO}_4 + 2\text{HF}$$

**Step 2 — Use the Standard Industry Factor**

$$\text{Phosphogypsum} = 4.5 \times \text{P}_2\text{O}_5 \text{ production (t/day)}$$

$$= 4.5 \times 300 = 1350 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Generation**

$$= 1350 \times 330 \text{ (operating days)} = 4,45,500 \text{ t/yr} \approx 4.5 \text{ lakh tonnes/year}$$

**Step 4 — Utilization Implication**

If only **20%** is utilized (e.g., cement retarder), then 80% × 1350 = **1080 t/day** must be stored in a **phosphogypsum pond** requiring ~**50 acres per year** at 5 m depth.

This is why **phosphogypsum utilization** is a critical environmental challenge for the Indian fertilizer industry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Integrated Pollution Control Architecture in a Fertiliser Complex

The following **Mermaid Block Diagram** maps the source of each pollutant to its dedicated control device, satisfying the Mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no markdown inside node labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[RAW MATERIALS IN]:::in
    NH3[AMMONIA PLANT]:::unit
    UREA[UREA PLANT]:::unit
    H3PO4[PHOSPHORIC ACID PLANT]:::unit
    DAP[DAP NPK GRANULATION]:::unit
    BOILER[COAL BOILER]:::unit

    SCRUB1[WET SCRUBBER NH3 RECOVERY]:::ctrl
    SCRUB2[UREA PRILL TOWER SCRUBBER]:::ctrl
    ESP[ESP FOR FLY ASH]:::ctrl
    BAG[BAG FILTER DAP DUST]:::ctrl
    FGDSO2[FGD LIME STONE]:::ctrl
    HFABS[HF SCRUBBER SILICA BED]:::ctrl

    AIR[STACK EMISSION MONITORING]:::out
    ETP1[PRIMARY TREATMENT NEUTRALIZATION]:::out
    ETP2[SECONDARY BIOLOGICAL ASP]:::out
    ETP3[TERTIARY RO ION EXCHANGE]:::out
    ZLD[MEE CRYSTALLIZER ZERO LIQUID DISCHARGE]:::out
    POND[PHOSPHOGYPSUM POND]:::out

    IN --> NH3
    IN --> UREA
    IN --> H3PO4
    IN --> DAP
    IN --> BOILER

    NH3 -->|NOx SO2| SCRUB1
    UREA -->|NH3 Urea Dust| SCRUB2
    BOILER -->|PM SO2| ESP
    BOILER -->|PM SO2| FGDSO2
    DAP -->|PM| BAG
    H3PO4 -->|HF SiF4| HFABS

    SCRUB1 --> AIR
    SCRUB2 --> AIR
    ESP --> AIR
    BAG --> AIR
    FGDSO2 --> AIR
    HFABS --> AIR

    NH3 -->|Process Condensate| ETP1
    H3PO4 -->|Pond Return Water| ETP1
    DAP -->|Granulator Liquor| ETP1
    ETP1 --> ETP2
    ETP2 --> ETP3
    ETP3 --> ZLD

    H3PO4 -->|Phosphogypsum| POND
    BOILER -->|Bottom Ash| POND
    ETP2 -->|ETP Sludge| POND

    classDef in fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:**
> - **Green nodes** = process units generating pollution
> - **Blue nodes** = air pollution control devices
> - **Pink nodes** = water/solid waste control & treatment
> - Arrows show pollutant flow direction

---

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Wet Scrubbing System for HF Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Gas Containing HF SiF4]:::in --> W1[Stage 1 Water Scrubber]:::ctrl
    W1 --> W2[Stage 2 Recycle Water Scrubber]:::ctrl
    W2 --> W3[Stage 3 Demister Pad]:::ctrl
    W3 --> STACK[Clean Gas to Stack]:::out
    W1 -->|Weak Acid| PUMP[Pump to Recirculation Tank]:::ctrl
    PUMP --> H2SiF6[Storage H2SiF6 25 percent]:::out
    PUMP -->|Make-up Water| W1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-stage counter-current scrubbing** can recover HF/H₂SiF₆ at **25–40% concentration**, which is a saleable industrial chemical. This demonstrates the **"Waste to Wealth"** philosophy in pollution control.

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Selection of Particulate Control Device

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Particle Size] --> B{Size in micrometres}
    B -->|Greater than 10| C[Choose Cyclone as Pre-cleaner]
    B -->|Between 1 and 10| D{Is dust wet or hygroscopic}
    B -->|Less than 1| E{Is gas temperature below 200C}
    D -->|Yes| F[Use Wet Scrubber]
    D -->|No| G[Use Bag Filter Fabric]
    E -->|Yes| H[Use ESP]
    E -->|No| I[Use High Temperature Bag Filter or Wet Scrubber]
    C --> J[Final Discharge to Stack]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

---

### 4.4 ETP Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RAW[Combined Fertiliser Effluent]:::in --> EQ[Equalization Tank HRT 8 to 12 hr]:::ctrl
    EQ --> NEUT[Neutralization Lime or H2SO4]:::ctrl
    NEUT --> CLAR1[Primary Clarifier]:::ctrl
    CLAR1 --> BIO[Aeration Tank ASP F/M 0.2 to 0.5]:::ctrl
    BIO --> CLAR2[Secondary Clarifier]:::ctrl
    CLAR2 --> TER[RO or Ion Exchange]:::ctrl
    CLAR2 --> SLUDGE[Sludge to Sludge Thickener]:::out
    SLUDGE --> PRESS[Filter Press Cake to Landfill]:::out
    TER --> ZLD2[MEE Crystallizer]:::ctrl
    ZLD2 --> SALES[Recovered Salt to Market]:::out
    ZLD2 -->|Pure Water| REUSE[Recycle to Cooling Tower]:::out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. List any three air pollutants generated in a phosphoric acid plant and state one control device for each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Hydrogen Fluoride (HF)** → Controlled by **wet scrubbing with water** in a packed-bed scrubber, recovering H₂SiF₆.
2. **Particulate matter (phosphogypsum dust)** → Controlled by **bag filters** at transfer points.
3. **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂)** → Controlled inherently by the **DCDA contact process** (>99.7% conversion).
*(Any 3 valid pairs: 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. Differentiate between ESP and Bag Filter as particulate control devices.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | ESP | Bag Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Collection principle | Electrostatic | Mechanical filtration |
| Efficiency for <1 µm | Excellent | Very Good |
| Pressure drop | Very low (~10 mmWC) | Higher (~100–150 mmWC) |
| Sensitivity to dust resistivity | High | None |
| Water consumption | None | None (dry) |
| Capex | High | Moderate |
| *(3 marks — 3 valid differences)* |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

---

#### **Question A: (14 Marks)**

**(a) Describe the working principle of a Venturi Scrubber for particulate control. (7 Marks)**
**(b) A fertilizer plant emits 1,50,000 Nm³/h of flue gas at 150 °C with 20 g/Nm³ dust loading. Design a two-field ESP for 99.5% efficiency. Take effective migration velocity w = 0.10 m/s. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- A Venturi scrubber consists of a **converging section**, a **throat**, and a **diverging section**.
- Hot, dust-laden gas is accelerated to high velocity (50–150 m/s) at the **throat**.
- A scrubbing liquid is injected at the throat, atomized into fine droplets.
- **Inertial impaction** + **diffusion** cause particles to collide with droplets.
- The dust-laden droplets are separated in a **cyclonic separator** at the diverging end.
- Efficiency increases with **gas velocity** and **liquid-to-gas ratio (L/G)**.
- Pressure drop is the key design parameter; **>500 mmWC** for high efficiency.
- Applications: urea prill tower scrubbing, HF absorption. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

Given:
- $Q = 1,50,000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} = 41.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\eta = 0.995$, $w = 0.10$ m/s

Apply Deutsch Equation:

$$A_c = -\frac{Q}{w} \ln(1-\eta) = -\frac{41.67}{0.10} \times \ln(1 - 0.995)$$

$$A_c = -416.7 \times \ln(0.005) = -416.7 \times (-5.298)$$

$$\boxed{A_c = 2207 \text{ m}^2}$$

For a **two-field ESP** with **3 passages per field** and plate height 12 m, total length per pass = 2207 / (2 × 12) ≈ **92 m per pass**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B: (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**(a) Discuss the sources, environmental impact, and utilization options for phosphogypsum. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Explain the working of a Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) system for NOx control in an ammonia plant reformer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Source:** Phosphogypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) is a by-product of **wet-process phosphoric acid** manufacturing; ~4.5 t per t of P₂O₅.
- **Reaction:**

$$\text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6\text{F}_2 + 10\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 20\text{H}_2\text{O} \to 6\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 10\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{HF}$$

- **Environmental impact:** 
  - Land occupation (gyp pond needs **40–50 acres/yr** per 1000 tpd P₂O₅ plant)
  - **Radiological hazard** (radon emission from U/Th in rock)
  - Leachate contaminating groundwater (F⁻, SO₄²⁻, P₂O₅)
- **Utilization options:**
  1. **Cement retarder** (most common — sets the rate of cement hardening)
  2. **Plaster of Paris / Gypsum boards**
  3. **Agriculture** (Ca/S source for soil conditioning)
  4. **Roads & embankments**
  5. **Ammonium sulphate fertilizer** (gypsum + (NH₄)₂CO₃) *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**
- **Source of NOx in reformer:** Thermal NOx from primary reformer burner (flame temp > 1500 °C).
- **SCR Principle:** NH₃ is injected upstream of a catalyst bed; NOx + NH₃ react on the catalyst surface at **300–400 °C** to form N₂ and H₂O.
- **Key reaction:**

$$4\text{NH}_3 + 4\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \to 4\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- **Catalyst:** V₂O₅/TiO₂ (honeycomb or plate type).
- **NH₃ slip** controlled < 5 ppm to prevent ammonium salt deposits.
- **SO₂ interference:** Forms ammonium bisulphate that fouls catalyst — managed by temperature control.
- **Removal efficiency:** 80–95%; can be **>95%** with two-stage SCR.
- **Location:** Downstream of **waste heat boiler** and **process gas cooler** to use available heat.
- **Capital cost:** High; opex dominated by ammonia cost. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes in ESP design** — converting Nm³/h to m³/s is often skipped. Always show it.
> 2. **Confusing DCDA (Double Contact Double Absorption) with SCSA** — both are sulphuric acid plant types; examiners test if you know **DCDA achieves 99.7% vs SCSA's 96–98%**.
> 3. **Writing "phosphogypsum is used in cement" without stoichiometry** — examiners want the reaction and the rate (3–5% retarder dosage).
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the standard** — e.g., writing "low NOx burners" without saying "**staged combustion**" loses a mark.
> 5. **In ETP numericals, missing the secondary clarification step** — students often go directly from aeration tank to "treated water" skipping the secondary clarifier.
> 6. **Writing "Zero Liquid Discharge" without mentioning MEE/crystallizer** — ZLD is not just RO; it requires **thermal evaporation**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Keep This Handy for Last-Minute Reading**

- **Three major pollution streams in fertilizer industry:** Air (NH₃, NOx, SO₂, HF, PM, urea dust) | Water (effluents with F⁻, P, NH₃-N) | Solid (phosphogypsum, slag, spent catalyst).
- **CPCB Red Category** — stringent consent requirements under Water Act 1974 & Air Act 1981.
- **Deutsch Equation** = foundation of ESP design. Remember the design form: $A_c = -\frac{Q}{w} \ln(1-\eta)$.
- **Cyclone → Scrubber → ESP → Bag Filter** is the typical hierarchy in decreasing/increasing efficiency for PM control.
- **DCDA process** for H₂SO₄ gives inherently low SO₂ emission — the cleanest route.
- **SCR uses NH₃ + V₂O₅/TiO₂ at 300–400 °C**; **SNCR** is non-catalytic at higher T.
- **Phosphogypsum** = 4.5 t per t of P₂O₅; major use in **cement retarder** (3–5% dosage).
- **FGD with limestone** gives **gypsum as a by-product** — closed-loop economics.
- **Effluent pH discharge standard: 6.5–8.5; F⁻ < 2 mg/L; NH₃-N < 50 mg/L.**
- **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** = ETP → MEE → Crystallizer → Salt recovery.
- **Pollution load (kg/day) = Flow (m³/d) × Conc (mg/L) × 10⁻³** — most-favoured KTU numerical format.
- **Noise standard for industrial area:** **75 dB(A) day / 70 dB(A) night.**
- **HRT for aeration tank in ASP:** typically **4–6 hours** for fertilizer industry effluent.
- **Stripping factor for NH₃ removal** is **pH-dependent** — maintain pH > 11 for efficient stripping.
- **Mass balance fundamentals** are critical for KTU 14-mark questions: always show **in = out + accumulation**.
- **Environmental Management System (ISO 14001:2015)** uses the **PDCA cycle** — Plan, Do, Check, Act.
- **CEMS** = Continuous Emission Monitoring System; mandatory for large fertilizer units.
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** Notification 2006 requires clearance for new fertilizer plants > **5000 tpy P₂O₅** equivalent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollution Prevention Strategies in Fertiliser Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pollution Prevention Strategies in Fertiliser Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pollution Prevention (P2)** in the fertiliser industry refers to the systematic, multi-tiered application of source-reduction techniques, in-process modifications, closed-loop recycling, and end-of-pipe treatment technologies designed to minimise the generation and release of gaseous, liquid, and solid pollutants arising from the manufacture, storage, handling, and transportation of nitrogenous, phosphatic, potassic, and complex fertilisers.

In strict alignment with the **U.S. EPA Pollution Prevention Act of 1990** and **UNEP Cleaner Production protocols** (both adopted in KTU 2024 scheme references), pollution prevention is hierarchically preferred over pollution control because it addresses the pollutant **at source** rather than treating it after formation.

The hierarchy, in descending order of preference, is:
1. **Elimination** (substitute the raw material or process entirely)
2. **Reduction** (use less of the pollutant-generating input)
3. **Reuse** (return the waste stream to the same process)
4. **Recycle** (route the waste to a different process)
5. **Treatment** (end-of-pipe remediation — least preferred)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Leaking Kitchen Tap" 💧**
>
> Imagine you have a kitchen tap that drips constantly, flooding the floor. You have **two options**:
> - **Option A (Pollution Control):** Place bigger and bigger buckets and mops on the floor to absorb the water. The tap still leaks; you are just managing the mess.
> - **Option B (Pollution Prevention):** Call a plumber and **fix the washer inside the tap**. The water never reaches the floor in the first place.
>
> Pollution prevention in a fertiliser plant is exactly the plumber's approach — we redesign the reactor, recover the unreacted ammonia, or scrub the off-gas **inside the process loop** instead of building massive effluent treatment plants downstream.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Standards

- **E-Factor (Environmental Factor):** Mass of waste produced per mass of desired product. Benchmark standard: **E < 1 for bulk fertilisers** is ideal; older urea plants operated at **E ≈ 2–5**.
- **Atom Economy:** Ratio of molecular weight of desired product to total molecular weight of all reactants. A higher atom economy means less by-product waste.
- **BAT / BREF Documents:** **Best Available Techniques** Reference documents issued by the **EU IPPC Bureau**, mandatory benchmarks for modern fertiliser plants under KTU 2024 case-study modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A 14-mark KTU ESE question on this module typically asks the student to *compare* two specific pollution prevention techniques (e.g., wet scrubbing vs. absorption recovery) for a named fertiliser stream, OR to *design* a closed-loop recycling strategy for a single unit operation such as a prilling tower or a phosphoric acid digester.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Pillar Strategic Framework

Pollution prevention in a fertiliser plant is structured around **five strategic pillars**, each addressing a different lifecycle stage of the pollutant.

### Pillar 1 — Raw Material Substitution
- Replace polluting feedstocks with cleaner alternatives.
- **Example:** In Single Super Phosphate (SSP) manufacture, replacing conventional **sulphuric acid** with **oleum or partially spent acid** drastically reduces fluoride volatilisation.
- **Example:** Switching from **soda ash-based** CO₂ scrubbing to **amine-based** scrubbing in ammonia plants increases CO₂ recovery and reduces Na₂CO₃ discharge.

### Pillar 2 — Process Modification & Intensification
- Redesign the reactor, separator, or heat exchanger to inherently generate less waste.
- **Example:** Replacing **once-through cooling water** with **closed-loop recirculated cooling towers** eliminates thermal pollution in receiving water bodies.
- **Example:** Using **Snamprogetti or Stamicarbon CO₂ stripping** in urea plants instead of traditional **total-recycle with carbamate condensation** reduces both NH₃ effluent and steam consumption.

### Pillar 3 — In-Process Recycling & Reuse
- Recover valuable intermediates and feed them back upstream.
- **Example:** **Carbamate recycle loop** in urea synthesis — unreacted NH₃ and CO₂ are condensed and returned to the reactor, achieving **>97% conversion** in modern plants.
- **Example:** **Phosphogypsum** (a by-product of phosphoric acid) is recycled into **gypsum plaster, cement retarder, or agricultural sulphur** instead of being stacked in ponds.

### Pillar 4 — Technology Substitution
- Adopt fundamentally cleaner production routes.
- **Example:** **Melt granulation** of urea (e.g., Hydro Agri process) eliminates the prilling tower, slashing NH₃ and urea-dust emissions.
- **Example:** **Haldor Topsøe S-200 / S-300** ammonia converter designs reduce natural-gas consumption per ton of NH₃ from **~38 GJ** (old) to **~28 GJ** (modern), indirectly cutting CO₂ emissions by ~25%.

### Pillar 5 — Good Housekeeping & Management
- Operator training, leak detection and repair (LDAR) programs, preventive maintenance, and material accounting.
- **Example:** **Valve-pack tightening schedules** on ammonia spheres, **fugitive-emission monitoring** with TVA-1000 FID detectors, and **spill containment bunding** around bulk-fertiliser storage.

## 2.2 The Four Pollution Streams and Their P2 Strategies

A fertiliser plant generates **four classes of waste**, each requiring a tailored P2 strategy.

| Stream Class | Typical Pollutants | Primary P2 Strategy | Secondary Treatment |
|--------------|--------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Gaseous** | NH₃, NOₓ, SO₂, HF, SiF₄, CO₂, urea dust | Recovery & recycle (acid scrubbing, carbamate recycle) | Wet scrubbers, SCR/SNCR for NOₓ |
| **Liquid (Effluent)** | High BOD/COD, ammoniacal N, fluoride, phosphate, heavy metals | Source segregation, condensate reuse | Biological nitrification-denitrification |
| **Solid (Sludge)** | Phosphogypsum, catalyst spent, slag | By-product utilisation (cement, plaster) | Secured landfill |
| **Fugitive** | Valve-pack leaks, flange emissions, storage tank breathing | LDAR program, double-seal valves, vapour recovery | Dilution only — last resort |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Adapted for Environmental Engineering)

> [!NOTE]
> Although Module 4 is descriptive, certain quantitative metrics are **frequently tested** as 3-mark sub-parts in KTU ESE papers. Memorise these.

| Metric | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Fertiliser Industry Value | Units |
|--------|---------|----------------|-----------------------------------|-------|
| **E-Factor** | $E = \dfrac{m_{waste}}{m_{product}}$ | $m_{waste}$ = total waste mass; $m_{product}$ = product mass | Urea: 0.1 – 0.3; DAP: 0.5 – 1.5 | kg/kg |
| **Atom Economy** | $AE = \dfrac{MW_{product}}{\sum MW_{reactants}} \times 100$ | $MW$ = molecular weight | Haber process NH₃: 100% (no by-product); Urea: 75% | % |
| **Process Mass Intensity** | $PMI = \dfrac{\sum m_{inputs}}{m_{product}}$ | $m_{inputs}$ = total input mass | Modern ammonia: ~3.0; Older plants: ~4.5 | kg/kg |
| **CO₂ Emission Factor (Haber-Bosch)** | $CO_{2,t} = \dfrac{F_{gas} \times EF_{gas}}{m_{NH_3}}$ | $F_{gas}$ = fuel feed; $EF_{gas}$ = emission factor | Best plants: **1.5 – 1.8** | t CO₂ / t NH₃ |
| **Scrubber Efficiency** | $\eta_{scrub} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | $C_{in}, C_{out}$ = inlet/outlet concentrations | Acid scrubber for NH₃: 95 – 99.5 | % |
| **Conversion per Pass (Urea)** | $X_{pp} = \dfrac{n_{NH_3,conv}}{n_{NH_3,feed}} \times 100$ | Moles converted / moles fed | Once-through: 50–60; Total recycle: 99+ | % |
| **Material Utilisation Efficiency** | $MUE = \dfrac{m_{useful\,output}}{m_{total\,input}} \times 100$ | Useful output / total input | Target: > 95% for bulk fertilisers | % |

> **Critical Reminder:** All LaTeX vertical bars in the formulas above use `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table syntax intact.

## 2.4 Why This Strategy Matters in the Real Engineering World

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every additional kg of NH₃ recovered = revenue saved + avoided environmental penalty under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** (Indian statutory benchmarks referenced in KTU 2024 syllabus). Pollution prevention is not merely environmental compliance — it is **economic optimisation**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations and Worked Frameworks

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating the E-Factor of a Urea Plant

> [!NOTE]
> A textbook KTU-style 7-mark problem. The examiner expects you to define E-Factor, list all waste streams from a **once-through CO₂ stripping urea plant**, and compute the metric.

### Given Data (Hypothetical Plant, 1 metric ton of urea basis)
- Urea product output ($m_{product}$) = **1000 kg**
- Unconverted NH₃ in process condensate = **12 kg**
- Unconverted CO₂ vented = **8 kg** (inert loss)
- Biuret formed (downgraded product) = **3 kg**
- Cooling-tower blowdown with residual urea = **5 kg**
- Spent caustic from CO₂ removal = **2 kg**
- Floor wash & spillages (recoverable) = **2 kg**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We apply the universal E-Factor definition:

$$
E = \frac{m_{waste}}{m_{product}}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify and sum all waste streams.**

$$
m_{waste} = m_{NH_3,eff} + m_{CO_2,vent} + m_{biuret} + m_{blowdown} + m_{caustic} + m_{spillage}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values (with units in kg).**

$$
m_{waste} = 12 + 8 + 3 + 5 + 2 + 2
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the arithmetic.**

$$
m_{waste} = 32 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the E-Factor formula.**

$$
E = \frac{32 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation and unit analysis.**

$$
E = 0.032 \text{ kg}_{waste} \cdot \text{kg}_{product}^{-1}
$$

### Interpretation (Valuation Key Pattern)

| Result Range | Verdict | What KTU Examiner Expects |
|--------------|---------|---------------------------|
| $E < 0.1$ | Excellent | State-of-the-art total-recycle plant |
| $0.1 \le E \le 0.5$ | Good | Typical modern CO₂ stripping plant |
| $E > 0.5$ | Poor | Once-through design; needs P2 intervention |

Since $E = 0.032$, the hypothetical plant is **excellent**, indicating effective pollution prevention. A *follow-up* 3-mark sub-part typically asks: "Suggest **two** further P2 measures to push $E$ below 0.02" — model answer: **install a vacuum carbamate condenser** and **recover biuret via fractional crystallisation**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Scrubber Efficiency Calculation for NH₃ Recovery

### Given Data
- Inlet NH₃ concentration in off-gas, $C_{in}$ = **2000 mg/Nm³**
- Outlet NH₃ concentration after dilute H₂SO₄ scrubber, $C_{out}$ = **20 mg/Nm³**
- Off-gas flow rate, $Q$ = **5000 Nm³/h**
- Operating hours per year, $t$ = **8000 h**
- Density of recovered (NH₄)₂SO₄ solution produced, $\rho$ ≈ **1200 kg/m³** for mass balance; Molar mass NH₃ = 17 g/mol, (NH₄)₂SO₄ = 132 g/mol.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the scrubber efficiency formula.**

$$
\eta_{scrub} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the concentration values.**

$$
\eta_{scrub} = \frac{2000 - 20}{2000} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
\eta_{scrub} = \frac{1980}{2000} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Final numerical answer.**

$$
\eta_{scrub} = 99.0 \text{ %}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert removed NH₃ to (NH₄)₂SO₄ mass per year (frequently asked follow-up).**

The mass of NH₃ removed per hour:

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = (C_{in} - C_{out}) \times Q = 1980 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 \times 5000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = 9.90 \times 10^6 \text{ mg/h} = 9.90 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Annual NH₃ removed:

$$
m_{NH_3,annual} = 9.90 \text{ kg/h} \times 8000 \text{ h/yr} = 79{,}200 \text{ kg/yr}
$$

Mass of (NH₄)₂SO₄ produced (stoichiometric ratio 2 NH₃ : 1 (NH₄)₂SO₄):

$$
m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} = m_{NH_3} \times \frac{MW_{(NH_4)_2SO_4}}{2 \times MW_{NH_3}}
$$

$$
m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} = 79{,}200 \times \frac{132}{2 \times 17}
$$

$$
m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} = 79{,}200 \times 3.882
$$

$$
m_{(NH_4)_2SO_4} \approx 307{,}500 \text{ kg/yr} \approx 307.5 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

### Interpretation
- A **99% efficient scrubber** is a high-performance unit; the **BAT-BREF benchmark** for NH₃ scrubbing in fertiliser complexes is **≥ 99%**.
- The recovered ammonium sulphate is **saleable**, turning a waste liability into ~₹ 1.5–2 crore annual revenue (assuming 2024 commodity prices).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always carry the **units through** every step. Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme penalise missing units heavily (typically 1 mark per incorrect/omitted unit in 14-mark problems).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Comparative Pollution Prevention Ranking Matrix

> This is a "design-type" KTU question: *"Compare and rank **three** P2 strategies for a DAP plant."*

| Criterion | Weightage | Strategy 1: Source Reduction (lower acid strength) | Strategy 2: Closed-Loop Recycle (gypsum pond water reuse) | Strategy 3: End-of-Pipe Treatment (lime precipitation of P) |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| **E-Factor reduction** | 30% | High (0.6) | Medium (0.4) | Low (0.2) |
| **Capital cost** | 20% | Medium | Low | High |
| **Operating cost** | 20% | Low | Low | High |
| **Energy impact** | 15% | Positive (less pumping) | Neutral | Negative |
| **Regulatory acceptance** | 15% | High | Medium | High |
| **Weighted Score** | **100%** | **0.75 × 100 = 75** | **0.45 × 100 = 45** | **0.30 × 100 = 30** |

> **Conclusion of the matrix:** Strategy 1 (Source Reduction) is the **preferred** pollution prevention technique, fully consistent with the **P2 hierarchy of preference** taught in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 The Pollution Prevention Hierarchy Pyramid

This is the foundational visual every KTU answer on this module should ideally include. The pyramid illustrates the descending priority of P2 strategies.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef top fill:#1a7f37,stroke:#0a4d22,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-size:14px
    classDef second fill:#2ea043,stroke:#1a7f37,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-size:13px
    classDef third fill:#9be9a8,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a4d22,font-size:13px
    classDef fourth fill:#ddf4ff,stroke:#0969da,stroke-width:2px,color:#0969da,font-size:12px
    classDef fifth fill:#fff8c5,stroke:#9a6700,stroke-width:2px,color:#9a6700,font-size:12px

    P5["ELIMINATION: New process route, no pollutant generated"]:::top
    P4["REDUCTION: Minimise feedstock / energy input"]:::second
    P3["REUSE: Return waste to same process"]:::third
    P2["RECYCLE: Route waste to another process"]:::fourth
    P1["TREATMENT: End-of-pipe remediation, lowest priority"]:::fifth

    P5 --> P4
    P4 --> P3
    P3 --> P2
    P2 --> P1
```

**How to read this diagram in an exam answer:**
- The topmost layer (Elimination) is the **most preferred** strategy because no waste is generated.
- Each descending layer represents a **less preferred** strategy because the pollutant has already been created and must be handled.
- In a 7-mark question, the examiner awards **at least 2 marks** for sketching this hierarchy with a single sentence on the principle.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pollution Prevention System in a Urea Plant

The following flow schematic maps the **functional interactions** between each P2 strategy in a real-world fertiliser complex. This addresses the KTU syllabus requirement for "design of pollution prevention systems."

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef input fill:#ddf4ff,stroke:#0969da,color:#0a4d22
    classDef process fill:#fff8c5,stroke:#9a6700,color:#5a3a00
    classDef p2 fill:#9be9a8,stroke:#1a7f37,color:#0a4d22
    classDef output fill:#ffd7d5,stroke:#b35900,color:#5a0000

    A1["Raw NH3 storage"]:::input
    A2["Raw CO2 storage"]:::input

    B1["NH3 CO2 Compressor"]:::process
    B2["Urea Reactor, 200 atm, 185 C"]:::process
    B3["Stripper and Carbamate Condenser"]:::process
    B4["Prilling Tower"]:::process

    C1["Carbamate Recycle Loop: P2 Pillar 3"]:::p2
    C2["NH3 Scrubber with H2SO4: P2 Pillar 2"]:::p2
    C3["Waste-Heat Recovery Boiler: P2 Pillar 4"]:::p2
    C4["Biuret Control via Residence Time: P2 Pillar 1"]:::p2
    C5["LDAR Fugitive Monitoring: P2 Pillar 5"]:::p2

    D1["Solid Urea Product, E factor less than 0.1"]:::output
    D2["Ammonium Sulphate By Product, saleable"]:::output
    D3["Clean Water Vapour to atmosphere"]:::output

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> B2
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C4
    B4 --> C2
    C2 --> D2
    B3 --> C3
    C3 --> D3
    B4 --> D1
    C5 -.monitors.-> B1
    C5 -.monitors.-> A1
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- **Blue nodes** = raw material inputs.
- **Yellow nodes** = core process equipment.
- **Green nodes** = pollution prevention units (the 5 pillars).
- **Red nodes** = outputs (only one is a waste, the others are saleable products or clean emissions).
- The **dashed arrows** from C5 represent *monitoring* rather than material flow — this emphasises the role of **good housekeeping** in P2.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting the Right P2 Strategy

This decision tree helps students tackle a typical KTU 14-mark question: *"Given a specific waste stream, recommend a P2 strategy."*

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef ask fill:#fff8c5,stroke:#9a6700,color:#5a3a00
    classDef yes fill:#9be9a8,stroke:#1a7f37,color:#0a4d22
    classDef no fill:#ffd7d5,stroke:#b35900,color:#5a0000
    classDef action fill:#ddf4ff,stroke:#0969da,color:#0a4d22

    Q1{Is the pollutant a recoverable raw material?}:::ask
    Q2{Can the process be redesigned to eliminate it?}:::ask
    Q3{Is the volume high enough to justify a recovery unit?}:::ask
    Q4{Is the pollutant regulated under EPA emission norms?}:::ask

    A1["RECYCLE: route back to upstream process"]:::yes
    A2["ELIMINATE: redesign reactor or substitute feedstock"]:::yes
    A3["REUSE: return to same process after minimal treatment"]:::yes
    A4["TREAT: install scrubber, biotreat, or secure landfill"]:::yes
    A5["Dilute and disperse via tall stack with monitoring"]:::action

    Q1 -- Yes --> A1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4
    Q4 -- No --> A5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE questions, the decision-tree approach scores high because it demonstrates *systematic engineering judgement* rather than rote listing. A 14-mark answer with this tree (and a 2-line explanation for each branch) easily secures **12–14 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

> **Define pollution prevention. How does it differ fundamentally from pollution control?**

**Model Answer:**

Pollution prevention (P2) is the in-process elimination or minimisation of waste generation at its source, achieved through raw material substitution, process modification, and in-plant recycling. Pollution control, in contrast, is the treatment of a waste stream **after** it has been generated, typically by end-of-pipe devices such as scrubbers, biotreaters, or electrostatic precipitators.

The fundamental difference is **temporal and systemic**: P2 intervenes *before* pollutant formation, whereas control intervenes *after*. P2 is preferred because it reduces both environmental burden and long-term operating cost, whereas control merely transfers the pollutant from one medium (e.g., air) to another (e.g., sludge).

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

> **List any three cleaner-production strategies applicable to a nitrogenous fertiliser (urea) plant.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Carbamate total-recycle** — condensing and returning unreacted NH₃ and CO₂ to the reactor, achieving >99% conversion.
2. **Melt granulation** — replacing the prilling tower to eliminate urea-dust and NH₃ emissions.
3. **H₂SO₄ scrubbing of vent gases** — recovering fugitive NH₃ as saleable ammonium sulphate.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** KTU 2024 scheme mandates that students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five-tier pollution prevention hierarchy** as applied to a phosphoric acid manufacturing plant. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A DAP plant generates the following waste streams per 1000 kg of DAP product. Calculate the **E-Factor** and suggest **two** process modifications to reduce it below 0.4. **(7 marks)**
>
> | Stream | Mass (kg) |
> |---|---|
> | Phosphogypsum slurry | 4800 |
> | Spent HF scrubber liquor | 25 |
> | NH₃ slip from granulator | 18 |
> | Cooling-tower blowdown | 12 |
> | Spent lube oil | 1 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The five-tier P2 hierarchy applied to a phosphoric acid plant (based on the wet-process via sulphuric acid digestion of rock phosphate):

1. **Elimination (Top Tier):** Switch to a **dry-process** phosphoric acid route (e.g., the **Prayon process** with reduced rock phosphate fineness) to minimise fluoride volatilisation. Theoretically eliminates the HF and SiF₄ emission problem.

2. **Reduction:** Use **beneficiated rock phosphate** with lower silica content, reducing HF generation stoichiometrically:
   $$\text{SiO}_2 + 6\text{HF} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SiF}_6 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
   A 1% reduction in silica feed reduces HF by ~6% per unit throughput.

3. **Reuse:** Recycle the **clarified phosphogypsum pond water** (rich in P₂O₅ and HF) back to the attack tank as dilution water, reducing fresh water intake and effluent volume.

4. **Recycle:** Route the **fluoride-rich scrubber liquor** (as fluosilicic acid, H₂SiF₆) to a downstream **aluminium fluoride** or **cryolite** manufacturing unit, converting a waste stream into a saleable product.

5. **Treatment (Bottom Tier):** Install an **impingement-type wet scrubber** with NaOH or water scrubbing as a last-resort measure to capture residual HF and SiF₄ before atmospheric release.

A block diagram would show: **Raw Rock Phosphate → Beneficiation → Digestion → Clarification → Concentration → Product**, with five parallel side-streams corresponding to the five P2 tiers, terminating in a final wet scrubber before the atmosphere.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Sum all waste streams.**

$$
