# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Clean Development Mechanism (CDM). How is it relevant to the fertilizer industry?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Clean Development Mechanism (CDM)** is a market-based flexible mechanism defined under **Article 12 of the Kyoto Protocol (1997)** that allows Annex I (developed) countries to invest in emission reduction projects in Non-Annex I (developing) countries and earn **Certified Emission Reductions (CERs)** — where 1 CER represents 1 tonne of CO₂ equivalent avoided.

**Relevance to Fertilizer Industry:**

(a) Indian fertilizer plants are prime hosts for CDM projects because India is a Non-Annex I country with no binding emission targets under Kyoto but with significant emission reduction potential.

(b) **N₂O abatement in nitric acid plants** is the most successful CDM application — N₂O has a GWP of 265, so each kg abated yields 265 CERs.

(c) Indian nitric acid plants have registered hundreds of CDM projects with the **Clean Development Mechanism Executive Board (CDM EB)**, generating significant revenue through CER trading.

(d) Projects enable technology transfer (e.g., **secondary catalytic reduction**, **tertiary abatement**) that reduces NOₓ/N₂O while improving plant profitability.

**Keywords for KTU Exam:** *Article 12, Kyoto Protocol, CERs, Non-Annex I, N₂O abatement, technology transfer, sustainable development.*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Briefly explain the salient features of the Paris Agreement (2015) with reference to its temperature goals.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Paris Agreement** adopted on **12 December 2015** at COP-21 (Paris) is the first universal climate treaty with **195+ signatories**, applicable to all parties under the UNFCCC framework.

**Salient Features:**

(a) **Temperature Goal**: Limit global average temperature rise to **well below 2°C** above pre-industrial levels, with efforts to limit it to **1.5°C**.

(b) **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)**: Each country submits its own 5-year action plans for emission reduction — a bottom-up, pledge-and-review approach replacing Kyoto's top-down binding targets.

(c) **Global Stocktake**: Every 5 years, parties assess collective progress (first in 2023).

(d) **Climate Finance**: Developed countries committed to mobilize **$100 billion/year** by 2020 for developing nations — now being renegotiated toward a New Collective Quantified Goal (NCQG).

(e) **Net-Zero Alignment**: Parties to peak emissions as soon as possible and achieve net-zero CO₂ by mid-century.

(f) **Transparency Framework**: Biennial transparency reports (BTRs) replace earlier BURs for all parties.

**Keywords:** *1.5°C goal, NDCs, Stocktake, climate finance, net-zero, transparency, COP-21.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024 Scheme)**: Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Module 4 Choice]

**(a)** Explain the salient features and emission obligations under the **Kyoto Protocol (1997)**. Discuss the three flexible mechanisms in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coal-based ammonia plant produces **1,500 tonnes/day of NH₃**. The plant emits **5.2 tCO₂/tNH₃** (including both fossil CO₂ and process CO₂). Calculate the annual CO₂ emissions and the equivalent number of CERs if the plant retrofits to natural gas reforming with emission factor of **1.8 tCO₂/tNH₃**, assuming 330 operating days/year. Comment on the significance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Kyoto Protocol Salient Features and Flexible Mechanisms:**

The **Kyoto Protocol**, adopted on **11 December 1997** and entered into force on **16 February 2005**, operationalizes the UNFCCC through binding emission reduction commitments for Annex I (developed) countries.

**Salient Features:**

(a) **Binding Quantitative Targets**: Annex I countries committed to an **average 5.2% reduction** in GHG emissions below 1990 levels over the first commitment period (2008–2012). Second commitment period (2013–2020): 18% below 1990.

(b) **Six GHGs Covered**: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, HFCs, PFCs, SF₆.

(c) **Common but Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR)**: Developing countries (Non-Annex I) had no binding targets but could participate voluntarily.

(d) **Compliance Committee** established to ensure adherence.

**Three Flexible Mechanisms:**

| Mechanism | Article | Parties | Unit Generated | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CDM** | Article 12 | Annex I ↔ Non-Annex I | CERs | Indian HNO₃ plant N₂O abatement |
| **JI** | Article 6 | Annex I ↔ Annex I | ERUs | Russia-EU joint forest project |
| **IET** | Article 17 | Annex I ↔ Annex I | AAUs | EU ETS Phase 1 trading |

**Part (b) — CO₂ Calculation:**

**Step 1: Annual NH₃ Production**
$$P_{annual} = 1500 \times 330 = 495{,}000 \text{ tonnes NH}_3/\text{year}$$

**Step 2: Baseline CO₂ Emissions (Coal-based)**
$$E_{baseline} = 495{,}000 \times 5.2 = 2{,}574{,}000 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

**Step 3: Project CO₂ Emissions (Natural Gas-based)**
$$E_{project} = 495{,}000 \times 1.8 = 891{,}000 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

**Step 4: Annual Emission Reduction**
$$\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{project} = 2{,}574{,}000 - 891{,}000 = 1{,}683{,}000 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year}$$

**Step 5: CERs Generated (1 CER = 1 tCO₂e)**
$$CERs = 1{,}683{,}000 \text{ CERs/year}$$

**Step 6: Significance and Interpretation**

(a) The retrofitted plant achieves **65.4% reduction** in direct CO₂ emissions — a transformational climate outcome.

(b) At a CER price of **€10/CER**, annual revenue ≈ **€16.83 million** (≈₹152 crore at 1€ = ₹90), enough to fund capital expenditure within **3–5 years**.

(c) The retrofitted plant aligns with **Paris Agreement NDC targets** for India's fertilizer sector (1.5 tCO₂/tNH₃ by 2030 ambition).

(d) Co-benefits include improved **specific energy consumption**, lower **particulate emissions**, and reduced **water consumption**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often confuse **CER** with **CO₂e** — they are equal in magnitude (1 CER = 1 tCO₂e) but CER is a **tradable commodity** under CDM, while CO₂e is a **physical quantity**. Do not interchange them loosely in derivations. Also remember: **annual production × emission factor = total emission** — students frequently miss the operating days multiplier.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Module 4 Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** with emphasis on its objectives, control measures, and impact on the fertilizer industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **sulfuric acid plant** with capacity **500 tonnes/day** emits **SO₂ at 1,200 ppm** in stack gas at **150°C and 101.325 kPa**. Stack gas flow rate is **25,000 Nm³/hour**. Calculate: (i) SO₂ mass emission rate in **kg/h**, (ii) whether the plant complies with CPCB limit of **400 mg/Nm³**, and (iii) the minimum SO₂ reduction efficiency required to achieve compliance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Montreal Protocol:**

The **Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer** was adopted on **16 September 1987** and entered into force on **1 January 1989**. It is the first UN treaty to achieve **universal ratification (197 parties)** and is widely regarded as the **most successful environmental treaty ever**.

**Objectives:**

(a) Protect the stratospheric **ozone layer** from depletion by controlling production and consumption of **Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS)**.

(b) Promote research and information exchange on ozone-depleting chemicals.

(c) Facilitate transfer of alternative technologies to developing countries.

**Control Measures:**

(a) **Annex A**: CFCs (CFC-11, CFC-12, CFC-13, CFC-14, CFC-115), Halons, Carbon tetrachloride, Methyl bromide — phaseout schedule.

(b) **Annex B**: Other CFCs, methyl chloroform — phaseout.

(c) **Annex C**: HCFCs (transitional substances) — phasedown by 2020 (developed), 2030 (developing).

(d) **Multilateral Fund**: Established under Article 10 to provide financial/technical assistance to developing countries.

(e) **Article 5 Countries**: Developing nations (including India) granted 10-year grace period.

**Impact on Fertilizer Industry:**

(a) Fertilizer plants historically used **CFC-11 and CFC-12** as refrigerants in ammonia plant cold storage and process cooling. These have been replaced with **ammonia refrigerant** (already available on-site) and **HFC alternatives**.

(b) **HCFC-22** used in some refrigeration systems is being phased down — replaced by **HFC blends** or **natural refrigerants** (CO₂, NH₃, hydrocarbons).

(c) Indirect benefit: Reduced regulatory cost burden and improved corporate environmental image.

(d) Kigali Amendment (2016) now controls HFCs, requiring phasedown — next compliance challenge.

**Part (b) — SO₂ Emission Calculation:**

**Given Data:**
- Plant capacity: 500 t/day H₂SO₄
- Stack SO₂ concentration: 1,200 ppm
- Stack temperature: 150°C = 423.15 K
- Pressure: 101.325 kPa
- Stack flow: 25,000 Nm³/hour (already at normal conditions, so no further T,P correction needed since Nm³ is at 0°C, 101.325 kPa)
- CPCB limit: 400 mg/Nm³
- Molecular weight of SO₂: 32 + 32 = 64 kg/kmol

**(i) Mass Emission Rate Calculation:**

For a gas mixture at standard conditions, the conversion from ppm (volumetric) to mg/Nm³ uses the ideal gas relation. At normal conditions (0°C, 101.325 kPa), 1 kmol of ideal gas occupies **22.414 Nm³**.

Mass concentration:
$$C_{mass} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times M}{22.414} \times 10^3$$

Substituting:
$$C_{mass} = \dfrac{1200 \times 64}{22.414} \times 10^3 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

$$C_{mass} = \dfrac{76{,}800}{22.414} \times 10^3 = 3426.83 \times 10^3 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

Wait — let me verify with the fundamental formula at standard conditions. At 0°C, 101.325 kPa:
$$C_{mg/Nm^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times M \times 1000}{22.414}$$

$$C_{mg/Nm^3} = \dfrac{1200 \times 64}{22.414} = 3426.83 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

**Step: Mass Emission Rate**
$$Q_{SO_2} = C_{mass} \times Q_v$$

$$Q_{SO_2} = 3426.83 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/Nm}^3 \times 25{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

$$Q_{SO_2} = 3426.83 \times 25 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$Q_{SO_2} = 85{,}670.75 \text{ kg/h} \approx 85.67 \text{ t/h}$$

**Note on Temperature Correction**: Since the flow rate is given in **Nm³** (normal cubic meters at 0°C, 101.325 kPa), the concentration at the elevated stack temperature (150°C) must be normalized. The actual volumetric flow at 150°C would be:
$$Q_{actual} = 25{,}000 \times \dfrac{423.15}{273.15} = 38{,}732 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

But since we are computing mass emission rate, mass is conserved and the answer **85.67 t/h** is correct using the standard-state concentration.

**(ii) Compliance Check:**

Concentration: **3,426.83 mg/Nm³** ≫ **400 mg/Nm³** (CPCB limit)

**Plant is in NON-COMPLIANCE — exceeds limit by 8.57 times.**

**(iii) Required Reduction Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{required} = \dfrac{C_{actual} - C_{limit}}{C_{actual}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{required} = \dfrac{3426.83 - 400}{3426.83} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{required} = \dfrac{3026.83}{3426.83} \times 100\% = 88.32\%$$

**Plant must achieve 88.32% SO₂ reduction efficiency** to comply with CPCB norms. This typically requires installing a **Double Contact Double Absorption (DCDA)** converter or upgrading to a **Wet Gas Sulphuric Acid (WSA)** process.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: A common error is treating the given stack flow (25,000 Nm³/h) as actual m³/h without normalizing. Always confirm whether flow rate is in **Nm³** (normal) or **Am³** (actual). For mass emission calculations using the ideal gas law, Nm³ is preferred since 1 kmol = 22.414 Nm³ exactly. Also, students often forget the **× 10³ unit conversion** when switching from kg to mg, losing a full mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PESFT633 Module 4: Global Emission Legislation**

### Core Frameworks (Chronological Order):
- **Vienna Convention (1985)** → umbrella treaty for ozone protection
- **Montreal Protocol (1987)** → CFCs, Halons, HCFCs phaseout; Article 5 grace for developing countries
- **UNFCCC (1992)** → umbrella convention for climate change; CBDR principle
- **Kyoto Protocol (1997)** → binding targets for Annex I; 3 flexible mechanisms (CDM, JI, IET); 6 GHGs
- **Paris Agreement (2015)** → NDCs, 1.5°C goal, Global Stocktake every 5 years, Net-Zero by mid-century
- **Kigali Amendment (2016)** → HFC phasedown, refrigerant regulations

### Indian Legislation Stack:
- **Water Act (1974)** → CPCB/SPCB formation
- **Air Act (1981)** → Ambient air quality standards
- **EPA (1986)** → Umbrella environmental law
- **MSIHC Rules (1989)** → Hazardous chemical thresholds
- **Hazardous Waste Rules** → Solid waste management

### Critical Numbers (Memorize for Exam):
- $GWP_{CO_2} = 1$
- $GWP_{CH_4} = 28$
- $GWP_{N_2O} = 265$
- $ODP_{CFC-11} = 1.0$
- $ODP_{HCFC-22} = 0.055$
- Paris temperature goal: **1.5°C** (pursue) / **2°C** (limit)
- Kyoto reduction target: **5.2%** below 1990 levels (first period)

### Key Fertilizer-Specific Limits (CPCB):
- NOₓ in HNO₃ plant: **300 mg/Nm³**
- NH₃ in urea plant: **50 mg/Nm³**
- HF in DAP plant: **25 mg/Nm³**
- SO₂ in H₂SO₄ plant: **400 mg/Nm³**

### Essential Formulas to Memorize:
- $E_{CO_2e} = \sum m_i \times GWP_i$
- $CERs = (E_{baseline} - E_{project}) \times GWP$
- $C_{mg/Nm^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times M}{22.414}$
- $Q_{mass} = C_{mass} \times Q_v$
- $\eta_{reduction} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$

### Important Terms (Acronyms):
- **CDM**: Clean Development Mechanism
- **CER**: Certified Emission Reduction
- **NDC**: Nationally Determined Contribution
- **ODS**: Ozone Depleting Substance
- **CBDR**: Common But Differentiated Responsibilities
- **COP**: Conference of Parties
- **PAT**: Perform Achieve Trade (India)
- **CEMS**: Continuous Emission Monitoring System
- **DCDA**: Double Contact Double Absorption
- **FGD**: Flue Gas Desulfurization
- **SCR/SNCR**: Selective Catalytic Reduction / Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction

### Exam Strategy Tips for KTU 2024:
1. Always state the **specific article number** when mentioning Kyoto/CDM (e.g., Article 12 for CDM, Article 6 for JI, Article 17 for IET)
2. For numerical problems, always show **all six steps**: Given → Formula → Substitution → Calculation → Unit check → Engineering interpretation
3. Remember **22.414 Nm³/kmol** is the magic number for gas law conversions at standard conditions
4. For Indian compliance, **CPCB** is the apex body; **MoEFCC** is the ministry
5. In long answers, link global protocols to **specific Indian fertilizer plant examples** to demonstrate application-level understanding — this earns full marks in KTU ESE valuation
6. Always include **units** in final answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units in numerical problems
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Climate Change: GHG Emissions and Reducing the CO2 Footprint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT633 - HEALTH SAFETY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASPECTS OF FERTILIZER INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Climate Change: GHG Emissions and Reducing the CO2 Footprint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Topic

**Climate Change** in the context of the fertilizer industry refers to the long-term alteration of global and regional climate patterns caused primarily by the anthropogenic emission of **Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)**. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) defines climate change as "a change in the state of the climate that can be identified by changes in the mean and/or the variability of its properties and that persists for an extended period, typically decades or longer."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)** are gaseous constituents of the atmosphere, both natural and anthropogenic, that absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, thereby trapping heat in the lower atmosphere. The seven GHGs governed under the Kyoto Protocol and tracked by the IPCC are:
> 1. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — GWP = 1
> 2. **Methane (CH₄)** — GWP = **28** (AR5, 100-year)
> 3. **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)** — GWP = **265** (AR5, 100-year)
> 4. **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** — GWP range: **124 to 14,800**
> 5. **Perfluorocarbons (PFCs)** — GWP range: **7,390 to 12,200**
> 6. **Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆)** — GWP = **23,500**
> 7. **Nitrogen Trifluoride (NF₃)** — GWP = **16,100**

The **CO₂ Footprint** (or Carbon Footprint) of a fertilizer plant is formally defined as the **total mass of CO₂-equivalent (CO₂e) emissions** released directly and indirectly over the lifecycle of a defined quantity of fertilizer product, expressed in **kilograms (or tonnes) of CO₂ equivalent per tonne of product (kg CO₂e / t product)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** is a dimensionless factor that converts the impact of any given GHG to an equivalent mass of CO₂ over a fixed time horizon (usually 100 years), enabling a unified "CO₂-equivalent" metric for comparison and inventory accounting.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Earth wrapped in a **thick woollen blanket** that is slowly getting thicker every year. Sunlight enters freely and warms the ground, but the heat that tries to escape back into space gets trapped by this blanket. That blanket is made of GHGs. In a fertilizer plant, every process — from cracking natural gas to make hydrogen, to oxidising ammonia to make nitric acid, to manufacturing urea — adds another "thread" to this planetary blanket.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Greenhouse Analogy**: A glass greenhouse lets sunlight in but traps the warm air inside because glass absorbs the outgoing infrared (heat) radiation. Our atmosphere acts similarly: CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O molecules are the "glass panels," letting short-wave solar radiation in but absorbing the long-wave infrared radiation that the Earth re-emits. The thicker the GHG layer, the warmer the planet becomes.

**Why this matters for fertilizer plants specifically:**
- The fertilizer industry is one of the **largest industrial emitters of CO₂** globally (≈ **1.2 % of total global GHG emissions**, source: IFA & IPCC).
- A single **greenfield ammonia-urea complex** (e.g., 2,000 TPD ammonia) emits roughly **2.5 to 3.5 million tonnes of CO₂ per year** — equivalent to adding ~600,000 passenger cars to the road.
- **N₂O from nitric acid plants** is the single largest contributor to GWP per tonne of product, despite low mass emission, because of its extremely high GWP (265).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific GWP of CO₂** | GWP(CO₂) | **1** (reference gas) |
| **Specific GWP of CH₄** | GWP(CH₄) | **28** (AR5, 100-yr) |
| **Specific GWP of N₂O** | GWP(N₂O) | **265** (AR5, 100-yr) |
| **Molecular weight of CO₂** | M(CO₂) | **44.01 g/mol** |
| **Molecular weight of CH₄** | M(CH₄) | **16.04 g/mol** |
| **Molecular weight of N₂O** | M(N₂O) | **44.01 g/mol** |
| **Stoichiometric CO₂ from NH₃** | σ(NH₃) | **~1.27 t CO₂ / t NH₃** (theoretical minimum) |
| **Industry average for NH₃** | Avg | **1.6 to 2.2 t CO₂e / t NH₃** (current BAT) |
| **N₂O from HNO₃ (unabated)** | η(HNO₃) | **6 to 12 kg N₂O / t HNO₃** |
| **N₂O from HNO₃ (abated)** | η(HNO₃,abated) | **0.5 to 2.5 kg N₂O / t HNO₃** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Scopes of GHG Accounting in a Fertilizer Plant

The **GHG Protocol Corporate Standard** classifies emissions into three "scopes," which are universally accepted for fertilizer industry inventories:

- **Scope 1 — Direct Emissions**: Emissions from sources owned or controlled by the company.
  - Primary reformer stack (CO₂, CH₄ slip)
  - Auxiliary boiler flue gas
  - Process vents (CO₂, NH₃ slip)
  - Nitric acid plant absorber tail gas (N₂O, NOₓ)
  - Fugitive emissions from flanges, valves, compressors
- **Scope 2 — Indirect Emissions from Imported Energy**: Emissions from the generation of purchased electricity, steam, or heat consumed by the plant.
  - Grid electricity used for compressors, pumps
  - Imported steam
- **Scope 3 — Other Indirect Emissions**: Emissions that occur in the value chain (upstream raw material extraction, downstream product use).
  - Natural gas extraction & transport
  - Phosphate rock mining (for P-fertilizers)
  - End-use field emissions (e.g., N₂O from applied nitrogenous fertilizers)

## 2.2 Sources of GHG Emissions Specific to Fertilizer Manufacture

### A. Ammonia Plant (largest single CO₂ source in fertilizer industry)

The dominant CO₂ source is the **steam-methane reforming (SMR) process** used to produce syngas (H₂ + CO):

**Primary Reformer (Endothermic):**
$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{Ni cat.}} \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Secondary Reformer (Partial Oxidation):**
$$\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2$$

**Water-Gas Shift (CO shift to maximise H₂):**
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Reaction (theoretical):** CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂
> This means **every 16 kg of CH₄ consumed produces 44 kg of CO₂** purely from the process chemistry (stoichiometric minimum). Additional CO₂ arises from fuel combustion in the reformer furnace (≈ 0.4 to 0.6 t CO₂/t NH₃).

**Ammonia Synthesis Loop:**
$$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3 \quad \Delta H = -92.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### B. Nitric Acid Plant (largest source of N₂O)

The **Ostwald process** oxidises ammonia over Pt-Rh gauze catalysts:

$$4\text{NH}_3 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{NO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(burner)}$$

$$2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2 \quad \text{(oxidation)}$$

$$3\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NO} \quad \text{(absorption)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **side reaction** at the burner produces **N₂O** as an unintentional byproduct:
> 4NH₃ + 4O₂ → 2N₂O + 6H₂O
> This N₂O has **GWP = 265**, making nitric acid plants the **single highest-GWP per tonne** segment of the fertilizer industry, despite the low mass emission (kg-scale).

### C. Urea Plant

Urea is synthesised by reacting ammonia with the **CO₂ recovered from the ammonia plant's CO₂ removal section**:

$$2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The CO₂ sequestered into urea is **biogenic/anthropogenic captured carbon**. However, CO₂ emissions still occur from the **granulation section, prilling tower vents, and CO₂ stripping overheads**. Typical CO₂ emissions: **0.05 to 0.15 t CO₂/t urea** (excluding the biogenic-equivalent sequestration credit).

### D. Other Fertilizer Segments

- **Phosphoric acid**: Lower direct CO₂; main emissions are from sulphuric acid production and phosphate rock handling.
- **Ammonium nitrate**: Direct CO₂ negligible; main impact is N₂O during prilling/handling and field application.
- **Complex fertilizers (NPK)**: CO₂ from material handling and drying (fuel combustion).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CO}_2\text{e} = \sum_{i} (m_i \times \text{GWP}_i)$ | Total CO₂-equivalent emission from mixed GHG stream | tonnes CO₂e |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\text{NH}_3} = \dfrac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{2 \cdot M_{\text{NH}_3}} = \dfrac{44.01}{2 \times 17.03} = 1.29$ | Stoichiometric minimum CO₂ per tonne NH₃ from SMR process | t CO₂ / t NH₃ |
| 3 | $m_{\text{N}_2\text{O,e}} = m_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} \times 265$ | Convert N₂O mass to CO₂-equivalent | t CO₂e |
| 4 | $m_{\text{CH}_4\text{,e}} = m_{\text{CH}_4} \times 28$ | Convert CH₄ mass to CO₂-equivalent | t CO₂e |
| 5 | $C_{\text{footprint}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total,CO}_2\text{e}}}{P_{\text{product}}}$ | Specific carbon footprint of a product | kg CO₂e / t product |
| 6 | $E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{SMR}} = n_{\text{CH}_4} \times M_{\text{CO}_2}$ | CO₂ from stoichiometric reforming (per mol CH₄) | g CO₂ |
| 7 | $E_{\text{CO}_2,\text{fuel}} = F \times \text{NCV} \times \text{EF} \times \dfrac{44}{12}$ | CO₂ from fuel combustion (F = fuel mass, NCV = net calorific value, EF = emission factor) | t CO₂ |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = \dfrac{\text{N}_2\text{O mass emitted}}{\text{HNO}_3 \text{ produced}}$ | Specific N₂O emission intensity | kg N₂O / t HNO₃ |
| 9 | $C_{\text{credit,urea}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{M_{\text{urea}}} = 0.733$ | Theoretical CO₂ sequestered in urea (credit) | t CO₂ / t urea |
| 10 | $\text{Capture efficiency, \%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{E_{\text{stack,CO}_2}}{E_{\text{generation,CO}_2}}\right) \times 100$ | CO₂ capture rate of CCS system | % |

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reducing the CO₂ footprint is no longer an environmental "good to do" — it is an **economic and regulatory imperative**. The **EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)**, **India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) 2023**, and the **Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme** all assign a direct monetary cost (₹/tCO₂) to industrial emissions. For an Indian fertilizer unit exporting to the EU, the carbon cost can swing project IRR by **5 to 12 percentage points**.

**Real-world utility of CO₂ footprinting:**
- **Investment decisions** for greenfield / brownfield expansions.
- **Benchmarking** against Best Available Techniques (BAT) reference documents (BREFs).
- **Compliance reporting** to UNFCCC, CDP, and stock exchange ESG disclosures.
- **Technology selection** between grey, blue, and green ammonia pathways.
- **Carbon credit monetisation** under voluntary markets (Verra, Gold Standard).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric CO₂ from Ammonia Synthesis (SMR Pathway)

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the **minimum theoretical mass of CO₂** generated per tonne (1000 kg) of liquid ammonia produced by the standard steam-methane reforming route, considering only the chemistry (not the reformer fuel combustion).

**Step 1: Write the overall SMR + shift + synthesis chemistry (net reaction).**

From the SMR section in §2.2, the net reaction is:
$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Reformer + Shift)}$$

**Step 2: Write the ammonia synthesis reaction.**

$$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3$$

**Step 3: Combine the two reactions in stoichiometric proportion.**

For every 4 mol H₂ produced, 1 mol CO₂ is co-generated.
The synthesis requires 3 mol H₂ per 1 mol NH₃.
Therefore, H₂ needed per mol NH₃:
$$\dfrac{3 \text{ mol H}_2}{1 \text{ mol NH}_3}$$

CO₂ produced per mol H₂:
$$\dfrac{1 \text{ mol CO}_2}{4 \text{ mol H}_2} = 0.25 \text{ mol CO}_2 / \text{mol H}_2$$

**Step 4: Express CO₂ per mole of NH₃.**

$$\text{CO}_2 / \text{NH}_3 = 0.25 \times 3 = 0.75 \text{ mol CO}_2 / \text{mol NH}_3$$

**Step 5: Convert from molar ratio to mass ratio.**

$$\dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{m_{\text{NH}_3}} = \dfrac{0.75 \times 44.01}{1 \times 17.03} = \dfrac{33.01}{17.03} = 1.938$$

**Step 6: Verify with textbook standard value.**

> [!NOTE]
> The widely accepted theoretical stoichiometric CO₂ from SMR-based ammonia is **~1.27 to 1.94 t CO₂ / t NH₃** depending on whether one counts only the process-shift CO₂ (1.27) or includes the fuel-fired reformer combustion (≈ 0.6 additional). The IEA benchmark for state-of-the-art plants (BAT) is **≈ 1.5 t CO₂ / t NH₃** (process only).

The discrepancy arises because, in practice, **not all the carbon in the feed gas becomes CO₂** — some leaves as unconverted CH₄ slip, and the reforming reaction never proceeds to 100% equilibrium. With ~3% CH₄ slip and reformer fuel burn, the practical minimum is around **1.5 to 1.6 t CO₂ / t NH₃** for BAT.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CO₂-equivalent Emissions from a Nitric Acid Plant

**Problem Statement:** A nitric acid plant produces 1,000 tonnes of 100% HNO₃ per day. The N₂O concentration in the absorber tail gas is 1,500 ppmv, and the tail gas flow rate is 50,000 Nm³/h at NTP (0°C, 1 atm). Calculate the **CO₂-equivalent emission per tonne of HNO₃**.

**Given:**
- Tail gas flow: $Q = 50{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$
- N₂O concentration: $y_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 1500 \text{ ppmv} = 1500 \times 10^{-6}$
- GWP of N₂O (AR5, 100-yr): $= 265$
- Molecular weight of N₂O: $M_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 44.01 \text{ g/mol}$
- Molar volume at NTP: $V_m = 22.414 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kmol}$

**Step 1: Calculate volumetric flow of N₂O.**

$$Q_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = Q \times y_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 50{,}000 \times 1500 \times 10^{-6} = 75 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2: Convert volumetric to molar flow.**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{N}_2\text{O}}}{V_m} = \dfrac{75}{22.414} = 3.346 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3: Calculate mass flow of N₂O.**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = \dot{n}_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} \times M_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 3.346 \times 44.01 = 147.26 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4: Convert to daily mass emission.**

$$m_{\text{N}_2\text{O,daily}} = 147.26 \times 24 = 3{,}534.3 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5: Calculate specific emission per tonne HNO₃.**

$$\eta_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = \dfrac{3{,}534.3}{1{,}000} = 3.53 \text{ kg N}_2\text{O} / \text{ t HNO}_3$$

**Step 6: Convert to CO₂-equivalent.**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2\text{e}} = 3.53 \times 265 = 935.5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e} / \text{ t HNO}_3 = 0.936 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e / t HNO}_3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A nitric acid plant with 1,500 ppmv N₂O in the tail gas has a CO₂-equivalent intensity of **~0.94 t CO₂e / t HNO₃** purely from N₂O. With abatement (catalytic destruction reducing N₂O to <100 ppmv), this drops to **<0.07 t CO₂e / t HNO₃** — a **>90% reduction**. This is why **N₂O abatement is the single highest-leverage GHG reduction opportunity** in the entire fertilizer industry.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Plant CO₂e Intensity — Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** A 1,500 TPD ammonia-urea complex has the following emissions:

| Source | Mass Emission (t/day) | GHG |
|---|---|---|
| Reformer stack | 1,950 | CO₂ |
| Auxiliary boiler | 350 | CO₂ |
| Grid electricity | 90 tCO₂e/day | (Scope 2) |
| HNO₃ plant tail gas | 3.5 t N₂O/day | N₂O |
| Fugitive CH₄ | 0.5 t/day | CH₄ |
| Urea prilling | 60 t CO₂/day | CO₂ |

**Daily production:** 1,500 t NH₃, of which ~70% goes to **1,725 t/day urea** (NH₃:urea ratio ≈ 0.583).

**Step 1: Convert all GHGs to CO₂e.**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{e} = (1950 + 350 + 60) \times 1 + 3.5 \times 265 + 0.5 \times 28 + 90$$
$$= 2360 + 927.5 + 14.0 + 90 = 3{,}391.5 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e/day}$$

**Step 2: Calculate intensity per tonne NH₃.**

$$I_{\text{NH}_3} = \dfrac{3{,}391.5}{1{,}500} = 2.261 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e / t NH}_3$$

**Step 3: Apply urea CO₂ sequestration credit (optional but scientifically valid).**

$$C_{\text{credit}} = 1{,}725 \times 0.733 = 1{,}264.4 \text{ t CO}_2 \text{ sequestered/day}$$

**Step 4: Calculate net CO₂e (with sequestration credit).**

$$I_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{3{,}391.5 - 1{,}264.4}{1{,}500} = \dfrac{2{,}127.1}{1{,}500} = 1.418 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e / t NH}_3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for **not stating assumptions** about sequestration credits. Always declare explicitly whether you are reporting **gross** or **net** CO₂e, and whether the **biogenic** portion of CO₂ is counted.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reduction Strategies and Achievable CO₂ Footprint Targets

| Strategy | Technology | Reduction Potential | Maturity | Typical Capex (USD/t NH₃·yr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy efficiency** | Heat integration, pinch analysis, variable-speed drives | **5 to 15 %** | Mature (TRL 9) | 50 to 150 |
| **N₂O abatement in HNO₃** | Catalytic (Secondary/Tertiary), Non-selective catalytic reduction (NSCR) | **>90 %** of N₂O | Mature (TRL 9) | 20 to 60 |
| **CO₂ recovery from reformer** | MEA / aMDEA / Rectisol-based capture | **60 to 90 %** of process CO₂ | Mature (TRL 9) | 150 to 300 |
| **CCS (pipeline + storage)** | Compress → transport → sequester in saline aquifer / depleted reservoir | **>95 %** | Commercial (TRL 8) | 400 to 800 |
| **Green H₂ via electrolysis** | Alkaline / PEM / SOEC electrolysers powered by renewables | **>99 %** if renewable | Commercial (TRL 7 to 9) | 1,500 to 3,000 |
| **Blue H₂ + CCS** | SMR + 95% CO₂ capture | **≈ 90 %** | Commercial (TRL 9) | 600 to 1,200 |
| **Haber-Bosch electrified** | Electrochemical NH₃ synthesis (Li-mediated, molten salt) | **>90 %** | Pilot (TRL 4 to 6) | Not yet commercial |
| **Renewable PPA** | Wind/solar PPA for grid offset | **Scope 2 → 0** | Mature (TRL 9) | 20 to 50 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision rule for KTU answers:** When asked to "reduce the CO₂ footprint," always present the answer in the order **(1) Avoid → (2) Reduce → (3) Substitute → (4) Capture → (5) Offset** — this is the textbook hierarchy used by IChemE and IEA.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define Global Warming Potential (GWP). Why is N₂O considered a far more potent GHG than CO₂ on a per-molecule basis?**

**Model Answer:**

**Global Warming Potential (GWP)** is a dimensionless index that measures the **time-integrated radiative forcing** of a unit mass of a given greenhouse gas, relative to that of CO₂, over a specified time horizon (typically 100 years). It is calculated as:

$$\text{GWP}_i = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} a_i \cdot [c_i(t)] \, dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot [c_{\text{CO}_2}(t)] \, dt}$$

where $a_i$ is the **radiative efficiency (W m⁻² ppb⁻¹)** of gas *i*, $[c_i(t)]$ is its **time-decaying atmospheric concentration** (after pulse emission), and *TH* is the time horizon (100 years).

**Why N₂O is more potent:**
1. **Higher radiative efficiency** per molecule (~210× CO₂ in instantaneous forcing).
2. **Longer atmospheric lifetime** (~114 years for N₂O vs. ~variable for CO₂ absorbed by oceans/vegetation).
3. **Combined GWP = 265** (AR5), i.e., 1 kg N₂O traps the same heat as 265 kg CO₂ over 100 years.
4. Fertilizer-related N₂O sources (nitric acid plants, agricultural soils) release it without the natural sinks that remove CO₂.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any six sources of CO₂ emissions in an integrated ammonia-urea complex.**

**Model Answer (any six):**
1. **Primary reformer furnace stack** (largest single source; CO₂ from fuel combustion + process).
2. **Secondary reformer** (CO₂ from partial oxidation of CH₄).
3. **CO shift converter** (CO₂ formed via water-gas shift; later captured, but slip exists).
4. **Auxiliary boiler** (natural gas / fuel oil combustion for steam generation).
5. **CO₂ regeneration column** overheads (CO₂ slip during solvent regeneration).
6. **Flare system** (intermittent combustion of vent gases).
7. **Urea plant granulation** (CO₂ vented from prilling/granulation).
8. **Imported grid electricity** (Scope 2 — indirect CO₂ from coal-based Indian grid, ~0.7 to 0.9 kg CO₂/kWh).
9. **Fugitive emissions** from compressor seals, valve packings.
10. **Diesel generators** for emergency power.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat equations, explain the various sources of GHG emissions in a **nitric acid plant**. Calculate the CO₂-equivalent emission per tonne of 100% HNO₃ produced, given that the absorber tail gas contains 1,800 ppmv N₂O at a flow of 60,000 Nm³/h, and the plant produces 1,200 tonnes of HNO₃ per day. Use GWP of N₂O = 265.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **any three techniques** for reducing the CO₂ footprint of an ammonia plant. Compare their technological maturity (TRL), reduction potential, and economic viability.

---

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**GHG sources in a nitric acid plant (Bunsen-Ostwald route):**

The main GHG is **N₂O** (nitrous oxide), produced as an unwanted by-product in the ammonia oxidation burner:

$$4\text{NH}_3 + 4\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{N}_2\text{O} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(side reaction on Pt-Rh gauze)}$$

Other GHG / pollutant sources:
- **NOₓ** (NO, NO₂) in tail gas — also a GHG with GWP of N₂O/NO₂ = 265, NO = negligible.
- **CO₂** from natural gas-fired absorber acid cooler, reboiler, and tail gas heater.
- **NH₃ slip** (not a GHG, but a precursor to N₂O in atmosphere).

**Step 1: Volumetric flow of N₂O.**
$$Q_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 60{,}000 \times 1800 \times 10^{-6} = 108 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2: Molar flow.**
$$\dot{n}_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = \dfrac{108}{22.414} = 4.819 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3: Mass flow.**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 4.819 \times 44.01 = 212.07 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4: Daily mass.**
$$m_{\text{daily}} = 212.07 \times 24 = 5{,}089.7 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5: Specific emission per tonne HNO₃.**
$$\eta = \dfrac{5{,}089.7}{1{,}200} = 4.241 \text{ kg N}_2\text{O / t HNO}_3$$

**Step 6: CO₂-equivalent.**
$$\text{CO}_2\text{e} = 4.241 \times 265 = 1{,}123.9 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / t HNO}_3 = 1.124 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e / t HNO}_3$$

**Answer:** The plant emits **≈ 1.124 t CO₂e per tonne of HNO₃** purely from N₂O.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Three CO₂ reduction techniques for an ammonia plant:**

| # | Technique | Reduction Potential | TRL | Economic Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Energy efficiency via process integration** (pinch analysis, heat recovery from reformer flue gas, VSD on compressors) | 5–15 % plant-wide | 9 (Mature) | Lowest Capex (~₹50–150/t NH₃·yr). Payback 1–3 years. |
| **2** | **CO₂ capture & storage (CCS) from reformer flue gas** using MDEA / Rectisol, followed by pipeline transport to saline aquifer or EOR | 60–90 % of process CO₂ | 8–9 (Commercial) | Capex ₹400–800/t NH₃·yr. Viable only with carbon price >$50/tCO₂. |
| **3** | **Green H₂ via PEM/alkaline electrolysis** powered by solar/wind PPA, replacing SMR for H₂ generation | >90 % of total CO₂ (Scope 1+2) | 7–9 (Commercial, scaling) | Capex ₹1,500–3,000/t NH₃·yr. Strongly dependent on renewable LCOE. |

**Discussion:**
- **Maturity ladder:** Energy efficiency → CCS → Green H₂ (lowest to highest reduction, lowest to highest TRL risk).
- **Recommended phased approach:** Implement energy efficiency (years 0–2) → install CCS on existing plant (years 3–7) → transition to green H₂ at next major turnaround (years 8–15).
- **Decision driver:** Carbon pricing / CBAM exposure determines whether green H₂ is competitive.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of Carbon Footprint** as applied to fertilizer products. Describe the **methodology of CO₂e calculation** for a mixed GHG stream with a worked numerical example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **Carbon Capture and Utilisation (CCU)** pathways in reducing the CO₂ footprint of a fertilizer complex. Include at least two CCU routes with their chemistry.

---

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Carbon Footprint Definition:**
The carbon footprint of a fertilizer product is the **sum of all GHG emissions (in CO₂-equivalent mass)** released over its lifecycle — from raw material extraction, through manufacture, distribution, and use — divided by the functional unit (e.g., 1 tonne of product or 1 kg of nutrient N/P₂O₅/K₂O).

**Methodology of CO₂e calculation:**

**Step 1 — Boundary definition:** Decide on cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave, or gate-to-gate.

**Step 2 — Activity data collection:** Quantify each source (fuel burned, electricity consumed, raw material processed).

**Step 3 — Apply emission factors:**

$$E_{\text{CO}_2} = \text{Activity Data} \times \text{Emission Factor} \times \text{Oxidation Fraction}$$

**Step 4 — Convert each GHG to CO₂e:**

$$\text{CO}_2\text{e}_{\text{total}} = \sum_i m_i \times \text{GWP}_i$$

**Step 5 — Normalise by product output:**

$$C_f = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2\text{e}_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{product}}}$$

**Worked Example:**
Ammonia plant emits daily: 1,800 t CO₂ + 4 t CH₄ + 1.5 t N₂O + 100 t CO₂e (grid). Production = 1,500 t NH₃/day.

$$\text{CO}_2\text{e} = (1800 \times 1) + (4 \times 28) + (1.5 \times 265) + 100 = 1800 + 112 + 397.5 + 100 = 2{,}409.5 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e/day}$$

$$C_f = \dfrac{2{,}409.5}{1{,}500} = 1.606 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e / t NH}_3$$

This is **at the BAT benchmark** of 1.5–1.8 t CO₂e/t NH₃.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Carbon Capture and Utilisation (CCU) pathways:**

**CCU Route 1 — Urea synthesis (mature, on-site utilisation):**
The CO₂ removed in the ammonia plant's CO₂ removal section (e.g., Rectisol) is compressed and fed to the urea reactor:
$$2\text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{COONH}_4 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
This is **permanent sequestration** in solid/liquid urea, eliminating ~0.733 t CO₂ per t urea. Most Indian complexes already practise this.

**CCU Route 2 — Methanol / Formic acid synthesis (emerging):**
Captured CO₂ + green H₂ → Methanol:
$$\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
This provides a route to **renewable methanol** with end-use as fuel or chemical feedstock.

**CCU Route 3 — Mineral carbonation of CO₂:**
CO₂ + CaO / MgO / waste fly ash → CaCO₃ / MgCO₃ (stable carbonates). Slow kinetics; research-stage for fertilizer fly-ash.

**CCU Route 4 — Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR):**
Compressed CO₂ injected into depleted oil reservoirs to improve sweep efficiency. Used in US Gulf Coast and Middle East; viable in Indian offshore (Mumbai High) with adaptation.

**Role in CO₂ footprint reduction:**
- Converts a **waste liability** into a **revenue stream** or **avoided emissions credit**.
- Provides **decarbonisation pathway** without abandoning existing infrastructure.
- Synergises with the **circular economy** and **fertilizer self-consumption** of CO₂.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by GWP** when calculating CO₂e (e.g., using N₂O mass directly as "CO₂ emission").
> 2. **Mixing up molar volume at NTP (22.414) and STP (22.711)** — examiners deduct 1 mark per error.
> 3. **Not stating units** in final answer (always write "t CO₂e / t NH₃").
> 4. **Confusing Scope 1 / 2 / 3** — write which scope each emission belongs to.
> 5. **Not mentioning boundary assumptions** (cradle-to-gate vs gate-to-gate) — always declare.
> 6. **Using outdated GWP values** (e.g., AR4 = 298 for N₂O, AR5 = 265) — state the IPCC AR reference.
> 7. **Missing CCU credit** for urea-bound CO₂ in plant-wide inventories.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 (Climate Change & CO₂ Footprint):**

- ☑ **Seven GHGs** under Kyoto Protocol: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, HFCs, PFCs, SF₆, NF₃. **GWP values (AR5, 100-yr) must be memorised.**
- ☑ **CO₂e formula**: $\text{CO}_2\text{e} = \sum (m_i \times \text{GWP}_i)$. Always convert mass emissions to CO₂e before summing.
- ☑ **Ammonia SMR stoichiometry**: Theoretical minimum ≈ 1.27 t CO₂/t NH₃ (process only) and **BAT industry benchmark = 1.5 t CO₂/t NH₃**.
- ☑ **Nitric acid N₂O**: Unabated 6–12 kg N₂O/t HNO₃; abated 0.5–2.5 kg N₂O/t HNO₃; >90% reduction via catalytic abatement.
- ☑ **Urea CO₂ credit**: 0.733 t CO₂ sequestered per t urea (theoretical, from stoichiometry).
- ☑ **Three Scopes**: Scope 1 (direct) + Scope 2 (purchased energy) + Scope 3 (value chain).
- ☑ **Reduction hierarchy**: Avoid → Reduce → Substitute → Capture → Offset (always present in this order in KTU answers).
- ☑ **CCU Routes**: Urea synthesis, methanol synthesis, mineralisation, EOR — all real, commercially relevant in Indian fertilizer context.
- ☑ **BAT References**: IFA, IEA, IPCC AR5, GHG Protocol Corporate Standard, EU BAT BREF for Ammonia & Nitric Acid.
- ☑ **Always declare** in numerical answers: **(i) GWP source (AR4/AR5), (ii) system boundary, (iii) units, (iv) any sequestration credits applied, (v) assumed operating hours/days.**

> **Best of luck with your KTU Module 4 preparation — remember that examiners reward clarity, declared assumptions, and unit-aware numerical work!**
